- **Reach trucks, Order pickers:** Used in warehouse aisles.

#### G. Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)
Self-driven vehicles that follow **guide wires, magnetic strips, laser reflectors, or vision-based paths** in factories. They transport pallets, totes, or raw materials between workstations without human intervention.

**Guidance Technologies (Frequent KTU Question):**
- **Wire-guided:** Buried wire emits a frequency, vehicle senses via coil.
- **Magnetic tape:** Adhesive magnetic strip on floor.
- **Laser guidance:** Reflectors on walls, vehicle triangulates position.
- **Vision/SLAM:** Camera + LiDAR based, fully autonomous.

#### H. Cranes and Hoists
- **Bridge crane:** Moves on rails along the building length (X-axis) and across the bay (Y-axis); hoist provides Z-axis motion.
- **Gantry crane:** Similar to bridge but supported on legs, used outdoors.
- **Jib crane:** Rotates on a fixed column, covers a circular area.
- **Hoists:** Pure vertical lifting devices using chains or wire ropes, powered by electric, pneumatic, or manual mechanisms.

#### I. Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)
Computer-controlled systems with **aisles, racks, and extractors** that automatically store and retrieve items from defined locations. Types include:
- **Unit-load AS/RS:** For pallets (1+ ton).
- **Mini-load AS/RS:** For totes and cartons.
- **Vertical lift modules (VLMs):** Space-saving enclosed elevators.
- **Carousel systems:** Horizontal or vertical rotating shelves.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Material Transport Systems as per KTU 2024 Scheme.

| S.No | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Belt Conveyor Capacity: $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | $Q$ = t/h, $A$ = cross-section area (m²), $v$ = belt speed (m/s), $\rho$ = bulk density (t/m³) | Tonnage handled per hour |
| 2 | Belt Conveyor Power: $P = \frac{(Q \cdot L \cdot \mu \pm Q \cdot H)}{367 \cdot \eta}$ | $P$ in kW, $L$ = length (m), $\mu$ = friction factor, $H$ = lift height (m), $\eta$ = drive efficiency | Motor sizing |
| 3 | Screw Conveyor Capacity: $Q = 60 \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D_s^2}{4} \cdot p \cdot N \cdot \psi \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{-6}$ | $D_s$ = screw dia (mm), $p$ = pitch (mm), $N$ = rpm, $\psi$ = loading factor | Auger throughput |
| 4 | Pneumatic Conveying Air Requirement: $Q_{air} = \frac{m_s}{\mu_m}$ | $Q_{air}$ = air mass flow, $m_s$ = solids rate, $\mu_m$ = solids-to-air ratio | Air volume design |
| 5 | AGV Cycle Time: $T_c = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$ | $T$ in minutes, indices denote phases | Fleet sizing |
| 6 | Number of AGVs: $N_{AGV} = \frac{T_c \cdot D_{rate}}{60 \cdot C_{AGV}}$ | $D_{rate}$ = delivery demand (deliveries/hr), $C_{AGV}$ = AGV capacity | Required fleet count |
| 7 | Hoist Power: $P = \frac{W \cdot v}{6120 \cdot \eta}$ | $W$ = load (kg), $v$ = lift speed (m/min) | Motor power for lifting |
| 8 | Storage Utilization: $\eta_s = \frac{V_{used}}{V_{available}} \times 100\%$ | Volumes in m³ | Space efficiency |
| 9 | Crane Cycle Time: $T_{crane} = T_{hoist} + T_{bridge} + T_{trolley} + T_{load/unload}$ | All in seconds | Crane productivity |
| 10 | Conveyor Belt Tension (Te vs Ts): $T_e = T_s \cdot e^{\mu \alpha}$ | $T_e$ = tight side, $T_s$ = slack side, $\alpha$ = wrap angle (rad) | Capstan equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In numerical answers, **always state the units** explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per quantity if units are missing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Material Transport Systems are the **backbone of modern Industry 4.0 ecosystems**:
- **Automotive plants:** AGVs deliver parts to robotic assembly cells with **±10 mm** positional accuracy.
- **E-commerce warehouses (Amazon, Flipkart):** AS/RS combined with AGVs achieve **99.99% inventory accuracy**.
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Pneumatic conveying maintains **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)** compliance by avoiding contamination.
- **Steel and cement plants:** Belt conveyors handle **5000+ tons/day** over kilometers of distance.
- **Ports and shipping:** Container cranes operate at **30+ cycles/hour**, moving containers 30+ tons each.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Belt Conveyor Capacity

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Numerical):**
A belt conveyor is used to transport coal of bulk density **0.9 t/m³**. The belt is **800 mm wide** and troughed at **30° angle of surcharge**. The cross-section area of the loaded belt (troughed + surcharge) is given as **0.0525 m²**. The belt moves at a constant speed of **1.5 m/s**. Calculate the mass flow rate in **t/h**.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For a belt conveyor, the mass flow rate depends on the cross-sectional area of material on the belt, the belt velocity, and the bulk density.

$$
Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

Here, $A$ is in square meters, $v$ is in meters per second, and $\rho$ is in tonnes per cubic meter.

$$
Q = 3600 \cdot 0.0525 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.9
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step.**

First, multiply the constants $A$ and $v$:

$$
A \cdot v = 0.0525 \cdot 1.5 = 0.07875 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the density to get mass flow.**

$$
A \cdot v \cdot \rho = 0.07875 \cdot 0.9 = 0.070875 \ \text{t/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert from per-second to per-hour by multiplying by 3600.**

$$
Q = 0.070875 \cdot 3600 = 255.15 \ \text{t/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer with units.**

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 255.15 \ \text{tonnes per hour}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** A common student error is to forget the factor of 3600 (seconds per hour). Always check the units of the final answer.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Belt Conveyor Power Requirement

**Problem Setup:**
A belt conveyor is **80 m long horizontally** (no lift) and is used to transport **250 t/h** of material. The friction factor between belt and material is **0.03**. The drive efficiency is **0.85**. Calculate the required motor power in kW.

**Step 1 — Use the KTU power formula for horizontal belt conveyors.**

For purely horizontal motion, the lift term $H$ is zero:

$$
P = \frac{Q \cdot L \cdot \mu}{367 \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the given values.**

$Q = 250$ t/h, $L = 80$ m, $\mu = 0.03$, $\eta = 0.85$.

$$
P = \frac{250 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.03}{367 \cdot 0.85}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 250 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.03 = 600
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 367 \cdot 0.85 = 311.95
$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator.**

$$
P = \frac{600}{311.95} = 1.923 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply a safety factor (1.25 to 1.5) for design margin.**

$$
P_{design} = 1.923 \cdot 1.25 = 2.40 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Select the next standard motor size.**

Standard motor sizes: 0.75, 1.1, 1.5, 2.2, 3.0, 4.0, 5.5, 7.5 kW. The next standard size above 2.40 kW is **3.0 kW**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Select a 3.0 kW standard motor.}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern:** In KTU 2024, you are expected to (a) compute the theoretical power, (b) apply a service factor, and (c) state the selected motor rating. Skipping any of these three steps attracts partial mark loss.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Number of AGVs Required

**Problem Setup:**
A manufacturing plant needs **40 deliveries per hour** between the warehouse and the assembly line. Each AGV takes an average of **6 minutes** for a complete delivery cycle (load + travel + unload). Each AGV can carry **1 load per cycle**. Calculate the number of AGVs required.

**Step 1 — Identify the AGV fleet sizing formula.**

$$
N_{AGV} = \frac{T_c \cdot D_{rate}}{60 \cdot C_{AGV}}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values.**

$T_c = 6$ min, $D_{rate} = 40$ deliveries/h, $C_{AGV} = 1$ load/cycle.

$$
N_{AGV} = \frac{6 \cdot 40}{60 \cdot 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator.**

$$
N_{AGV} = \frac{240}{60} = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Add 1 spare AGV for redundancy and maintenance.**

$$
N_{AGV,total} = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total AGVs required} = 5 \ \text{(including 1 spare)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria for Material Transport

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a **favourite KTU question**. Memorize the conditions under which each transport system is selected.

| Selection Parameter | Belt Conveyor | Roller Conveyor | Forklift | AGV | AS/RS | Pneumatic |
|---------------------|---------------|------------------|----------|------|-------|-----------|
| **Load type** | Bulk, granular | Cartons, totes | Pallets, heavy | Pallots, totes | Pallots, totes | Powder, granules |
| **Distance** | Long (km) | Short (m) | Medium (m) | Medium (m) | Vertical (m) | Long (m) |
| **Path** | Fixed | Fixed | Variable | Programmable | Fixed racks | Fixed pipes |
| **Automation level** | Low–High | Low–High | Manual | Fully auto | Fully auto | Medium |
| **Flexibility** | Low | Low | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Initial cost** | High | Low | Low | High | Very high | Medium |
| **Hygiene suitability** | Medium | High | Low | High | High | Excellent |
| **Energy efficiency** | Medium | High (gravity) | Low | Medium (battery) | Medium | Low (compressors) |
| **KTU selection rule** | Bulk continuous flow | Light unit loads | Low-volume, variable routes | High-volume, fixed routes | High-density storage | Hazardous/sanitary |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Identifying the System from a Description (KTU Trick)

When a KTU question describes a scenario and asks "which material transport system is most suitable?", apply this 5-step logic:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the material form:** Is it bulk, unit, granular, liquid, or hazardous?
2. **Step 2 — Identify the path:** Is the path fixed, variable, or programmable?
3. **Step 3 — Identify the volume:** Is it high volume (continuous) or low volume (on-demand)?
4. **Step 4 — Identify the environment:** Indoor, outdoor, clean room, explosive, hygienic?
5. **Step 5 — Match to the selection matrix** above and justify with two engineering reasons.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Material Transport System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sourceZone["SOURCE ZONE"]
        R["Raw Material Storage"]
        Q1["Quality Inspection"]
    end

    subgraph primeMover["PRIMARY TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        C1["Belt / Roller Conveyors"]
        C2["Pneumatic Conveyors"]
        C3["Chain / Screw Conveyors"]
    end

    subgraph workZone["WORK-IN-PROCESS ZONE"]
        W1["Workstation 1 - Machining"]
        W2["Workstation 2 - Assembly"]
        W3["Workstation 3 - Testing"]
    end

    subgraph autoMover["AUTOMATED VEHICLE LAYER"]
        A1["AGV Fleet"]
        A2["Mobile Robots AMR"]
    end

    subgraph liftZone["LIFTING & STORAGE LAYER"]
        L1["Overhead Bridge Crane"]
        L2["AS/RS System"]
        L3["Vertical Lift Module"]
    end

    subgraph dispatchZone["DISPATCH ZONE"]
        D1["Finished Goods Buffer"]
        D2["Shipping Dock"]
    end

    R --> Q1
    Q1 --> C1
    Q1 --> C2
    Q1 --> C3
    C1 --> W1
    C2 --> W2
    C3 --> W3
    W1 --> A1
    W2 --> A1
    W3 --> A2

    A2 --> L2
    L1 --> L2

    L3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2

    ctrl["PLC / SCADA Central Controller"] -. Monitors & Commands .-> C1
    ctrl -. Monitors & Commands .-> A1
    ctrl -. Monitors & Commands .-> L1
    ctrl -. Monitors & Commands .-> L2
```


### 4.3 Process Flow: Material Flow from Incoming to Dispatch

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define a belt conveyor and list any four of its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **belt conveyor** is a continuous material handling device that uses an endless flexible belt running over two or more pulleys to transport bulk or unit loads from one location to another.

**Four main components:**
1. **Belt** – the carrying medium (rubber, PVC, fabric-reinforced).
2. **Drive pulley (head pulley)** – powered by a motor; provides traction.
3. **Idler rollers** – support the belt and the load.
4. **Tail pulley** – the take-up end, often with a tensioning mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative acceptable components:** Take-up assembly, loading chute, discharge scraper, cleaning brush, drive gearbox, electric motor.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a wire-guided AGV and a laser-guided AGV.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wire-Guided AGV | Laser-Guided AGV |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Guidance medium** | Buried electrical wire in floor | Laser reflectors mounted on walls/columns |
| **Installation cost** | High (floor cutting required) | Low–Medium (no floor work) |
| **Path flexibility** | Very low (path is fixed at install) | High (paths can be re-programmed) |
| **Accuracy** | ±10 mm | ±10 mm or better |
| **Maintenance** | Wire damage hard to detect | Reflectors may get dirty |
| **Use case** | Stable, long-term layouts | Frequently changing factories |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**a)** Explain the working principle of a **pneumatic conveying system** with a neat sketch. Classify it into **dilute phase** and **dense phase**, listing the differences in **4 points**. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A belt conveyor is used to transport **350 t/h** of iron ore over a horizontal distance of **120 m**. The friction factor is **0.04**, the drive efficiency is **88%**, and the lift height is **10 m**. Calculate the **motor power** required and select a **standard motor size**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Working Principle:**
A pneumatic conveying system transports dry bulk materials (powders, granules) suspended in a gas stream inside an enclosed pipeline. A **blower or compressor** generates the air flow, a **feeder** (rotary valve or venturi) introduces the material into the airstream, and the **separation device** (cyclone, bag filter) at the receiving end separates the material from the air.

**Key Components:**
1. Air mover (compressor / blower / fan)
2. Feeder / injector
3. Conveying pipeline
4. Material-air separator (cyclone)
5. Air filtration unit (bag filter)
6. Receiving hopper

**Differences between Dilute and Dense Phase Pneumatic Conveying:**

| S.No | Parameter | Dilute Phase | Dense Phase |
|------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | Solids-to-air ratio ($\mu_m$) | 1 : 30 to 1 : 100 | 20 : 1 to 200 : 1 |
| 2 | Air velocity | 15 – 30 m/s (high) | 1 – 8 m/s (low) |
| 3 | Pressure | Low pressure, low pressure drop | High pressure (2–10 bar) |
| 4 | Particle degradation | High (fast air, particles bounce) | Low (gentle, slug flow) |
| 5 | Energy consumption per ton | Higher | Lower |
| 6 | Application | Light, non-fragile powders | Abrasive, fragile, sticky materials |

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**
For a belt conveyor with both horizontal length and vertical lift, the KTU formula is:

$$
P = \frac{(Q \cdot L \cdot \mu) + (Q \cdot H)}{367 \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$Q = 350$ t/h, $L = 120$ m, $\mu = 0.04$, $H = 10$ m, $\eta = 0.88$.

**Step 3 — Compute the horizontal friction term.**

$$
Q \cdot L \cdot \mu = 350 \cdot 120 \cdot 0.04 = 1680
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the vertical lift term.**

$$
Q \cdot H = 350 \cdot 10 = 3500
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two terms for total resistance.**

$$
R_{total} = 1680 + 3500 = 5180
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the product of 367 and efficiency.**

$$
P = \frac{5180}{367 \cdot 0.88} = \frac{5180}{322.96} = 16.04 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply a service factor of 1.25.**

$$
P_{design} = 16.04 \cdot 1.25 = 20.05 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Select the next standard motor size.**

Standard sizes: 18.5, 22, 30 kW. The next higher size is **22 kW**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Selected motor rating} = 22 \ \text{kW}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Describe the working of an **AS/RS system** with a block diagram. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of AS/RS. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A manufacturing unit requires **50 deliveries/hour** between the finished goods store and the dispatch bay. Each AGV operates on a cycle time of **8 minutes** and can carry **one pallet per trip**. Determine the **number of AGVs required**, including one **standby**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Working of AS/RS:**
An Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS) is a computer-controlled combination of **racks, aisles, extractors (stack cranes), conveyors, and a Warehouse Management System (WMS)** that automatically stores and retrieves items to/from defined rack locations.

**Operation Sequence:**
1. **Receiving:** Pallets arrive via inbound conveyor and are scanned.
2. **Storage:** The WMS assigns an empty slot; a stacker crane inserts the pallet.
3. **Tracking:** WMS updates inventory database in real-time.
4. **Retrieval:** On demand, the stacker crane extracts the pallet and places it on the outbound conveyor.
5. **Dispatch:** Pallet moves to the shipping dock.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.4 — Stacker Crane):**

The stacker crane moves along the **X-axis** (down the aisle) and **Y-axis** (across the bay), with a **Z-axis lift** to access multi-level racks. It is commanded by the WMS via PLC.

**Four Advantages of AS/RS:**
1. **Space efficiency:** Vertical utilization can reduce floor space by up to **85%**.
2. **Inventory accuracy:** Achieves **99.99%** accuracy with barcode/RFID.
3. **Labor reduction:** Operates 24/7 with minimal human intervention.
4. **Improved safety:** No manual climbing or heavy lifting on racks.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **High capital cost:** ₹ 2–10 crore initial investment depending on size.
2. **Rigid system:** Inflexible to sudden changes in product dimensions; redesign cost is high.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the AGV fleet formula.**

$$
N_{AGV} = \frac{T_c \cdot D_{rate}}{60 \cdot C_{AGV}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$T_c = 8$ min, $D_{rate} = 50$ deliveries/h, $C_{AGV} = 1$ pallet/trip.

$$
N_{AGV} = \frac{8 \cdot 50}{60 \cdot 1} = \frac{400}{60} = 6.67
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next whole number.**

Since we cannot have fractional AGVs:

$$
N_{AGV,operational} = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Add 1 standby AGV for maintenance and breakdowns.**

$$
N_{AGV,total} = 7 + 1 = 8
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total AGVs required} = 8 \ \text{(7 operational + 1 standby)}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Material Transport Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 3600 factor** when converting belt conveyor capacity from per-second to per-hour. This single error costs the full method mark.
> 2. **Missing the lift term ($Q \cdot H$)** in the power formula when the conveyor is inclined. Always check if the path is horizontal or inclined.
> 3. **Not selecting a standard motor** after the calculation. KTU examiners expect the **next standard size**, not the exact calculated value.
> 4. **Confusing AGV cycle time** with travel time only. Cycle = load + travel + unload + idle.
> 5. **Ignoring units in the final answer.** A numerically correct answer without units loses 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
>
> **Bonus Tip:** Always draw a **sketch or block diagram** even if not explicitly asked — it earns **easy 1–2 marks** as presentation in KTU 2024 evaluations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Transport Systems (KTU Module 2)**

- **Five categories:** Conveyors, Industrial Trucks, AGVs, Cranes/Hoists, AS/RS.
- **Belt conveyor capacity formula** $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ is the **most repeated KTU numerical**.
- **Belt conveyor power** $P = \frac{(Q \cdot L \cdot \mu \pm Q \cdot H)}{367 \cdot \eta}$ — sign depends on inclined or horizontal motion.
- **Screw conveyor** is for granular material; **Pneumatic conveyor** is for powders and hygienic applications.
- **Roller conveyor** is best for cartons and totes; **gravity roller** requires 3°–5° slope.
- **AGV guidance methods:** Wire, magnetic tape, laser (most common in modern plants), vision/SLAM (latest).
- **AGV cycle time** includes all 4 phases: load, travel, unload, idle. Fleet size = $(T_c \cdot D)/(60 \cdot C) + \text{standby}$.
- **Crane types:** Bridge (indoor, X–Y), Gantry (outdoor, with legs), Jib (workstation, rotation), Monorail (single axis), Stacker (AS/RS aisles).
- **AS/RS advantages:** space saving, accuracy, safety, 24/7 operation. **Limitations:** high cost, low flexibility.
- **Always convert units** and **select standard motor sizes** in numerical answers.
- **Industrial trucks** = manual, low-flexibility path, low cost. **AGVs** = automatic, programmable path, higher cost.
- **Capstan equation** $T_e = T_s \cdot e^{\mu \alpha}$ gives belt tensions on drive pulley; high wrap angle = better grip.
- **Pneumatic conveying** is preferred for **explosive, toxic, or hygienic** materials due to enclosed pipeline.
- **Belt conveyor max inclination** = 15°–20° for bulk, up to 30° with cleats.
- **Standard motor service factor** for design: 1.25 to 1.5.
- **Selection matrix** (Section 3.4) is the **key decision tool** for "which system to use" type KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Systems** in Industrial Automation refer to the integrated hardware and software infrastructure used for the temporary or permanent holding, protection, retrieval, and movement of raw materials, work-in-process (WIP) inventory, finished goods, and tools within a manufacturing or distribution environment.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, a **Storage System** is a critical subsystem of a **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** that interfaces the **Manufacturing Cell**, the **Material Handling System (MHS)**, and the **Centralized Computer Control System**. It eliminates the traditional warehouse by providing *in-line, on-demand* material availability.

### 1.2 Classification of Storage Systems

For KTU examination purposes, storage systems are classified into **four** primary categories:

| S.No | Class | Typical Application |
|------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Bulk Storage Systems** | Raw material silos, hoppers, tank farms |
| 2 | **Rack Storage Systems** | Pallet racks, bin racks, cantilever racks |
| 3 | **Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)** | FMS buffers, distribution centres |
| 4 | **Carousel / VLM Systems** | Order picking, tool storage, small parts |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Smart Library" Analogy:** Think of an industrial storage system exactly like a modern university library. A **carousel** is like a rotating bookshelf in the lobby. An **AS/RS** is the robotic crane in the basement archive that fetches the exact book (or pallet) you request. A **bulk silo** is the underground tank storing the library's reserve of ink and paper. The librarian (the **WMS software**) tells the crane where each item is and in what order to retrieve it.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Vocabulary)

- **Unit Load:** A standardized pallet, tote, or container that is moved as a single entity.
- **Storage Location Index `(i, j, k)`:** The 3D coordinate identifying a cell (bay, row, level).
- **I/O Station (Input/Output Station):** The fixed point where material enters or exits the storage system.
- **S/R Machine:** The Storage and Retrieval machine (crane) that physically executes moves.
- **Throughput `λ`:** The number of storage or retrieval transactions completed per unit time, measured in **transactions per hour (TPH)**.
- **Dwell Time:** The time a unit load remains in storage before being retrieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PCRUT501 emphasizes the *quantitative* performance of AS/RS — students must master the **single-command and dual-command cycle time derivations** as they are recurrent 7 and 14-mark questions in the KTU University Examination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Objectives of a Storage System

The KTU curriculum lists four engineering objectives that any storage system must fulfil:

1. **Space Optimization** — Minimize the *footprint* per unit stored.
2. **Inventory Visibility** — Real-time knowledge of *what* is stored, *where*, and *how many*.
3. **Throughput Maximization** — Reduce the *transaction time* per pick/deposit.
4. **Material Protection** — Shield goods from environmental damage, theft, and misplacement.

### 2.2 Architecture of a Unit-Load AS/RS

A canonical unit-load AS/RS comprises five engineering subsystems:

- **Rack Structure** — A 3D matrix of bays × rows × levels built from cold-rolled steel.
- **Storage/Retrieval (S/R) Machine** — A rail-guided crane moving along the aisle on the floor and along the rack-top rail.
- **Pickup/Delivery (P/D) Station** — A conveyor interfaced pallet station at the aisle end.
- **Aisle-Transfer Carriage** — Used in *multi-aisle* systems to move the S/R machine between aisles.
- **Warehouse Management System (WMS)** — The supervisory computer that tracks inventory and issues commands.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR ESE.** Note: All absolute-value or magnitude indicators use `\vert` so as not to break the markdown table syntax.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | Storage Capacity `C` | $C = 2 \cdot X \cdot Y \cdot Z$ | unit loads |
| 2 | Single-Command Cycle Time `T_sc` | $T_{sc} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_x}{3 \cdot V_h} + \dfrac{2 \cdot L_y}{3 \cdot V_v} + T_p$ | seconds |
| 3 | Dual-Command Cycle Time `T_dc` | $T_{dc} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_x}{3 \cdot V_h} + \dfrac{2 \cdot L_y}{3 \cdot V_v} + 2 \cdot T_p$ | seconds |
| 4 | Max Throughput (SC only) `λ_sc` | $\lambda_{sc} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{sc}}$ | TPH |
| 5 | Max Throughput (DC only) `λ_dc` | $\lambda_{dc} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{dc}}$ | TPH |
| 6 | Mixed-Command Throughput `λ_mix` | $\lambda_{mix} = \dfrac{3600}{\alpha \cdot T_{sc} + (1-\alpha) \cdot T_{dc}}$ | TPH |
| 7 | Mean Horizontal Travel Distance | $\bar{d}_h = \dfrac{L_x}{3}$ | metres |
| 8 | Mean Vertical Travel Distance | $\bar{d}_v = \dfrac{L_y}{3}$ | metres |
| 9 | Number of Transactions per Hour | $N_{tx} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot \eta}{T_{cycle}}$ | transactions/hr |
| 10 | Rack Length / Height | $L_x = B \cdot w \quad ; \quad L_y = N \cdot h$ | metres |

**Variable Legend:**
- $X$ = number of bays per row, $Y$ = number of rows, $Z$ = number of levels
- $L_x$ = total horizontal aisle length, $L_y$ = total vertical height
- $V_h$ = horizontal velocity of S/R machine (m/s)
- $V_v$ = vertical velocity of S/R machine (m/s)
- $T_p$ = pickup or deposit time (s)
- $\alpha$ = fraction of cycles that are single-command (0 ≤ α ≤ 1)
- $B$ = number of bays, $N$ = number of levels, $w$ = bay width, $h$ = level height
- $\eta$ = system utilization efficiency (typically 0.75 – 0.90)

### 2.4 Storage Strategy — FIFO vs. LIFO vs. Random

| Strategy | Discipline | Where Used | Trade-off |
|----------|------------|------------|-----------|
| **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) | Oldest load retrieved first | Perishables, dated chemicals | Lower rack density, longer travel |
| **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) | Most-recent load retrieved first | Push-back racks, drive-in | High density, risk of obsolescence |
| **Random** | Any free location chosen | AS/RS with WMS | Maximum flexibility, requires tracking |
| **Dedicated (Class-Based)** | Item family → fixed zone | ABC analysis | Predictable retrieval time |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** In the **automotive sector**, unit-load AS/RS buffers stamped body panels between the press shop and the body shop, achieving `λ_dc ≈ 60–80` TPH per aisle. In **e-commerce fulfilment** (Amazon-style), horizontal carousels combined with pick-to-light systems deliver *order-to-pick* latencies under 15 minutes. In **petrochemical plants**, bulk storage systems (silos, spheroidal tanks) decouple upstream refining from downstream distribution, providing a 7–14 day *strategic buffer* against supply-chain shocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Storage Capacity `C`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step derivation for a double-sided unit-load AS/RS rack.**

A unit-load rack is built as a *double-sided* structure: each rack column holds one unit load on its **left face** and one on its **right face** (the S/R machine operates in the central aisle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loads per column} &= 2 \quad \text{(one on each face)} \\
\text{Total columns} &= X \cdot Y \quad \text{(}X \text{ bays } \times Y \text{ rows)} \\
\text{Loads per level (sweeping all columns)} &= 2 \cdot X \cdot Y \\
\text{Total loads (all } Z \text{ levels)} &= 2 \cdot X \cdot Y \cdot Z
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the *storage capacity* is:

$$
C = 2 \cdot X \cdot Y \cdot Z \quad \text{unit loads}
$$

This is the form examiners expect. If the rack is *single-sided* (rack against a wall), simply drop the factor of **2**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Travel Distances (Chebyshev's Law)

For a *random* storage policy, the probability of any storage location being selected is **uniformly distributed** over the rack. We must find the **expected absolute distance** from the I/O station to a random location.

**Horizontal axis (length $L_x$):** Let $U_1, U_2$ be two i.i.d. uniform random variables on $[0, L_x]$ representing the *pick* and *deposit* x-coordinates.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{d}_h &= E\bigl[\, \vert U_1 - U_2 \vert \,\bigr] \\
&= \int_{0}^{L_x} \!\!\! \int_{0}^{L_x} \frac{\vert u_1 - u_2 \vert}{L_x^{2}} \, du_1 \, du_2 \\
&= \frac{L_x}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vertical axis (height $L_y$):** By identical reasoning,

$$
\bar{d}_v = E\bigl[\, \vert V_1 - V_2 \vert \,\bigr] = \frac{L_y}{3}
$$

> This is the celebrated *one-third rule* in materials handling — under random storage, the mean travel distance is one-third of the corresponding axis length, **not** one-half.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Single-Command Cycle Time `T_sc`

A **single-command (SC) cycle** is the simplest AS/RS transaction: the S/R machine either *stores* one load *or* *retrieves* one load, then returns to the I/O station. We assume the *random-storage* policy, so the inbound and outbound locations are independent and uniformly distributed.

**Step 1 — Horizontal travel (out-and-back):**

$$
T_h = \frac{\bar{d}_h^{out} + \bar{d}_h^{back}}{V_h} = \frac{L_x/3 + L_x/3}{V_h} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical travel (out-and-back):**

$$
T_v = \frac{\bar{d}_v^{out} + \bar{d}_v^{back}}{V_v} = \frac{L_y/3 + L_y/3}{V_v} = \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v}
$$

**Step 3 — Pickup or deposit time:** The crane engages or releases one load.

$$
T_p = \text{pickup or deposit time (specified by manufacturer)}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine (horizontal and vertical travel occur in parallel — *chequered* motion):**

The hoist and the trolley move **simultaneously**, so the wall-clock travel time is the **maximum** of the two:

$$
T_{travel} = \max(T_h, T_v) \quad \text{(parallel/chequered motion)}
$$

For a *worst-case* (serialized) assumption used in textbook problems:

$$
T_{travel} = T_h + T_v = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v}
$$

**Step 5 — Add pickup/deposit:**

$$
\boxed{\; T_{sc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + T_p \;}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Dual-Command Cycle Time `T_dc`

A **dual-command (DC) cycle** is *one round trip in which the S/R machine performs a store AND a retrieve* — the most efficient use of a single trip. The crane:

1. Picks up an inbound load at the I/O station (cost: $T_p$).
2. Travels to the storage location and deposits it (cost: $T_h^{out} + T_v^{out} + T_p$).
3. Travels to the retrieval location (cost: $T_h^{in} + T_v^{in}$).
4. Picks up the outbound load (cost: $T_p$).
5. Returns to the I/O station (cost: $T_h^{back} + T_v^{back}$).

**Aggregating all travel segments (assuming serialized worst-case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &= 2 T_p + \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} \\
&= 2 T_p + \frac{4 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{4 L_y}{3 V_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, the **classical KTU convention** (as in Groover's *Automation, Production Systems, and CIM*) averages the two legs and writes:

$$
\boxed{\; T_{dc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + 2 T_p \;}
$$

The underlying assumption: the two legs are statistically identical, so the per-leg averages equal $\tfrac{2 L_x}{3 V_h}$ and $\tfrac{2 L_y}{3 V_v}$ respectively. This is the form expected in the answer script.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark Question):**
> A unit-load AS/RS has **40 bays** and **12 levels**. Bay width = 1.5 m, level height = 1.0 m. The S/R machine has $V_h = 2.0$ m/s and $V_v = 0.5$ m/s. Pickup/deposit time $T_p = 8$ s. The system uses a **dual-command** policy exclusively.
> **Find:** (i) Storage capacity, (ii) Dual-command cycle time, (iii) Maximum throughput.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

$$
L_x = 40 \times 1.5 = 60 \text{ m}, \quad L_y = 12 \times 1.0 = 12 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Storage Capacity (double-sided rack):**

$$
C = 2 \times 40 \times 1 \times 12 = 960 \text{ unit loads}
$$

**Step 3 — Dual-Command Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &= \frac{2 \times 60}{3 \times 2.0} + \frac{2 \times 12}{3 \times 0.5} + 2 \times 8 \\
&= \frac{120}{6} + \frac{24}{1.5} + 16 \\
&= 20 + 16 + 16 \\
&= 52 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Throughput:**

$$
\lambda_{dc} = \frac{3600}{52} \approx 69.23 \text{ TPH}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common error is to use $L_x/2$ instead of $L_x/3$ for the average distance. Always state "random storage → Chebyshev's one-third rule" in the answer script to score full marks.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Single-Command | Dual-Command |
|--------|----------------|--------------|
| Loads moved per trip | 1 | 2 |
| Pickup/deposit events | 1 | 2 |
| Total travel distance per trip | $2 \bar{d}_h + 2 \bar{d}_v$ | $4 \bar{d}_h + 4 \bar{d}_v$ (two legs) |
| Time per load | $T_{sc}$ | $T_{dc}/2$ |
| Best TPH (for equal $V_h$, $V_v$, $T_p$) | Lower | **Higher** by ≈15–25% |
| Practical limit | One load queueing | Requires *both* inbound and outbound queues to be non-empty |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Unit-Load AS/RS

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid block below renders a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* showing the data and material flow between the five engineering subsystems. Purely alphanumeric node IDs and unformatted uppercase labels are used to ensure the diagram compiles cleanly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WMS["WAREHOUSE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM WMS"]
    CTRL["S/R MACHINE CONTROLLER PLC"]
    SR["STORAGE AND RETRIEVAL MACHINE CRANE"]
    RACK["RACK STRUCTURE BAYS X ROWS X LEVELS"]
    PD["PICKUP AND DELIVERY I/O STATION"]
    CONV["INBOUND AND OUTBOUND CONVEYORS"]
    PROD["PRODUCTION CELL OR CUSTOMER"]

    WMS -- INVENTORY DATA AND COMMAND QUEUE --> CTRL
    CTRL -- MOTION COMMANDS --> SR
    SR -- BIDIRECTIONAL MOTION --> RACK
    SR -- LOAD HANDLING --> PD
    PD -- PALLET TRANSFER --> CONV
    CONV -- MATERIAL FLOW --> PROD
    PROD -- RETURNS ORDERS --> WMS
    RACK -- STATUS FEEDBACK --> SR
    PD -- I/O CONFIRMATION --> CTRL
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Storage Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT["STORAGE SYSTEMS"]
    BULK["BULK STORAGE SILOS AND TANKS"]

    MINI["MINI LOAD AS/RS"]

    ROOT --> BULK
    ROOT --> RACK
    ROOT --> ASRS
    ROOT --> CARO

    ASRS --> MINI
    CARO --> HORZ
    CARO --> VERT
```

### 4.3 Transaction Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> SC_STORE : INBOUND COMMAND
    IDLE --> SC_RETRIEVE : OUTBOUND COMMAND
    IDLE --> DC_CYCLE : DUAL COMMAND QUEUED
    SC_STORE --> TRAVEL_HV : MOVE TO LOCATION
    TRAVEL_HV --> DEPOSIT : T_PICKUP
    DEPOSIT --> RETURN_IO : TRAVEL BACK
    RETURN_IO --> IDLE : CYCLE COMPLETE
    SC_RETRIEVE --> TRAVEL_HV2 : MOVE TO LOCATION
    TRAVEL_HV2 --> PICKUP : T_PICKUP
    PICKUP --> RETURN_IO2 : TRAVEL BACK
    RETURN_IO2 --> IDLE : CYCLE COMPLETE
    DC_CYCLE --> TRAVEL_STORE : STORE LEG
    TRAVEL_STORE --> DEPOSIT2 : FIRST T_P
    DEPOSIT2 --> TRAVEL_RETRIEVE : MOVE TO RETRIEVE LOC
    TRAVEL_RETRIEVE --> PICKUP2 : SECOND T_P
    PICKUP2 --> RETURN_IO3 : TRAVEL BACK
    RETURN_IO3 --> IDLE : DC CYCLE COMPLETE
```
