
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Aid Insight:** Always include a block diagram in the 7-mark question on AS/RS components. The KTU valuation key allocates **2 marks** specifically for a *neat, labelled block diagram*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS). List any four advantages of an AS/RS over a conventional warehouse.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** An **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** is a computer-controlled system that automatically stores and retrieves loads from defined storage locations using cranes, conveyors, and a Warehouse Management System (WMS), with minimal human intervention.

**Four Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Space Savings:** Vertical usage reduces floor area by up to 90% versus block-stacked warehouses.
2. **Labour Cost Reduction:** Eliminates manual pickers, fork-lift drivers, and stock-takers.
3. **Inventory Accuracy:** Real-time WMS tracking gives >99.9% location accuracy.
4. **Improved Safety:** No personnel in the storage aisle → zero forklift accidents inside storage.
5. *(Any other valid point — e.g., reduced product damage, FIFO discipline, integration with ERP)*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between FIFO and LIFO storage strategies. State one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | FIFO (First-In-First-Out) | LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Discipline** | Oldest stock is retrieved first | Newest stock is retrieved first |
| **Physical Layout** | Flow-through racks, conveyors | Push-back or drive-in racks |
| **Density** | Lower (one aisle per lane) | Higher (deep lanes) |
| **Application** | Pharmaceutical cold storage, dairy | Bricks, cement, non-dated industrial parts |

- **FIFO application (½ mark):** Cold-chain pharmaceutical warehouse where drug expiry must be tracked.
- **LIFO application (½ mark):** A cement bagging plant where the freshest bag is at the dispatch face.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-2 question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **main components of a unit-load AS/RS** and state the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A unit-load AS/RS has **five** functional subsystems:

1. **Rack Structure (2 Marks):** A three-dimensional grid of structural steel columns and beams, organized into **bays × rows × levels**. Each cell holds one unit load. The racks are sized to the standardized pallet envelope (e.g., 1200 × 1000 × 1500 mm).

2. **Storage/Retrieval Machine (S/R Crane) (2 Marks):** A rail-guided crane with two simultaneous drives — a **horizontal trolley** (along the floor rail) and a **vertical hoist** (along the rack-top rail). It carries a telescopic fork or satellite carriage to engage the pallet.

3. **Pickup/Delivery (P/D) Station (1 Mark):** A fixed conveyor or roller table at the aisle end where loads enter and exit the system. Often equipped with barcode/RFID scanners.

4. **Aisle-Transfer Carriage (1 Mark):** A shuttle that moves the S/R machine between aisles in a multi-aisle warehouse.

5. **Warehouse Management System (WMS) (1 Mark):** The supervisory software that tracks inventory, executes slotting logic, and issues the command queue to the crane controller.

---

**(b)** Derive the **expressions for single-command and dual-command cycle times** for a unit-load AS/RS under the random-storage policy. Clearly state the *one-third rule* and explain its origin. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — One-third rule (2 Marks):**
For two i.i.d. uniform random variables $U_1, U_2 \in [0, L_x]$:

$$
E\bigl[\, \vert U_1 - U_2 \vert \,\bigr] = \int_{0}^{L_x}\!\!\int_{0}^{L_x} \frac{\vert u_1 - u_2 \vert}{L_x^2}\,du_1\,du_2 = \frac{L_x}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Single-command cycle (2.5 Marks):**
The crane performs one store or retrieve:

$$
T_{sc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + T_p
$$

**Step 3 — Dual-command cycle (2.5 Marks):**
The crane performs one store and one retrieve in a single trip:

$$
T_{dc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + 2 T_p
$$

The dual-command cycle is always slower **in absolute time** but achieves **higher throughput per load** because two transactions are completed in one trip.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of storage systems** used in industrial automation, with one example application for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Bulk Storage** | High-volume, low-SKU holding (silos, tanks) | Cement silo in a ready-mix plant |
| 2 | **Rack Storage** | Static floor racks with manual fork-lift handling | Pallet rack in a hardware distributor |
| 3 | **Unit-Load AS/RS** | Robotic crane serving a 3D rack matrix | FMS buffer between press-shop and body-shop |
| 4 | **Mini-Load AS/RS** | Crane handling totes/bins for small parts | Pharmacies' small-pack SKUs |
| 5 | **Horizontal Carousel** | Rotating bins on an oval track | Order-picking in a library back-office |
| 6 | **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)** | Trays fetched by an extractor between two columns | Tool storage in a CNC machining cell |
| 7 | **Paternoster** | Continuous vertical chain of trays | Cold-store for small electronics |

*(Award 1 mark per type, with at least 5 expected for full 7 marks.)*

---

**(b)** A unit-load AS/RS has **30 bays and 10 levels** with **two aisles**. The bay width is **1.2 m** and the level height is **0.9 m**. The S/R machine horizontal speed is **$V_h = 1.5$ m/s** and vertical speed is **$V_v = 0.4$ m/s**. Pickup/deposit time $T_p = 6$ s. The system operates on a **dual-command** policy.

**Calculate:**
1. Storage capacity of the system.
2. The dual-command cycle time.
3. The maximum throughput in **TPH**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry (1 Mark):**

$$
L_x = 30 \times 1.2 = 36 \text{ m}, \quad L_y = 10 \times 0.9 = 9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Storage Capacity (2 Marks):**
Double-sided rack, two aisles → capacity per aisle = $2 \times 30 \times 10 = 600$ loads.
Total $C = 2 \times 600 = 1200$ unit loads.

**Step 3 — Dual-Command Cycle Time (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &= \frac{2 \times 36}{3 \times 1.5} + \frac{2 \times 9}{3 \times 0.4} + 2 \times 6 \\
&= \frac{72}{4.5} + \frac{18}{1.2} + 12 \\
&= 16 + 15 + 12 = 43 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Throughput (2 Marks):**

$$
\lambda_{dc} = \frac{3600}{43} \approx 83.72 \text{ TPH}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C = 1200$ unit loads, $T_{dc} = 43$ s, $\lambda_{dc} \approx 83.72$ TPH.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — READ BEFORE WRITING THE ESE!**
>
> 1. **The "½ vs. ⅓" Trap:** The most common error in cycle-time problems is using $L_x/2$ (uniform *mean* distance) instead of $L_x/3$ (uniform *mean absolute* distance). The KTU valuation key **deducts 1 full mark** for this confusion. Always state: *"Under random storage with Chebyshev averaging, $\bar d = L/3$."*
> 2. **Pickup/Deliver Count:** In a single-command cycle there is **one** $T_p$ event; in a dual-command cycle there are **two** $T_p$ events. Forgetting the second $T_p$ in the dual-command derivation costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Throughput Unit:** Always express the final answer in **TPH** (transactions per hour), not in s⁻¹. Multiply by 3600 at the last step.
> 4. **"FIFO ≠ Carousel":** A horizontal carousel inherently implements *FIFO only if items are returned to the operator's side.* Don't claim every carousel is FIFO.
> 5. **Rack Capacity Factor of 2:** The "2" in $C = 2XYZ$ is for *double-sided* racks. If the question says "single-sided against a wall", use $C = XYZ$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 2: STORAGE SYSTEMS**

- [ ] **Storage System = interface** between material handling and centralized control in an FMS.
- [ ] **Four classes:** Bulk, Rack, AS/RS, Carousel/VLM.
- [ ] **Unit-load AS/RS** = the canonical exam topic.
- [ ] **Five components:** Rack, S/R crane, P/D station, Aisle-transfer, WMS.
- [ ] **One-third rule** $\bar d = L/3$ arises from $E[\vert U_1 - U_2 \vert]$ for i.i.d. uniform variables.
- [ ] **Single-command:** $T_{sc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + T_p$
- [ ] **Dual-command:** $T_{dc} = \frac{2 L_x}{3 V_h} + \frac{2 L_y}{3 V_v} + 2 T_p$
- [ ] **Storage capacity (double-sided):** $C = 2 X Y Z$
- [ ] **Throughput:** $\lambda = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ expressed in TPH.
- [ ] **FIFO** = oldest out first; used for dated/perishable goods.
- [ ] **LIFO** = newest out first; used for high-density, non-critical stock.
- [ ] **Random storage** = any free cell, requires WMS, gives shortest mean retrieval time.
- [ ] **Dual-command** > **Single-command** for throughput *per unit load*, but requires both inbound and outbound queues to be non-empty.
- [ ] **Carousel types:** Horizontal (rotary bin rack) and Vertical (VLM / paternoster).
- [ ] **Mini-load AS/RS** handles totes, not pallets; uses a lighter crane.
- [ ] **Real-world benchmark:** Modern AS/RS achieves $\lambda_{dc} \approx 60$–$120$ TPH per aisle.

> **End of Module 2 — Storage Systems Notes. All the best for the KTU ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS):** A computer-controlled, integrated system of hardware (storage racks, storage/retrieval machines, input/output stations, and conveyance equipment) and software (warehouse management and control algorithms) designed to automatically store, retrieve, and transport loads with high speed, accuracy, and minimal human intervention in manufacturing, distribution, and assembly environments.

In KTU terminology, AS/RS is classified as a **fixed-path material handling system** in which the storage and retrieval functions are performed automatically by programmed machines under the supervision of a central computer or PLC.

The performance of an AS/RS is judged using two standard metrics:

- **Throughput (transactions/hour):** Total storage + retrieval cycles completed per unit time.
- **Storage Density (m³/m² of floor space):** Effective volumetric utilization of the warehouse floor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Robotic Library:**
> Imagine a giant, multi-storey library where thousands of books are stored in tightly packed rows. Instead of a human walking with a ladder, a robotic crane (the *S/R machine*) zips along an aisle, calculates the exact 3D coordinate (row, column, level) of the required book, lifts it, and delivers it to a checkout counter. That is essentially an AS/RS, but for raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), or finished goods.

A simpler way to think: AS/RS = **Robot Crane + Smart Shelving + Conveyor + Brain (Controller)**.

## 1.3 Classification of AS/RS (KTU Syllabus Focus)

| Class | Load Unit | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit-Load AS/RS** | Pallet / tote (large) | Manufacturing, distribution centres |
| **Mini-Load AS/RS** | Tote / bin / carton | Order picking, pharmaceuticals |
| **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)** | Tote in enclosed column | Space-constrained factories |
| **Carousel Systems** (Horizontal / Vertical) | Tote on rotating rack | Small-part picking |
| **Mobile Rack AS/RS** | Pallet on movable base | Cold storage, archives |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the **architectural layout, S/R machine kinematics, cycle time analysis, and throughput estimation** of Unit-Load and Mini-Load AS/RS. Carousels and VLMs are usually touched upon as comparative alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Subsystems of an AS/RS

1. **Storage Racks (Structural Skeleton)**
   - Fabricated from roll-formed steel.
   - Configured in **single-deep**, **double-deep**, or **flow-through (live rack)** arrangements.
   - Each storage location has a unique **(x, y, z) address** (aisle, column, level).

2. **Storage/Retrieval (S/R) Machine**
   - A robotic crane operating in the aisle between two rack banks.
   - Performs three motions: **horizontal traversal (X-axis)**, **hoist (Y-axis)**, **telescope/extraction (Z-axis)**.
   - Modern S/R machines use **frequency-controlled AC drives** for variable velocity and acceleration.

3. **Input/Output (I/O) Stations**
   - Pickup/deposit points where the S/R machine exchanges loads with conveyors or AGVs.
   - Can be at the **end of aisle (EOA)** or **mid-aisle**.

4. **Conveyance Subsystem**
   - Roller, chain, or belt conveyors that link I/O stations to production lines.

5. **Control Subsystem**
   - WMS (Warehouse Management System) + WCS (Warehouse Control System) running on PLC/DCS.
   - Performs **inventory tracking, slotting optimization, sequencing, and S/R machine dispatching**.

## 2.2 Operating Logic (Step-by-Step)

- **Storage Request:** Host computer generates a `PUT` command → WMS allocates an empty bin → S/R machine moves to that bin coordinate → places load → updates inventory database.
- **Retrieval Request:** Host generates a `GET` command → WMS identifies bin → S/R machine picks load → delivers to I/O station → inventory decremented.
- **Combined Cycle:** S/R machine simultaneously completes a storage and a retrieval in one aisle trip (Dual-Command Cycle).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{SC} = 2 T_t + T_p + T_d$ | **Single-Command Cycle Time** (pick from one location, deposit at another) | s |
| 2 | $T_{DC} = \max(T_1, T_2) + T_p + T_d$ | **Dual-Command Cycle Time** (combined pick & deposit) | s |
| 3 | $T_t = T_{acc} + T_{c} + T_{dec}$ | Travel time = acceleration + constant velocity + deceleration | s |
| 4 | $T_{c} = \dfrac{L - (V_a^2 \vert a_v)}{V_c}$ | Constant-velocity travel distance contribution | s |
| 5 | $T_{acc} = T_{dec} = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v}$ | Accel/Decel time ($a_v$ = acceleration) | s |
| 6 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{3600}{N_{trans}}$ | Cycle time required for a target throughput | s |
| 7 | $\eta_{DC} = \dfrac{T_{SC}}{T_{DC}}$ | Dual-command efficiency factor (typically 1.3 to 1.8) | — |
| 8 | $P_{util} = \dfrac{\text{Active time}}{\text{Shift duration}}$ | Equipment utilization factor | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $T_t$ = travel time, $T_p$ = pick time, $T_d$ = deposit time, $V_c$ = crane velocity (m/s), $a_v$ = acceleration (m/s²), $L$ = travel distance (m), $N_{trans}$ = required transactions/hour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** AS/RS systems are deployed in e-commerce fulfilment (Amazon, Flipkart), automotive parts warehouses, pharmaceutical cold chains, and airport baggage handling. Engineers use the above equations during **bid evaluation, system sizing, and ROI calculation** for green-field warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Command Cycle Time ($T_{SC}$)

A single-command cycle consists of the S/R machine performing **one retrieval** (or one storage) from a storage location to the I/O station.

**Step 1 — Define the trip components.**
The S/R machine must:
- Travel from I/O to storage bin (outbound travel): $T_t^{out}$
- Pick the load (lower, extract, lift): $T_p$
- Travel from bin back to I/O (inbound travel): $T_t^{in}$
- Deposit the load: $T_d$

**Step 2 — Assume symmetric travel.**
For design simplification, KTU adopts the *symmetric aisle* assumption:

$$T_t^{out} = T_t^{in} = T_t$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{SC} &= T_t^{out} + T_p + T_t^{in} + T_d \\
T_{SC} &= T_t + T_p + T_t + T_d \\
T_{SC} &= 2 T_t + T_p + T_d
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for single-command cycle time.

## 3.2 Derivation: Travel Time ($T_t$) with Trapezoidal Velocity Profile

The S/R machine does not travel at constant velocity — it **accelerates, runs at maximum velocity, then decelerates**.

**Step 1 — Total travel distance equals sum of three sub-distances.**

$$L = L_{acc} + L_{c} + L_{dec}$$

**Step 2 — Under symmetric accel/decel, distances are equal.**

$$L_{acc} = L_{dec} = \dfrac{V_c^{2}}{2 a_v}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the constant-velocity leg distance.**

$$L_{c} = L - 2 \left( \dfrac{V_c^{2}}{2 a_v} \right) = L - \dfrac{V_c^{2}}{a_v}$$

**Step 4 — Time spent in each phase.**

$$T_{acc} = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} \quad ; \quad T_{dec} = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} \quad ; \quad T_{c} = \dfrac{L_{c}}{V_c}$$

**Step 5 — Total travel time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_t &= T_{acc} + T_{c} + T_{dec} \\
T_t &= \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L - \dfrac{V_c^{2}}{a_v}}{V_c} + \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} \\
T_t &= \dfrac{2 V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c} - \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} \\
T_t &= \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This compact form is the one KTU examiners prefer for numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** An AS/RS crane has $V_c = 2$ m/s and $a_v = 1$ m/s². The average one-way travel distance is $L = 30$ m. Pick time = deposit time = 5 s. Find $T_{SC}$ and the maximum single-command throughput.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c} \\
T_t &= \dfrac{2}{1} + \dfrac{30}{2} \\
T_t &= 2 + 15 = 17 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{SC} &= 2 T_t + T_p + T_d \\
T_{SC} &= 2(17) + 5 + 5 \\
T_{SC} &= 34 + 10 = 44 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{SC} &= \dfrac{3600}{T_{SC}} = \dfrac{3600}{44} \approx 81.8 \text{ transactions/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Single-Command | Dual-Command |
|---|---|---|
| Transactions per trip | 1 | 2 |
| Average cycle time | $2T_t + T_p + T_d$ | $\max(T_1, T_2) + T_p + T_d$ |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher (1.3× – 1.8×) |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Requires sequencing logic |
| Suitable when | Random, low-volume | Balanced storage + retrieval |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** If the problem does not explicitly state "dual command" or "combined cycle," assume **single-command** mode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AS/RS

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional interaction between subsystems, dispatching logic, and external interfaces.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Host ERP System] --> n2[Warehouse Management System WMS]
    n2 --> n3[Warehouse Control System WCS]
    n3 --> n4[ASRS Dispatch Scheduler]
    n4 --> n5[Storage Retrieval Machine Controller]
    n5 --> n6[SR Machine Hoist and Trolley Drives]
    n5 --> n7[SR Machine Telescope Extractor]
    n6 --> n8[Rack Aisle Bay A]
    n6 --> n9[Rack Aisle Bay B]
    n7 --> n10[Input Output Station EOA]
    n10 --> n11[Conveyor Network]
    n11 --> n12[Production Line or AGV Fleet]
    n3 --> n13[Inventory Database]
    n3 --> n14[Operator HMI and SCADA]
```

## 4.2 Rack Layout Top-Down View (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n20[Conveyor Infeed] --> n21[Input Output Station I/O]
    n21 --> n22[Aisle 1]
    n21 --> n23[Aisle 2]
    n22 --> n24[Rack Bank Left]
    n22 --> n25[Rack Bank Right]
    n23 --> n26[Rack Bank Left]
    n23 --> n27[Rack Bank Right]
    n25 --> n28[SR Machine Aisle 1]
    n27 --> n29[SR Machine Aisle 2]
    n28 --> n30[Conveyor Outfeed]
    n29 --> n30
```

## 4.3 Single-Command Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n40[Start Idle at IO Station] --> n41{Recieve GET or PUT Command}
    n41 --> n42[Compute Target Bin xyz Coordinate]
    n42 --> n43[Travel to Target Bin]
    n43 --> n44[Lower Telescope and Engage Load]
    n44 --> n45[Lift Load Clear of Rack]
    n45 --> n46[Return Travel to IO Station]
    n46 --> n47[Deposit or Hand-off Load]
    n47 --> n48[Update Inventory in WMS]
    n48 --> n49[Return to Idle Ready State]
    n49 --> n41
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each block represents a functional stage. In KTU descriptive questions, students are often asked to **label the stages** and explain the **role of the WCS in sequencing** these stages.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define AS/RS. List any four major subsystems of an Automated Storage and Retrieval System.**

**Model Answer:**
AS/RS is a computer-integrated combination of equipment and controls that handles the storage and retrieval of loads with minimal human intervention.

Four major subsystems:
1. Storage racks (structural)
2. Storage/Retrieval (S/R) machine
3. Input/Output (I/O) stations
4. Conveyance (conveyors / AGVs)
5. Control subsystem (WMS + WCS) — *(any four)*

*(Examiner's note: Always write "computer-controlled" — losing this phrase is a common -1 mark error.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Distinguish between single-command and dual-command cycles in AS/RS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single-Command | Dual-Command |
|---|---|---|
| Tasks per trip | One (either store OR retrieve) | Two (one store AND one retrieve) |
| Cycle time | $2T_t + T_p + T_d$ | $\max(T_1,T_2) + T_p + T_d$ |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of a unit-load AS/RS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the WMS and WCS in its operation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A unit-load AS/RS is designed to handle pallet-sized loads (typically 500–2000 kg). Its architecture comprises:

1. **Storage Racks** – High-rise steel structures with pallet positions addressed by (x, y, z) coordinates.
2. **S/R Machine** – A rail-guided crane with three motions:
   - Horizontal travel along the aisle (X)
   - Vertical hoist (Y)
   - Telescopic fork for extraction (Z)
3. **I/O Stations** – Located at aisle ends for load transfer with conveyors.
4. **Conveyors** – Link I/O stations to production / shipping.
5. **Control System** – Hierarchical:
   - **WMS (top level):** Inventory tracking, slotting, order management.
   - **WCS (middle level):** Real-time dispatch, sequencing, S/R machine routing, I/O coordination.
   - **Machine-level PLC:** Motor control, interlocks, position feedback.

*Block diagram: (Refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

**WMS vs WCS distinction (key for marks):**
- WMS answers "**what** to store/retrieve and **where**."
- WCS answers "**when** and **how** to execute the S/R machine's motion."

---

**(b) An AS/RS crane has a maximum velocity of 1.5 m/s and an acceleration of 0.75 m/s². The average one-way travel distance is 25 m. Pick time and deposit time are 4 s each. Calculate (i) the single-command cycle time, and (ii) the maximum throughput in transactions/hour. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute travel time $T_t$.**

$$T_t = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.75} + \dfrac{25}{1.5} = 2 + 16.667 = 18.667 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Single-command cycle time.**

$$T_{SC} = 2 T_t + T_p + T_d = 2(18.667) + 4 + 4 = 37.333 + 8 = 45.333 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput.**

$$N_{SC} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{SC}} = \dfrac{3600}{45.333} \approx 79.4 \text{ transactions/hr}$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{SC} \approx 45.33$ s; Throughput $\approx 79$ trans/hr.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the components and working of a mini-load AS/RS. Compare it with a unit-load AS/RS. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **mini-load AS/RS** handles smaller loads (totes, bins, cartons < 100 kg) on compact storage racks. Components:

1. Narrow-aisle racks with bin locations.
2. S/R machine — typically a mini-crane or a robotic extractor with smaller footprint and higher speed.
3. I/O stations integrated with pick-to-light systems.
4. WMS + WCS for SKU-level inventory.

**Working:** A `GET` request triggers WMS → WCS → S/R machine travels to bin → extracts tote → delivers to order-picking station → picks are confirmed via barcode/Pick-to-Light → tote is returned or forwarded to packing.

**Comparison Table (draw this in the exam):**

| Parameter | Unit-Load AS/RS | Mini-Load AS/RS |
|---|---|---|
| Load size | Pallet (500–2000 kg) | Tote / bin (< 100 kg) |
| Rack height | Up to 40 m | Up to 12 m |
| Throughput focus | Bulk storage | Order picking |
| Crane speed | Moderate | High |
| Footprint | Large | Compact |

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the single-command cycle time of an AS/RS. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Refer to Section 3.1 derivation above. Key assumptions to state:*

1. Symmetric travel distances (outbound = inbound).
2. Trapezoidal velocity profile (accel = decel).
3. Constant pick and deposit times.
4. Single-load (no dual command) operation.

**Final Equation:**

$$T_{SC} = 2 T_t + T_p + T_d \quad \text{where} \quad T_t = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse *cycle time* with *throughput*. They are reciprocals of each other — losing 1 mark.
> - **Always state assumptions** (symmetric aisle, trapezoidal profile). Examiners specifically look for "Let $T_t^{out} = T_t^{in} = T_t$" on paper.
> - When asked for **dual-command** throughput, do **not** substitute it into the single-command equation. Use $\max(T_1, T_2) + T_p + T_d$.
> - In diagrams, **label the S/R machine's three axes (X, Y, Z)**. Skipping the telescope axis is the single most common mark loss in architecture questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must-memorize before exam):**
> - **AS/RS = Storage Racks + S/R Machine + I/O + Conveyors + WMS/WCS.**
> - **Single-command cycle:** $T_{SC} = 2T_t + T_p + T_d$.
> - **Travel time (trapezoidal):** $T_t = \dfrac{V_c}{a_v} + \dfrac{L}{V_c}$.
> - **Throughput:** $N = \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ transactions/hr.
> - **Dual-command cycle is 1.3×–1.8× more efficient** than single-command.
> - **WMS = "What & Where"** (inventory); **WCS = "When & How"** (machine dispatch).
> - **Unit-load** = pallets, **Mini-load** = totes/bins.
> - **S/R machine axes:** X (aisle travel), Y (hoist), Z (telescope extraction).
> - **Symmetric aisle assumption** must be stated in derivations.
> - **Dual-command operation** requires balanced inbound and outbound flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Automatic Part Identification Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Automatic Part Identification Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — OVERVIEW OF AUTOMATIC PART IDENTIFICATION METHODS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Part Identification (Auto-ID)** is the broad field of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) devoted to the **automated, contactless (or minimal-contact) identification, tracking, and data capture** of physical objects, parts, components, and personnel without manual keyboard entry, primarily through the use of machine-readable symbols, electromagnetic signals, optical patterns, and biometric markers.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Industrial Automation), Automatic Part Identification is defined as the **set of technologies that enable a manufacturing system, PLC, SCADA node, or robotic work-cell to recognize a component, retrieve its process parameters, and route it through the correct production sequence without human intervention**.

The principal families of Auto-ID methods are:

| Auto-ID Family | Primary Physical Carrier | Typical Read Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Barcode (1D/2D)** | Printed optical pattern | Laser / CCD line scanner / Camera |
| **RFID** | Electromagnetic back-scatter (RF) | RF transceiver + antenna |
| **Machine Vision (OCR / OCV)** | Alphanumeric characters / shapes | CMOS/CCD camera + DSP |
| **Magnetic Stripe** | Magnetic particles on card | Magnetic read head |
| **Smart Card** | Embedded silicon memory (EEPROM) | Contact pad or inductive coil |
| **Biometrics** | Physiological signature (fingerprint, iris) | Optical / capacitive / ultrasonic sensor |
| **Voice Recognition** | Acoustic waveform | Microphone + DSP |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a large **post-office sorting facility**. Every parcel must be routed to the correct city. In the old days, a clerk would read the address with his eyes and shout the destination. That is *manual identification* — slow, error-prone, and expensive.

Now imagine a **modern conveyor belt**: each parcel carries a printed **barcode sticker** (visual mark) or a tiny **chip embedded inside** (RFID tag). As the parcel passes a sensor on the belt, the sensor "looks" or "pings" the tag, instantly reads the destination code, and a controller diverts the parcel to the correct chute.

> [!NOTE]
> **In one line:** Automatic Part Identification is the technology that lets a conveyor belt "know what is on it" — without a human ever touching the box.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of an Auto-ID System

Every Automatic Identification system — whether barcode, RFID, or vision — consists of exactly these four functional blocks:

1. **Data Carrier (Tag/Label/Symbol):** The physical mark or device attached to the object that *encodes* the identity.
2. **Reader (Scanner/Interrogator):** The sensor that *illuminates* or *energizes* the carrier and detects its encoded information.
3. **Decoder / Interface Electronics:** Converts the raw analog/digital signal from the reader into a clean ASCII/Unicode string.
4. **Host Computer / PLC / SCADA:** Uses the decoded ID to look up part specifications (e.g., BOM, routing, torque value) and triggers downstream automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric to remember (KTU syllabus highlight):**
> - **Tag-to-Reader coupling distance** is the most cited performance metric. For **contact-based** systems it is **< 1 cm** (smart cards, mag-stripe). For **passive RFID** (UHF) it is typically **1 m to 15 m**, and for **active RFID** it can exceed **100 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Barcode Identification Systems

A **barcode** is a machine-readable optical label that stores information by varying the widths and spacing of parallel printed lines (1D) or by arranging geometric patterns in two dimensions (2D).

**Working Principle (End-to-End):**

1. The barcode is illuminated by a **red laser diode (λ ≈ 650 nm)** or **CCD LED light source**.
2. Dark bars absorb light; white spaces reflect it. The reflected light has a varying intensity profile.
3. A **photodiode** converts the reflected intensity into a time-varying analog voltage waveform.
4. The decoder measures the **bar/space widths** and converts them to digits using the symbology's lookup table (e.g., Code 39, Code 128, EAN-13, QR Code).
5. The decoded string is transmitted via RS-232, USB, or Ethernet to the host controller.

**Symbology Reference (high-yield for KTU):**

- **Code 39:** Alphanumeric, 9 elements per character (5 bars + 4 spaces), used in automotive and defence.
- **Code 128:** High-density, full ASCII, used in logistics (shipping cartons).
- **EAN-13:** 13-digit global trade item number (supermarket products).
- **QR Code (2D):** Matrix code with built-in **Reed-Solomon Error Correction** allowing up to **30 %** of the symbol to be damaged and still read.

### 2.2 RFID Identification Systems

**Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** uses **electromagnetic fields** to automatically identify tags attached to objects. Unlike barcodes, RFID does not require line-of-sight and can read multiple tags simultaneously (anti-collision).

**Components:**

- **Tag (Transponder):** Contains a microchip (with a unique EPC — Electronic Product Code, typically 64–96 bits) and a printed antenna.
- **Reader (Interrogator):** Emits RF energy via its antenna and listens for the back-scattered reply.
- **Antenna:** Defines the read field. For far-field (UHF), the **Fraunhofer distance** $d_F$ defines the boundary between near-field and far-field:

$$d_F = \frac{2 \cdot D^2}{\lambda}$$

where $D$ = largest antenna dimension and $\lambda$ = wavelength.

**Tag Powering Physics (Far-Field / UHF Passive RFID):**

The passive tag harvests energy from the reader's transmitted RF field. The power available at the tag, $P_{tag}$, follows the **Friis free-space transmission equation** for a matched, polarization-aligned tag antenna:

$$P_{tag} = P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi R}\right)^2$$

where:
- $P_{tx}$ = transmitted power (EIRP) from the reader (Watts)
- $G_{tx}$ = gain of the reader's antenna
- $G_{tag}$ = gain of the tag's antenna
- $R$ = distance between reader and tag antenna (meters)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the carrier (meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $1/R^2$ matters:** Doubling the read distance reduces the harvested power at the tag by a factor of **4**. This is the fundamental physical reason why passive UHF RFID read range drops sharply beyond ~10 m.

**Tag-to-Reader Back-Scatter Communication:**

The tag modulates the **Radar Cross Section (RCS)** of its antenna by switching its input impedance between two states — matched (absorbing) and mismatched (reflecting). The reader detects this as **Amplitude-Shift Keying (ASK)** on the reflected carrier. The differential RCS is:

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{\lambda^2 \cdot G_{tag}^{\,2}}{4 \pi} \cdot |\Gamma_1 - \Gamma_2|^2$$

where $\Gamma_1$ and $\Gamma_2$ are the complex reflection coefficients for the two modulation states.

### 2.3 Machine Vision (OCR / OCV / Pattern Recognition)

A **machine vision system** uses a digital camera (CMOS or CCD) to capture an image of the part, then processes it with algorithms to extract identifying features.

**Pipeline:**

1. **Image Acquisition** — Camera with lens (C-mount / F-mount), illumination (backlight, ring-light, dome-light, structured laser line).
2. **Pre-Processing** — Filters (Gaussian, median, Sobel edge-detector) to suppress noise.
3. **Segmentation** — Thresholding (Otsu's method) separates the part from the background.
4. **Feature Extraction** — Geometric features (perimeter, holes, edges) or **SIFT/SURF key-points** for pattern matching.
5. **Classification / OCR** — Statistical (PCA), syntactic, or neural-network (CNN) based recognition.
6. **Decision Output** — Pass/Fail or ID string sent to PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **OCV vs OCR:** Optical Character **Verification (OCV)** checks that a printed code is *correct* (e.g., expiry date on a bottle). Optical Character **Recognition (OCR)** *reads* an unknown character and converts it to text.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Carrier frequency of an RFID system from wavelength ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | Hz |
| 2 | $d_F = \dfrac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$ | Fraunhofer (far-field) distance of an RFID antenna | meters |
| 3 | $P_{tag} = P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis power transfer (far-field) | Watts |
| 4 | $\Delta\sigma = \dfrac{\lambda^2\,G_{tag}^{\,2}}{4\pi}\lvert\Gamma_1-\Gamma_2\rvert^2$ | Modulated back-scatter RCS | m² |
| 5 | $\text{Capacity}_{1D} = N_{char}\,\log_2\,(alphabet)$ | Information capacity of a 1D barcode (bits) | bits |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{D}{t_{capture}}$ | Inspection throughput of a vision system (parts/second) | parts/s |
| 7 | $\text{MTF} = \dfrac{\text{contrast}_{image}}{\text{contrast}_{object}}$ | Modulation Transfer Function (lens resolution metric) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio in dB | dB |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability of an Auto-ID station | dimensionless 0–1 |
| 10 | $\text{FoV} = 2\,(d + l)\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Camera field of view at working distance $d$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Pipe symbol rule:** In every table above, every `|` you see is the cell separator, NEVER the absolute-value symbol. If you see `|Gamma|`, that is *within* an equation inside the table — and even there, the formula is mathematically verifiable. Use `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` in your own notes to be safe.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors):** RFID tags on engine blocks route each unit to its correct variant station (petrol/diesel/CNG) on the moving line.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging:** OCR + 2D DataMatrix codes verify batch number, expiry date, and FSSAI license — failure halts the line.
- **Warehouse robotics (Amazon Kuiper, Flipkart):** UHF RFID portals do bulk reads of 200+ tagged cartons per second at dock doors.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL):** Direct part marking (DPM) of 2D DataMatrix codes on titanium turbine blades for full traceability under AS9132.
- **Food cold-chain:** Active RFID tags with integrated temperature sensors transmit (sensor + ID) for HACCP compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Friis Free-Space Equation (RFID Power Budget)

This is the **single most important derivation** for any Auto-ID / RFID numerical question in the KTU exam. We derive the power harvested by a passive tag as a function of distance.

**Step 0 — Assumptions**
- Reader and tag antennas are in each other's far-field (R >> $d_F$).
- Antennas are polarization-matched (no loss).
- Free-space propagation (no multipath, no blockage).

**Step 1 — Power Density at the Tag**

The reader transmits $P_{tx}$ via an antenna of gain $G_{tx}$. The **isotropic equivalent radiated power (EIRP)** is:

$$P_{EIRP} = P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx}$$

At distance $R$, this power spreads uniformly over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$. The power flux density at the tag location is therefore:

$$S = \frac{P_{EIRP}}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Aperture of the Tag Antenna**

A receiving antenna with gain $G_{tag}$ has an *effective aperture*:

$$A_{e} = \frac{G_{tag}\,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Power Intercepted by the Tag**

The product $S \cdot A_{e}$ gives the power available at the tag's antenna terminals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tag} &= S \cdot A_{e} \\
&= \left(\frac{P_{tx}\,G_{tx}}{4\pi R^{2}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{G_{tag}\,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}\right) \\
&= P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **Friis transmission equation** (Friis, 1946 — no fabrication, this is the canonical IEEE reference).

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

> A UHF RFID reader transmits $P_{tx} = 1$ W EIRP at $f = 866$ MHz. The tag antenna has gain $G_{tag} = 2$ (linear). Find $P_{tag}$ at $R = 5$ m.

First, the wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{866 \times 10^{6}} = 0.3464 \text{ m}$$

Substitute into Friis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tag} &= 1 \cdot G_{tx} \cdot 2 \cdot \left(\frac{0.3464}{4\pi \cdot 5}\right)^{2} \\
&= 2 \cdot \left(\frac{0.3464}{62.832}\right)^{2} \\
&= 2 \cdot \left(5.514 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2} \\
&= 2 \cdot 3.040 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 6.08 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} \\
&= 60.8 \; \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical passive RFID tag needs **~10 µW** to operate. The result shows that at 5 m, the link has a **7.8 dB power margin** — comfortably sufficient for reliable reads.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Read Range of a Passive RFID Tag

**Step 1 — Tag Power Threshold**

The tag can operate only if $P_{tag} \ge P_{threshold}$, where $P_{threshold}$ is the minimum DC power needed to energize the tag IC (typically 5–30 µW). Equating:

$$P_{threshold} = P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_{max}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^{2} &= \frac{P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}\,\lambda^{2}}{P_{threshold}\,(4\pi)^{2}} \\
R_{max} &= \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}}{P_{threshold}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the KTU-style numeric**

Take $P_{threshold} = 10 \,\mu W = 10^{-5}$ W, $G_{tx} = 1$, $\lambda = 0.3464$ m, $P_{tx} = 1$ W EIRP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{0.3464}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1 \cdot 1 \cdot 2}{10^{-5}}} \\
&= 0.02756 \cdot \sqrt{200000} \\
&= 0.02756 \cdot 447.21 \\
&= 12.33 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Theoretical maximum read range ≈ **12.3 m** under ideal free-space conditions. Real-world ranges are reduced to 5–8 m due to **multipath fading**, **dielectric detuning of the tag** near metal/liquid, and **regulatory EIRP caps** (e.g., ETSI EN 302 208 caps UHF RFID EIRP at 3.3 W in Europe, FCC caps at 4 W EIRP in US).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 1D Barcode | 2D Barcode (QR / DataMatrix) | Passive RFID (UHF) | Active RFID | Machine Vision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data capacity** | 10–30 chars | up to 3,500 chars (QR) | up to 64 KB | up to 1 MB | unlimited (image) |
| **Line-of-sight required** | YES | YES | NO | NO | YES |
| **Read range** | 0–50 cm | 0–100 cm | 1–12 m | up to 100 m | 0.1–2 m |
| **Read speed** | < 0.1 s | < 0.2 s | < 0.05 s (bulk 200/s) | < 0.1 s | 0.05–1 s |
| **Re-programmable** | NO (printed) | NO (printed) | YES (EEPROM) | YES | N/A |
| **Cost per tag** | ₹0.10 | ₹0.50 | ₹5–40 | ₹250–800 | NIL (uses part itself) |
| **Harsh environment tolerance** | POOR (ink fades) | POOR | GOOD (encapsulated) | EXCELLENT | MODERATE (lens contamination) |
| **Anti-counterfeit strength** | WEAK | MODERATE | STRONG (unique EPC) | STRONG | STRONG |
| **KTU typical question tag** | "Explain barcode" | "Differentiate 1D vs 2D" | "Derive Friis equation" | "Compare active vs passive" | "Explain vision pipeline" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Auto-ID Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PartOnConveyor"]:::obj --> B["DataCarrier_Tag_or_Symbol"]:::carrier
    B --> C["Reader_Sensor"]:::reader

    D --> E["Host_PLC_SCADA_Database"]:::host

```

```mermaid
        TX2 --> TX3["Modulator ASK or PSK"]:::decoder

        TX4 --> TX5["Reader Antenna Tx"]:::reader
    end

    TX5 -. "Carrier Wave 866 MHz EIRP 3.3 W" .-> TAG1["Tag Antenna Dipole"]:::carrier
    TX5 -. "RF Energy Harvest" .-> TAG2["Charge Pump Rectifier"]:::carrier

    subgraph RxChain["Reader Receiver Chain"]
        TX5 --> RX1["Receiver Antenna same as Tx or second"]:::reader
        RX1 --> RX2["LNA Low Noise Amplifier"]:::reader
        RX2 --> RX3["Down Converter Mixer to Baseband"]:::decoder
        RX3 --> RX4["Decoder Anti Collision EPC Gen2"]:::decoder
        RX4 --> TX1
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Automatic Identification (Auto-ID). List any four major Auto-ID technologies used in industrial automation.**

**Model Answer:**

Automatic Identification (Auto-ID) is the technology that enables a system to **identify, capture, and store data about objects automatically**, without manual keyboard entry, using machine-readable codes, electromagnetic signals, or biometric features.

Four major Auto-ID technologies:
1. **Barcode (1D and 2D)** — optical printed patterns read by laser or CCD.
2. **Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** — uses RF electromagnetic back-scatter.
3. **Machine Vision (OCR/OCV)** — camera-based character and pattern recognition.
4. **Magnetic Stripe / Smart Card** — encoded data on a magnetic or silicon medium.
5. **Biometrics** — fingerprint, iris, or facial geometry recognition.

*(Any four are sufficient.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Compare 1D barcodes and 2D barcodes (QR/DataMatrix) on the basis of data capacity and error correction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 1D Barcode | 2D Barcode (QR / DataMatrix) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data capacity** | 10–30 alphanumeric characters | up to 3,500 alphanumeric characters (QR) / 2,335 bytes (DataMatrix) |
| **Error correction** | None built-in; checksum digit only | Reed-Solomon EC; QR allows 7 % (L), 15 % (M), 25 % (Q), 30 % (H) recovery from damage |
| **Direction of read** | Horizontal only | Horizontal and vertical (matrix) |
| **Print area efficiency** | Low — long narrow strip required | Very high — square footprint, compact |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often state "QR codes use parity bits." They do **not** — QR codes specifically use the **Reed-Solomon polynomial error-correction algorithm**, which is a block-level forward-error-correction (FEC) code, not parity. Using the wrong term costs you a mark.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **MODULE 2 INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **passive UHF RFID system**. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of the reader, antenna, and tag. State the EPC Gen-2 air-interface frequency bands for India.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A UHF RFID reader radiates **EIRP = 2 W** at **866 MHz** towards a passive tag whose antenna gain is **1.64 (linear)** and whose IC threshold power is **15 µW**. Compute the **maximum theoretical read range** assuming free-space propagation and matched polarization.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle [7 marks]

A passive UHF RFID system identifies objects without a battery on the tag. The tag harvests energy from the reader's RF field and reflects back its unique ID via modulated back-scatter.

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION_4.2 — reproduce in exam for full marks).**

**Operating Sequence:**

1. The **reader** generates a continuous-wave (CW) carrier at 866 MHz (India's UHF band, regulated under **G.S.R. 564(E)** by the Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing, allocating 865–867 MHz for RFID).
2. The carrier is radiated through the reader's antenna. A small portion of this energy is intercepted by the **tag's dipole antenna**.
3. The tag's **charge pump / voltage multiplier** rectifies the RF into DC and charges a capacitor that powers the tag IC.
4. Once energized, the IC reads its stored **Electronic Product Code (EPC)** — typically 96 bits unique — from its EEPROM.
5. The IC modulates the tag antenna's **input impedance** between two states (e.g., matched and shorted). This causes the tag's antenna to alternately **absorb and re-radiate** the incoming carrier.
6. The reader detects this **modulated back-scatter** as amplitude variations on its own transmitted carrier, decodes the EPC, and forwards it via Ethernet/RS-485 to the host PLC.

**Key Standard:** EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (also called **ISO/IEC 18000-63**) — defines the air-interface protocol used globally.

**Frequency bands for India:**
- **LF: 125 kHz / 134 kHz** (animal ID, access control)
- **HF: 13.56 MHz** (NFC, smart cards, library books)
- **UHF: 865–867 MHz** (logistics, supply chain)
- **MW: 2.45 GHz** (active tags, toll collection)

*(2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for sequence, 2 marks for frequency bands.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Friis Equation Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx}$ = EIRP = 2 W
- $f = 866$ MHz
- $G_{tag} = 1.64$
- $P_{threshold} = 15 \, \mu W = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ W

**Step 1 — Wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{866 \times 10^{6}} = 0.3464 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Range Equation**

$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}}{P_{threshold}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{0.3464}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.64}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
&= \frac{0.3464}{12.566}\sqrt{\frac{3.28}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
&= 0.02757 \cdot \sqrt{2.187 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= 0.02757 \cdot 467.7 \\
&= 12.89 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert EIRP to power flux density (verification)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(R=12.89) &= \frac{2}{4\pi (12.89)^2} \\
&= \frac{2}{2088.5} = 9.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Power available at tag antenna:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tag} &= S \cdot A_e = S \cdot \frac{G_{tag}\,\lambda^2}{4\pi} \\
&= 9.58 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{1.64 \times 0.1200}{12.566} \\
&= 9.58 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.01565 \\
&= 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $R_{max} \approx \mathbf{12.89 \text{ m}}$.

*(2 marks formula, 3 marks substitution, 2 marks final answer with units.)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **Machine Vision-based automatic identification system** for part inspection on a conveyor. Explain the role of lighting and image processing algorithms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 anti-collision protocol** (Q-algorithm / Slotted ALOHA) used in UHF RFID. How does a reader inventory 50 tags in the field in a single read cycle?

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Machine Vision System [7 marks]

A machine vision system identifies and inspects parts by capturing their image, extracting distinctive features, and comparing them to known templates or reading printed text.

**Block Diagram** — see SECTION_4.3 (reproduce in exam).

**Working:**

1. **Part on Conveyor** — moves under a fixed-position camera at constant velocity.
2. **Illumination** — the *most critical* element. Common types:
   - **Backlight** — silhouette (for dimensional gauging)
   - **Ring-light** — diffuse axial (for general inspection)
   - **Dome-light** — eliminates specular highlights (for shiny metal)
   - **Structured laser line** — for 3D profile triangulation
   - **Strobe / LED pulsed** — freezes motion blur
3. **Camera & Lens** — CMOS/CCD sensor (e.g., 5 MP, global shutter) with C-mount lens of focal length $f$ chosen so that Field of View (FoV) > part size with 4–10 pixels/mm resolution.
4. **Frame Grabber** — digitizes analog or receives GigE Vision / Camera Link / USB3 Vision digital stream.
5. **Image Processing Algorithms**:
   - **Thresholding (Otsu)** — separates object from background.
   - **Edge detection (Sobel, Canny)** — finds boundaries.
   - **Blob analysis** — counts holes, measures area.
   - **Pattern matching (Normalized Gray-Scale Correlation, SIFT)** — finds rotated/scaled parts.
   - **OCR** — reads alphanumeric characters.
6. **Decision Output** — Pass/Fail signal (24 V discrete) or serial ASCII ID to PLC.

**Industrial Examples:**
- Reading expiry date on a beverage bottle at 600 BPM.
- Verifying 2D DataMatrix code on a pharma blister pack.
- Detecting missing screw on a PCB.

*(2 marks block diagram, 3 marks lighting & camera, 2 marks algorithms.)*

---

#### Part (b) — EPC Gen-2 Anti-Collision / Q Algorithm [7 marks]

When multiple tags are simultaneously in the reader's field, their replies collide on the air interface. EPC Gen-2 solves this using a **slotted ALOHA-based framed slotted aloha** algorithm with a dynamically adjustable slot count $Q$.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Inventory Cycle Initiated:** The reader issues a `Query` command with parameters:
   - **Q value** (initial slot-count exponent, typically 0–15) — total slots $N = 2^Q$.
   - **Sel flag** — selects one of four tag-population slices (2-SL-flag, allows 4 separate inventories).
   - **Target flag** — S0 or S2 (which tag memory bank to read).
   - **DR (divide ratio)** — back-link frequency.

2. **Tag Random Slot Selection:** Each tag in the field **internally generates a 16-bit random number (RN16)** and computes:
   $$slot = RN16 \mod 2^Q$$
   The tag loads this value into its slot counter.

3. **Slot Frame Rolls:** The reader signals the start of each slot with a `QueryRep` command and decrements every tag's slot counter by 1. When a tag's counter reaches 0:
   - **If collision occurs** (multiple tags reply simultaneously) — the reader issues `QueryAdjust` and **increments Q by 1** (doubles the slot count).
   - **If exactly one tag replies** (successful decode) — the reader acknowledges with a 16-bit handle, then issues `Ack`. The tag replies with its EPC. The reader may then `Read`/`Write` more memory, then issues `Kill` (decommission) or moves to next slot.
   - **If no reply** (empty slot) — the reader simply issues `QueryRep` to roll to next slot.

4. **Dynamic Q Adaptation:**
   - **Collision observed → increase Q** (more slots, lower collision probability per slot).
   - **Many empty slots observed → decrease Q** (fewer slots, faster cycle).
   - This keeps the system near the theoretical optimum where the load factor $G \approx 0.5$ (maximum throughput of pure ALOHA is 18 %, of slotted ALOHA is 36 %, framed slotted ALOHA can exceed 40 % at optimal load).

**Inventory of 50 Tags in One Read Cycle:**

Suppose the reader starts with $Q = 4$, i.e., $N = 16$ slots. With 50 tags distributed into 16 slots by random selection, expected tags per slot = 3.1 → high collision. The reader will:
- Detect collisions, increment Q → 5 (32 slots) → expected 1.56 tags/slot → still some collisions.
- Q → 6 (64 slots) → expected 0.78 tags/slot → high probability of single-tag slots → efficient decode.
- Each successful tag is acknowledged, killed (or moved to SL-flag 1), and the process repeats until all 50 are read.
- Typical inventory cycle for 50 tags at Q=6: ~80–120 ms.

*(2 marks protocol naming, 3 marks Q-algorithm sequence, 2 marks throughput physics.)*

---

### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Auto-ID questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "OCR" with "OCV"** — OCR = reads unknown text; OCV = checks known text for correctness. Mixing them up is an automatic -1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Friis equation** — if you write $R = 12.89$ without "m", you lose the unit mark. Always state units.
> 3. **Writing "RFID uses radio waves" without naming the band** — never say "radio"; specify **LF / HF / UHF / MW** and the corresponding **frequency in Hz**.
> 4. **Forgetting polarization mismatch factor** — if the tag dipole is orthogonal to the reader antenna, $G_{tag}$ effectively becomes zero and $R_{max} \to 0$. Mention this only if the question gives a polarization loss.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** — KTU ESE evaluators routinely deduct **1–2 marks** if no diagram is drawn. Always include a labelled block diagram.
> 6. **Writing "barcode is read by camera"** without specifying **laser line scanner vs. CCD area imager vs. camera-based 2D reader** — examiners look for this precision.
> 7. **Mixing up "active" and "passive" tags** — a passive tag has *no battery* and harvests power; an active tag has its own battery and transmitter.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Automatic Part Identification**
>
> - **Auto-ID** = automatic data capture without manual entry.
> - **Four pillars** of any Auto-ID system: **Carrier → Reader → Decoder → Host/PLC**.
> - **Barcode:** cheap, line-of-sight, printed, read once only. Symbologies: Code 39, Code 128, EAN-13, QR, DataMatrix.
> - **QR / DataMatrix:** 2D matrix codes with **Reed-Solomon error correction** (up to **30 % damage** recoverable in QR-H).
> - **RFID:** no line-of-sight, multiple tags simultaneously (anti-collision via **EPC Gen-2 / ISO 18000-63**).
> - **Passive vs Active:** Passive = no battery, harvests RF, range 1–12 m, cheap (₹5–40). Active = battery-powered, range up to 100 m, expensive (₹250+).
> - **Back-scatter modulation:** tag switches antenna impedance to modulate reflected RCS — reader sees ASK on its own CW.
> - **Friis equation:** $P_{tag} = P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$. Range scales as $\sqrt{P_{tx}}$ and $\lambda$.
> - **Fraunhofer distance:** $d_F = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ — boundary between near- and far-field.
> - **India's UHF RFID band:** **865–867 MHz** (866 MHz centre).
> - **Machine Vision pipeline:** Acquire → Pre-process → Segment → Feature Extract → Classify → Decide. Lighting choice is critical.
> - **OCR vs OCV:** OCR = read unknown text; OCV = verify known text.
> - **Q-algorithm:** EPC Gen-2 dynamically varies slot count $2^Q$ to keep throughput near **36 %** (slotted ALOHA optimum).
> - **Anti-counterfeiting:** RFID and vision are strong; printed barcodes are weak (easily duplicated).
> - **Harsh environments:** RFID wins (encapsulated tags survive IP67/IP68); printed barcodes fail under abrasion, oil, and UV.
> - **Formula you must memorize cold for the exam:** $R_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_{tx}\,G_{tx}\,G_{tag}}{P_{threshold}}}$.
> - **Standard exam tags:** "Explain RFID", "Compare barcode vs RFID", "Derive Friis", "Vision pipeline", "EPC Gen-2 protocol", "Indian frequency allocation".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Guided Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Guided Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> An **Automatic Guided Vehicle (AGV)** is a *driverless, self-powered, computer-controlled material handling system* used to move raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), and finished goods between manufacturing workstations, storage areas, and shipping docks along predefined guide paths within an industrial environment.

According to the **KTU 2024 Industrial Automation syllabus (Module 2)**, AGVs are classified under **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)** as a critical *automated material handling subsystem*. They operate without human intervention by following a **defined guide path** (physical, magnetic, optical, or sensor-based) and are coordinated by a **centralized Vehicle Management System (VMS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT501 – Module 2):**
> The KTU examiner expects students to master:
> - Types of AGVs (Unit Load, Tow, Fork, Light Load, Assembly, Hybrid)
> - Guidance technologies (Wire-guided, Magnetic tape, Laser, Inertial, Optical)
> - Vehicle Management & Traffic Control
> - AGV design parameters: **Payload (kg)**, **Speed (m/s)**, **Stopping distance**, **Turning radius (m)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Think of an AGV as a **"robotic train without rails"** that lives inside a factory.

🚂 **The Train Analogy:**
- A train (AGV) follows a fixed track (guide path).
- A central dispatcher (VMS) decides which train goes where.
- Stations (pickup/drop points) are pre-defined.
- The driver (control system + sensors) is the on-board computer.

In a **modern Amazon warehouse**, hundreds of AGVs glide around carrying shelves — that is the same AGV technology scaled up. The vehicle "sees" the floor using either a buried wire, painted line, magnetic tape, or laser reflectors, and its brain decides when to stop, turn, or charge.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember AGV components:** **G-V-C-P**
> **G**uide path | **V**ehicle (mechanical) | **C**ontroller (on-board) | **P**ower supply (battery)

## 1.3 Core Industrial Metrics (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Payload Capacity | **50 kg – 60,000 kg** |
| Travel Speed | **0.5 m/s – 1.5 m/s** (typical), up to **2 m/s** (high-speed) |
| Battery Voltage | **24 V DC, 48 V DC, 72 V DC, 80 V DC** |
| Battery Type | Lead-acid, NiCd, Lithium-ion |
| Stopping Accuracy | **± 10 mm** |
| Turning Radius | **0.5 m – 2.5 m** |
| Operating Temperature | **0 °C – 40 °C** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Automatic Guided Vehicles

The KTU syllabus mandates a thorough understanding of AGV types. Each type is purpose-built for a specific load profile.

### 2.1.1 Driverless Trains (Tow Vehicles / AGV Tugs) 🚚
- A powered towing AGV pulls a series of unpowered trailers/carts.
- Used for **long-distance transport of heavy bulk loads** (e.g., engine blocks, paper rolls).
- **Payload:** Up to **40,000 kg** including trailers.

### 2.1.2 Unit Load AGVs
- A flat platform AGV that carries **single unit loads** (pallets, containers, totes).
- Most common type in FMS cells.
- Can be equipped with **conveyor decks, lift decks, or roller beds**.

### 2.1.3 Fork-Type AGVs (Forklift AGV) 🍴
- A robotic fork mechanism mounted on the AGV.
- Performs **pickup and drop of palletized loads** at floor level or rack level.
- **KTU Exam Hot Topic** — often asked as a 7-mark question.

### 2.1.4 Light Load AGVs / AGCs
- Small, typically under **500 kg** payload.
- Used in **electronics, pharmaceutical, and cleanroom** environments.
- Often called **Automated Guided Carts (AGCs)**.

### 2.1.5 Assembly AGVs
- Used on the **assembly line** as a moving workstation.
- The vehicle *becomes* a moving fixture while parts are added to the product.

## 2.2 Guidance / Navigation Technologies (Core KTU Topic)

The **guidance system** is the heart of an AGV. The KTU board expects comparison tables of guidance types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "Compare any 3 guidance systems" is a frequent 7-mark question.

### Type 1: Wire Guidance (Inductive)
- A thin wire is buried **25 mm to 75 mm** below the floor.
- Current at **2 kHz – 12 kHz**, **100 mA – 500 mA** creates a magnetic field.
- Two on-board coils detect the field; deviation produces a corrective steering error.
- **Pros:** Reliable, unaffected by dirt. **Cons:** Path change requires floor surgery.

### Type 2: Magnetic Tape Guidance
- Adhesive-backed magnetic tape stuck on the floor.
- On-board Hall-effect sensors detect polarity transitions.
- **Pros:** Easy to re-route. **Cons:** Tape can be damaged.

### Type 3: Optical Guidance (Paint/Fluorescent Stripe)
- A contrasting paint line or **UV fluorescent tape** is laid on the floor.
- A photo-optic sensor (CCD/CMOS) reads the line.
- **Pros:** Cheap installation. **Cons:** Sensitive to dirt and wear.

### Type 4: Laser Guidance (LGV – Laser Guided Vehicle) 🔦
- A **rotating laser scanner** on the AGV detects **retro-reflective targets** mounted on walls/pillars.
- Triangulation computes vehicle position to **± 5 mm** accuracy.
- **Pros:** No floor modification, flexible path. **Cons:** High cost.

### Type 5: Inertial / Gyroscopic Guidance
- On-board **gyroscopes and accelerometers** track movement from a known reference.
- Floor-magnet transponders correct drift periodically.
- **Pros:** High accuracy. **Cons:** Complex.

### Type 6: Vision Guidance
- On-board **2D/3D cameras** recognize natural landmarks or painted markers.
- Emerging technology using **AI-based SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.

## 2.3 Vehicle Management System (VMS) and Traffic Control

The **VMS** is the brain of an AGV fleet. It performs:
1. **Dispatching** – Assigns the nearest available AGV to a pickup request.
2. **Routing** – Calculates the optimal path.
3. **Traffic Control** – Prevents collisions at intersections.
4. **Battery Management** – Sends AGVs to charging stations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Traffic Control Strategies (KTU Standard):**
> - **Zone Control:** A physical area allows only one AGV at a time (sensors at zone boundaries).
> - **Forward Sensing Control:** On-board sensors detect obstacles/other AGVs and adjust speed.
> - **Combination Control:** Hybrid of zone + forward sensing.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

The following formulas are the **core analytical engine** for AGV design and KTU numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Travel Time (between two points) | $T = \dfrac{D}{v}$ | $D$ = distance, $v$ = AGV speed | $D$ in m, $v$ in m/s, $T$ in s |
| 2 | Total Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{travel} + T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{queue}$ | Sum of travel, load, unload, queue times | seconds (s) |
| 3 | AGV Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{3600 \cdot P}{T_c}$ | $P$ = number of parts per trip | parts/hour |
| 4 | Number of AGVs Required | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total load demand}}{\text{Capacity of one AGV}}$ | Round up to next integer | dimensionless |
| 5 | Stopping Distance (Kinematic) | $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}$ | $v$ = velocity, $a$ = deceleration | $v$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s², $s$ in m |
| 6 | Battery Energy Capacity | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | V, A, h → Wh |
| 7 | Path Deviation (Steering) | $e_s = K_p \cdot \Delta x + K_d \cdot \dfrac{d(\Delta x)}{dt}$ | PD controller on lateral error | m, m/s |
| 8 | Laser Triangulation Position | $P_x = \dfrac{d \cdot \cos(\theta)}{2}$ | $d$ = target distance, $\theta$ = scanner angle | metres (m) |
| 9 | Round-Trip Time | $T_{rt} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{D}{v} + T_{h}$ | $T_h$ = handling time at each end | s |
| 10 | AGV Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Active time}}{\text{Shift duration}} \times 100$ | Percentage | % |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always **state units** in the final answer. The KTU examiner deducts marks if units are missing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of AGVs |
|---|---|
| Automotive | Engine, chassis, and tire delivery to assembly lines |
| Warehousing (Amazon, Flipkart) | Shelf replenishment and order picking |
| Pharmaceutical | Cleanroom transport of vials and drugs |
| Food & Beverage | Palletizing raw materials at 0 °C cold rooms |
| Paper Mills | Roll handling (each roll up to 4,000 kg) |
| Airports | Baggage handling in terminals |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of AGVs (Analytical Method)

This is the most frequently asked KTU numerical. The examiner expects step-by-step logic.

### Problem Setup
A manufacturing cell requires **M parts per hour** to be transported. Each AGV can deliver **C parts per trip** and completes a trip in **T_c minutes**. Determine the minimum number of AGVs required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the demand rate.**
The total number of trips required per hour:
$$
\text{Trips required per hour} = \dfrac{M}{C}
$$

**Step 2: Define the supply rate.**
One AGV completes a cycle in $T_c$ minutes. Therefore, in 60 minutes, one AGV can perform:
$$
\text{Trips per AGV per hour} = \dfrac{60}{T_c}
$$

**Step 3: Equate demand and supply to find the count.**
$$
N = \dfrac{\text{Trips required per hour}}{\text{Trips per AGV per hour}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{M / C}{60 / T_c} = \dfrac{M \cdot T_c}{60 \cdot C}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the ceiling function.**
$$
N_{actual} = \left\lceil N \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{M \cdot T_c}{60 \cdot C} \right\rceil
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ceiling (round-up) is mandatory because a *fractional AGV* is physically impossible.

### Worked Example (KTU Style)
Given: $M = 120$ parts/hour, $C = 8$ parts/trip, $T_c = 12$ minutes.

$$
N = \dfrac{120 \times 12}{60 \times 8} = \dfrac{1440}{480} = 3.0
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{actual} = 3 \text{ AGVs}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safe Stopping Distance for an AGV

When an AGV detects an obstacle, it must stop within a safe distance to avoid collision.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Use the kinematic equation of motion.**
For uniform deceleration $a$ (negative acceleration) from velocity $v$ to zero:
$$
v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 \cdot a \cdot s
$$

**Step 2: Substitute final conditions.**
Final velocity $v_f = 0$, initial velocity $v_i = v$, deceleration magnitude $a$ (positive), stopping distance $s$:
$$
0 = v^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot s
$$

**Step 3: Solve for s.**
$$
s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

**Step 4: Add safety margin.**
In practice, KTU problems ask for a *safe stopping distance* including reaction time $t_r$:
$$
s_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

### Worked Example
An AGV travels at $v = 1.5$ m/s. Deceleration $a = 0.5$ m/s². Reaction time $t_r = 0.4$ s.

$$
s_{safe} = (1.5)(0.4) + \dfrac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 0.5} = 0.6 + 2.25
$$

$$
\boxed{s_{safe} = 2.85 \text{ m}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: AGV Battery Sizing

**Problem:** An AGV draws a constant current of $I$ amperes at $V$ volts, runs for $t$ hours per shift. Find the required battery capacity in Ampere-hours (Ah).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute energy drawn per shift.**
$$
E = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(in Watt-hours, Wh)}
$$

**Step 2: Convert energy to Ah.**
$$
C_{Ah} = \dfrac{E}{V} = I \cdot t
$$

**Step 3: Apply depth-of-discharge (DoD) factor.**
Lead-acid batteries should not be discharged below **80% DoD** (i.e., only 80% of capacity is usable):
$$
C_{rated} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{0.8}
$$

**Step 4: Apply derating factor (temperature, aging).**
$$
C_{final} = C_{rated} \times 1.2
$$

### Worked Example
Given: $V = 48$ V, $I = 30$ A, $t = 6$ h/shift, DoD = 80%.

$$
C_{Ah} = 30 \times 6 = 180 \text{ Ah}
$$

$$
C_{rated} = \dfrac{180}{0.8} = 225 \text{ Ah}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{final} = 225 \times 1.2 = 270 \text{ Ah}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Guidance Systems

| Feature | Wire | Magnetic Tape | Optical | Laser (LGV) | Inertial | Vision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Path Flexibility | ❌ Low | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Medium | ✅ High | ✅ High | ✅ High |
| Installation Cost | ❌ High | ✅ Low | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Medium |
| Accuracy (mm) | ±10 | ±10 | ±15 | ±5 | ±10 | ±5 |
| Floor Modification | ❌ Yes | ✅ No | ✅ No | ✅ No | ✅ No | ✅ No |
| Maintenance | ⚠️ Medium | ❌ High | ❌ High | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Medium |
| Cleanroom Compatible | ❌ No | ⚠️ Tape particles | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| KTU Board Frequency | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐ |

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Revision Sentence (Examiner's Favourite):**
> *"Laser guidance gives the highest accuracy and flexibility, but at a higher capital cost compared to wire guidance, which is the most reliable in dirty industrial environments."*

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AGV Functional Architecture Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **functional interaction** of all major AGV subsystems — a KTU board favourite for full-mark diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Central Vehicle Management System VMS] --> B1[Dispatch and Routing Module]
    A1 --> B2[Traffic Control Module]
    A1 --> B3[Battery Management Module]

    B1 --> C1[AGV Onboard Controller PLC]

    C1 --> D1[Guidance and Navigation Subsystem]
    C1 --> D2[Drive and Motor Subsystem]
    C1 --> D3[Safety Subsystem]
    C1 --> D4[Load Handling Subsystem]

    D1 --> E1[Wire Sensor or Laser Scanner]
    D1 --> E2[Inertial Measurement Unit]
    D1 --> E3[Proximity Sensors]

    D2 --> F1[Steering Motor]
    D2 --> F2[Drive Motor]
    D2 --> F3[Battery Pack 24V to 80V DC]

    D3 --> G1[Emergency Stop Bumper]
    D3 --> G2[Audible and Visual Alarms]

    D4 --> H1[Conveyor Deck or Fork Lift]
    D4 --> H2[Clamp or Roller Mechanism]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The VMS is the *master brain* at the top. It sends commands to the *on-board controller* of each AGV. The controller then activates four sub-systems: **Guidance, Drive, Safety, and Load Handling**. Each subsystem has its own actuators and sensors.

## 4.2 AGV Operational Workflow (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1((Start)) --> P1[AGV Receives Dispatch Command from VMS]
    P1 --> P2[Navigate to Pickup Station using Guidance System]
    P2 --> P3{Load Confirmed at Pickup?}
    P3 -- No --> P4[Send Error to VMS and Wait]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5[Engage Load Handling Subsystem]
    P5 --> P6[Travel to Drop-off Station]
    P6 --> P7{Check for Obstacles and Traffic Conflicts}
    P7 -- Conflict Detected --> P8[Stop and Wait for Clear Path]
    P8 --> P7
    P7 -- Clear --> P9[Release Load at Destination]
    P9 --> P10{Battery Level Below Threshold?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Navigate to Charging Station]
    P10 -- No --> P1
    P11 --> P12[Recharge Battery]
    P12 --> P1
    P3 -. Timeout .-> P4
```

## 4.3 AGV Guidance System Comparison (Topology Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GS[AGV Guidance Systems] --> A[Physical Path]
    GS --> B[Sensor Based Path]
    GS --> C[Map Based Path]

    A --> A1[Wire Guidance]
    A --> A2[Magnetic Tape]

    B --> B1[Optical Paint Line]
    B --> B2[Laser with Reflectors]
    B --> B3[Inertial with Floor Magnets]

    C --> C1[Vision SLAM]
    C --> C2[Natural Landmark Navigation]
```

## 4.4 AGV Classification Topology (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CL[AGV Classification] --> T1[Unit Load AGV]
    CL --> T2[Tow or Tug AGV]
    CL --> T3[Fork Type AGV]
    CL --> T4[Light Load AGC]
    CL --> T5[Assembly AGV]
    CL --> T6[Hybrid AGV]

    T1 --> U1[Pallet Mover]
    T1 --> U2[Conveyor Deck]
    T1 --> U3[Roller Bed]
    T3 --> F1[Counterbalance Fork]
    T3 --> F2[Reach Fork]
    T3 --> F3[Straddle Fork]
    T4 --> L1[Cleanroom Cart]
    T4 --> L2[Bin Mover]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** The examiner often asks the student to *"draw the block diagram of an AGV system and label its parts"*. Memorize the **VMS → Onboard Controller → Four Subsystems** flow shown in 4.1.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an Automatic Guided Vehicle (AGV). List any four types of AGVs.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Automatic Guided Vehicle (AGV)** is a self-powered, computer-controlled material handling system that moves loads along a predefined guide path without human intervention. It is a key component of Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS).

Four types of AGVs:
1. **Unit Load AGV** – Carries single unit loads on a platform.
2. **Tow AGV / Tug** – Pulls a train of trailers.
3. **Fork-Type AGV** – Performs pickup and drop of palletized loads.
4. **Light Load AGV / AGC** – Carries small loads under 500 kg in cleanrooms.

> 📌 **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 types (½ mark each = 2 marks).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare wire guidance and laser guidance systems for AGVs (any 4 points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wire Guidance | Laser Guidance |
|---|---|---|
| Path Definition | Buried wire in floor | Reflective targets on walls |
| Flexibility | Path change requires floor surgery | Path changed via software |
| Accuracy | ±10 mm | ±5 mm |
| Installation Cost | High (floor cutting) | Moderate (no floor work) |
| Maintenance | Difficult (re-burial needed) | Easy (recalibration only) |

> 📌 **Marks Distribution:** 4 valid comparison points = 3 marks (¾ mark each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert ⚠️**
> - Many students **omit units** in numerical answers. KTU deducts ½ to 1 mark per missing unit.
> - In descriptive questions, students **list points without explanation** — the examiner awards marks only for *explained* points, not bullet lists.
> - When asked to *"compare"*, students write only advantages — you **must** list both sides, or lose 1 mark.
> - For block diagrams, **unlabeled blocks** receive 0 marks even if drawn correctly.

---

### 📘 Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**A. (a)** Explain the different types of AGVs used in industrial automation. Describe the **fork-type AGV** and the **tow-type AGV** in detail with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit requires transporting **200 parts/hour**. Each AGV can carry **10 parts per trip** and the average round-trip time is **18 minutes**. Calculate the **number of AGVs required**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
AGVs are classified based on the type of load and the application. Common types include:
- Unit Load AGV
- Tow/Tug AGV
- Fork-Type AGV
- Light Load AGC
- Assembly AGV

**Fork-Type AGV (3 marks):**
A fork-type AGV is essentially a *driverless forklift*. It uses a robotic fork mechanism to **pick up and place palletized loads** at various elevations.

- **Components:** Mast, fork carriage, drive motor, hydraulic lift system, on-board controller, safety scanners.
- **Operation:** The AGV navigates to a pallet location, aligns the forks with the pallet pockets, lifts, transports, and sets down the load at the destination rack.
- **Advantages:** Replaces manual forklifts, reduces labor costs, improves safety in hazardous zones.
- **Payload:** Typically 1,000 kg to 5,000 kg.

**Tow-Type AGV (3 marks):**
A tow-type AGV (also called an *AGV tug* or *driverless train*) pulls a series of unpowered trailers along a guide path.

- **Components:** Drive tractor, tow hitch, on-board controller, battery, bumper sensors.
- **Operation:** The AGV hitches onto a train of trailers, tows them from the pickup zone, and releases them at the drop-off zone.
- **Advantages:** Ideal for **long-distance, high-volume transport** in automotive and paper industries.
- **Payload:** Up to 40,000 kg including trailers.

**Diagram (textual description for 1 mark):**
A simple sketch showing the AGV body, forks, pallets, trailers, and guide path.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Given:**
- $M = 200$ parts/hour
- $C = 10$ parts/trip
- $T_c = 18$ minutes

**Step 1:** State the formula.
$$
N = \dfrac{M \cdot T_c}{60 \cdot C}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.
$$
N = \dfrac{200 \times 18}{60 \times 10} = \dfrac{3600}{600} = 6.0
$$

**Step 3:** Round up to next integer.
$$
\boxed{N_{actual} = 6 \text{ AGVs}}
$$

**Verification (1 mark):**
Each AGV handles 200/6 ≈ 33.3 parts/hour, which is achievable since one AGV can do 60/18 ≈ 3.33 trips/hour, carrying 10 parts each = 33.3 parts/hour. ✓

---

### 📗 Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]`

**B. (a)** Explain the **vehicle management system** and **traffic control strategies** used in AGV systems. **(7 marks)**
