**(b)** An AGV travels at **1.2 m/s**. It must be able to stop safely within **1.5 m** when an obstacle is detected. The controller's reaction time is **0.3 seconds**. Calculate the **minimum deceleration** required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a):

**Vehicle Management System (VMS) – 4 marks:**

The VMS is the centralized software that manages an entire AGV fleet. Its functions are:

1. **Dispatching:** When a pickup request comes from a workstation, the VMS selects the *nearest available* AGV based on position and load.
2. **Routing:** Calculates the shortest/cheapest path from current position to destination, considering traffic and battery state.
3. **Traffic Control:** Prevents two AGVs from occupying the same path segment at the same time.
4. **Battery Management:** Monitors battery levels of all AGVs; redirects low-battery units to charging stations.
5. **Job Tracking:** Logs all transactions for production tracking and analytics.

**Traffic Control Strategies – 3 marks:**

| Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone Control** | Path is divided into zones; only one AGV allowed per zone. | Fixed-route dense traffic |
| **Forward Sensing Control** | On-board sensors detect other AGVs/obstacles; speed reduced or stopped. | Open layouts |
| **Combination Control** | Hybrid: zones for intersections, forward sensing for open paths. | Most modern FMS |

---

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Given:**
- $v = 1.2$ m/s
- $s_{safe} = 1.5$ m
- $t_r = 0.3$ s

**Step 1:** State the safe stopping distance formula.
$$
s_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values.
$$
1.5 = (1.2)(0.3) + \dfrac{(1.2)^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the reaction distance.
$$
1.5 = 0.36 + \dfrac{1.44}{2 \cdot a}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate $a$.
$$
1.14 = \dfrac{1.44}{2 \cdot a}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $a$.
$$
2 \cdot a = \dfrac{1.44}{1.14} = 1.2632
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{1.2632}{2} = 0.6316
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{min} \approx 0.632 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

**Physical interpretation (1 mark):**
A deceleration of 0.632 m/s² corresponds to about 0.064 g, which is comfortable for both the load and the AGV structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

✅ **Definition:** AGV = driverless, self-powered, computer-controlled material handling system on a predefined guide path.

✅ **5 Key AGV Types to Memorize:** Unit Load, Tow/Tug, Fork-Type, Light Load (AGC), Assembly AGV.

✅ **6 Guidance Systems:** Wire, Magnetic Tape, Optical, Laser (LGV), Inertial, Vision (SLAM).

✅ **Most Accurate Guidance:** Laser guidance (±5 mm) — favourite 7-mark question.

✅ **Cheapest Installation:** Magnetic tape and optical paint (no floor cutting).

✅ **Vehicle Management System (VMS):** Dispatch + Routing + Traffic Control + Battery Management.

✅ **Traffic Control:** Zone Control (single AGV per zone), Forward Sensing (on-board), Combination.

✅ **Key Formula — Number of AGVs:** 
$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{M \cdot T_c}{60 \cdot C} \right\rceil
$$

✅ **Key Formula — Safe Stopping Distance:** 
$$
s_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

✅ **Key Formula — Battery Sizing:** 
$$
C_{final} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{\text{DoD}} \times 1.2
$$

✅ **Typical Speeds:** 0.5 to 1.5 m/s (standard), up to 2 m/s (high-speed AGVs).

✅ **Payload Range:** 50 kg (AGC) to 60,000 kg (heavy tow AGV).

✅ **Most Common Application:** Automotive assembly lines + modern e-commerce warehouses.

✅ **Stop Accuracy:** ±10 mm for wire-guided, ±5 mm for laser-guided.

✅ **Block Diagram Order:** VMS → On-board Controller → Guidance, Drive, Safety, Load Handling.

✅ **Examiner Always Looks For:** Units in numerical answers, explained (not listed) points in theory, *both* sides of any "compare" question, and a **neat labeled diagram** in any 7-mark question.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection** refer to integrated arrangements of transducers, signal conditioning circuits, processing electronics, and decision-making algorithms that are deployed on the shop floor to **automatically evaluate manufactured components** for dimensional accuracy, geometric tolerancing, surface integrity, and functional conformance — without continuous human supervision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Focus):** Automated inspection sensors are the "eyes, ears, and fingertips" of a smart factory. They convert physical phenomena (light, sound, position, force, magnetic field) into electrical signals, which are then processed to generate GO/NO-GO decisions, statistical quality metrics, or feedback signals for closed-loop process control.

In the framework of **Industry 4.0 and Smart Manufacturing**, these sensors form the physical layer of the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, where every inspection event is logged, time-stamped, and made available for digital twins and predictive analytics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automated Doctor's Clinic"

Imagine a super-specialty **diagnostic clinic** where a patient walks in and within seconds, an array of instruments automatically measures:
- **Height & weight** (dimensional check),
- **Heartbeat & blood pressure** (functional check),
- **X-ray & MRI** (internal defect check),
- **Skin reflectance** (surface quality check).

The patient does not speak, yet the doctor receives a complete diagnostic report. Now replace the patient with a **manufactured component**, the instruments with **inspection sensors**, and the doctor with a **PLC / Industrial PC**. That is precisely what an automated sensor-based inspection station does.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Universal Truths of Inspection Sensors:**
> 1. **Contact vs Non-Contact** — Touching the workpiece (probes) vs. measuring from a distance (light/sound).
> 2. **Active vs Passive** — Emitting energy (laser) vs. merely receiving ambient signals (camera).
> 3. **Quantitative vs Qualitative** — Returning a numerical value (μm) vs. returning a binary answer (PASS/FAIL).

## 1.3 Classification of Automated Inspection Sensors

| Class | Sub-Type | Typical Use | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional** | LVDT, Capacitive, Laser Triangulation | Length, thickness, gap | Contact / Non-contact |
| **Surface** | Vision cameras, Confocal, Interferometric | Roughness, defects, texture | Non-contact |
| **Proximity / Position** | Inductive, Capacitive, Magnetic, Optical | Presence, counting, indexing | Both |
| **Geometric / Form** | CMM probes, Laser scanners | GD&T, flatness, cylindricity | Contact / Non-contact |
| **Force / Pressure** | Load cells, Piezoelectric, Strain gauges | Assembly verification | Contact |
| **Vision-based** | 2D cameras, 3D vision, Hyperspectral | Identification, defect recognition | Non-contact |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold-Coded for Memory)

- **Resolution** — The smallest detectable change in the measured quantity (e.g., **1 µm**).
- **Accuracy** — Closeness of the reading to the true value (e.g., **±5 µm**).
- **Repeatability (Precision)** — Ability to produce the same reading under identical conditions (e.g., **±2 µm**).
- **Linearity** — Constant sensitivity across the measurement range.
- **Hysteresis** — Difference in output for increasing vs. decreasing input.
- **Bandwidth / Response Time** — Speed of sensor reaction (e.g., **10 kHz**).
- **Measurement Range** — Min-to-max input the sensor can handle (e.g., **0–50 mm**).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"**R**esolution is **R**aw, **A**ccuracy is **A**bsolute, **R**epeatability is **R**eliable."* — Always remember in KTU exams, **Repeatability is always ≤ Accuracy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Automated Inspection System

Every sensor system, regardless of type, obeys the same **five-stage functional chain**:

1. **Energy/Signal Source** — Illumination, laser, ultrasonic pulse, or physical contact.
2. **Sensing Element (Transducer)** — Converts the physical variable into an electrical one.
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, filtering, linearization, A/D conversion.
4. **Processing / Decision Unit** — Microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC running inspection logic.
5. **Output / Actuation** — GO/NO-GO signal, sorting actuator, data log, or feedback to CNC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theory Focus:** The biggest marks go to understanding *why* a particular sensor is selected. The decision matrix is governed by the **Inspection Requirement → Sensor Suitability → Cost-Benefit** chain.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Sensor Categories

### 2.2.1 Optical / Vision Sensors
- **Principle:** A 2D/3D camera captures the image of the workpiece under controlled lighting; image-processing algorithms extract features (edges, holes, color, OCR characters).
- **Components:** *Lens + Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) + Lighting (LED, laser, structured light) + Frame grabber + Processor*.
- **Strengths:** Non-contact, full-field inspection, very high speed, ideal for 100% inline inspection.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to ambient light, dust, vibration; requires robust image processing.

### 2.2.2 Laser-Based Sensors (Triangulation & Time-of-Flight)
- **Laser Triangulation:** A laser spot is projected on the surface; its reflected position on a position-sensitive detector (PSD) is proportional to the height of the surface.
- **Laser Time-of-Flight (LIDAR):** Distance is computed from the time taken by the laser pulse to return.
- **Resolution:** Sub-micrometre to a few millimetres; **Range:** 1 mm to several metres.

### 2.2.3 Proximity Sensors
- **Inductive:** Detects only metallic targets via eddy-current losses; range typically 0.8–60 mm.
- **Capacitive:** Detects any material (metal, plastic, liquid) by change in capacitance.
- **Magnetic (Hall-effect / Reed):** Used for position feedback on pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders.
- **Ultrasonic:** Detects via echo of high-frequency sound (40–200 kHz); works on transparent/reflective surfaces where optical fails.

### 2.2.4 Contact-Based Dimensional Sensors
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):** A contactless inductive transducer with essentially infinite resolution, very high accuracy.
- **Strain Gauge Load Cells:** Wheatstone-bridge-based force/weight measurement.
- **CMM Touch Probes:** A ruby-tipped stylus touches the workpiece; 3D coordinates are recorded with micrometric accuracy.

### 2.2.5 Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)
The **CMM is the gold standard** of dimensional metrology. It consists of three orthogonal axes (X, Y, Z) carrying a probe that touches specific points on the workpiece. Modern CMMs use **multi-axis scanning probes** and can measure GD&T parameters like flatness, cylindricity, and position.

### 2.2.6 Machine Vision vs. Human Vision
| Parameter | Human Inspector | Machine Vision |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Slow (seconds) | Fast (ms) |
| Fatigue | High | None |
| Consistency | Variable | Excellent |
| Cost (long run) | High | Low |
| Adaptability | Excellent | Requires training |
| Data logging | Manual | Automatic |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Below, the absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` and `\mid` (not the literal `|` pipe) to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical Magnification | $M = \dfrac{h_{i}}{h_{o}} = \dfrac{v}{u}$ | dimensionless | $h_i$ image height, $h_o$ object height |
| 2 | Thin Lens Equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u}$ | $m^{-1}$ | Sign convention per KTU 1st-year physics |
| 3 | LVDT Output (Theoretical) | $V_{out} = K \cdot \Delta x$ | Volts | $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm) |
| 4 | Capacitive Sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Farads | Change in $d$ → displacement |
| 5 | Wheatstone Bridge Balance | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Ω | Strain gauge condition |
| 6 | Laser Triangulation | $z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x}$ | mm | $f$ lens focal, $b$ baseline, $x$ PSD shift |
| 7 | Time-of-Flight Distance | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m | $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 8 | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$ | m | $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s in air |
| 9 | Camera Field of View | $FoV = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{\text{Magnification}}$ | mm | Determines inspection area |
| 10 | Pixel Resolution | $r_{px} = \dfrac{FoV_{x}}{N_{x}}$ | mm/pixel | $N_x$ = number of pixels horizontally |
| 11 | Depth of Field (DOF) | $DOF \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot N \cdot c}{M^{2}}$ | mm | $N$ f-number, $c$ circle of confusion |
| 12 | CMM Volumetric Accuracy | $A = E_{xyz} \pm \dfrac{L}{K}$ | µm | $L$ measured length, $K$ spec constant |
| 13 | Inductive Sensor Range | $S_{r} \propto \sqrt{\rho / \mu_{r}}$ | mm | $\rho$ = target resistivity |
| 14 | Hall-effect Voltage | $V_{H} = R_{H} \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot B}{t}$ | V | $R_H$ = Hall coefficient |
| 15 | Sensor Hysteresis Error | $\%\text{Hyst} = \dfrac{\vert y_{up} - y_{down} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | $y_{FS}$ = full-scale output |
| 16 | Repeatability (Standard) | $R = k \cdot \sigma$ | units | $k=2$ (95%), $k=3$ (99.7%) |
| 17 | Strain from Strain Gauge | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{GF}$ | dimensionless | $GF \approx 2.0$ to 2.2 for constantan |
| 18 | Force from Load Cell | $F = \dfrac{V_{out}}{S}$ | N | $S$ = sensitivity (V/N) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Sensors Save the Day

- **Automotive paint shops:** Vision systems inspect paint uniformity and detect runs, sags, or dust nibs.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging:** Vision systems verify batch codes, expiry dates, blister-pack integrity.
- **Electronics SMT lines:** Laser profilometers measure solder-paste height pre-reflow.
- **Aerospace blade inspection:** Multi-axis CMMs measure airfoil profiles within ±5 µm.
- **Food industry:** Vision + NIR sensors detect foreign bodies, ripeness, and count items.
- **CNC machining:** Tool-length probes (Renishaw) + tool-setters enable closed-loop zero-defect machining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pixel Resolution of a Vision System

> **Problem Setup:** A machine-vision camera is fitted with a lens of focal length **f = 25 mm** to inspect a workpiece of length **L = 80 mm** along its horizontal axis. The image sensor has **N_x = 2048 pixels** horizontally. The working distance is **d = 250 mm** (object distance, $u \approx 250$ mm). Determine the **pixel resolution (mm/pixel)** on the object.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the magnification M using the thin lens equation.**

Apply the thin-lens formula:
$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u}$$

Here $u = 250$ mm and $f = 25$ mm, therefore:
$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{25} - \dfrac{1}{250}$$

Compute the numerical value:
$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{10 - 1}{250} = \dfrac{9}{250}$$

Invert to obtain $v$:
$$v = \dfrac{250}{9} \approx 27.78 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnification M.**

$$M = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{27.78}{250} \approx 0.1111$$

This means each 1 mm on the object maps to 0.1111 mm on the image sensor (an image of ~8.89 mm length for the 80 mm part).

**Step 3 — Compute the field of view on the object side.**

If the horizontal sensor active width is $S_x$ (assume a typical 2/3" sensor, $S_x \approx 8.8$ mm), then:
$$FoV_{x} = \dfrac{S_x}{M} = \dfrac{8.8}{0.1111} \approx 79.2 \text{ mm}$$

This confirms the lens choice is appropriate for the 80 mm workpiece (just barely covers it).

**Step 4 — Compute pixel resolution (mm per pixel).**

$$r_{px} = \dfrac{FoV_{x}}{N_{x}} = \dfrac{79.2}{2048} \approx 0.0387 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Each pixel represents approximately **38.7 µm on the part surface**. Hence, this vision system can resolve features down to ~80 µm (2 pixels) — perfectly adequate for inspecting features above 100 µm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Laser Triangulation Displacement Sensor Calibration

> **Problem Setup:** A laser triangulation sensor has a baseline $b = 50$ mm between the laser emitter and the position-sensitive detector (PSD). A converging lens of focal length $f = 30$ mm focuses the reflected laser spot onto the PSD. When a target is moved by $\Delta z = 5$ mm towards the sensor, the spot shifts on the PSD by $x = 2.5$ mm. Verify the theoretical model $z = (f \cdot b)/x$ and compute the **sensitivity $dz/dx$**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the triangulation formula:**

The classical laser-triangulation geometric relation is:
$$z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x}$$

where $z$ = distance from the sensor to the target, $f$ = lens focal length, $b$ = baseline, and $x$ = spot position on PSD.

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$z = \dfrac{30 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}}{2.5 \text{ mm}} = \dfrac{1500}{2.5} = 600 \text{ mm}$$

So the target was originally at **z = 600 mm** from the sensor.

**Step 3 — Verify the displacement:**

If the target moves to $z' = 600 - 5 = 595$ mm, the new spot shift becomes:
$$x' = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{z'} = \dfrac{30 \times 50}{595} \approx 2.521 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity (slope):**

The differential sensitivity is:
$$\dfrac{dz}{dx} = -\dfrac{f \cdot b}{x^{2}}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\dfrac{dz}{dx} = -\dfrac{30 \times 50}{(2.5)^{2}} = -\dfrac{1500}{6.25} = -240 \text{ mm/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A negative sign confirms that **as the target approaches, the spot shifts the opposite way on the PSD**. The magnitude of 240 mm/mm means a 1 mm PSD shift corresponds to a 240 mm distance change — this is the *amplifying property* of triangulation geometry that makes it ideal for high-resolution micro-displacement measurement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain-Gauge Load Cell Force Computation

> **Problem Setup:** A cantilever-type load cell uses a full Wheatstone bridge with four active strain gauges. The bridge excitation voltage is $V_{ex} = 10$ V. The gauge factor $GF = 2.1$, strain $\varepsilon = 800 \; \mu\varepsilon$, and the output voltage is measured as $V_{out} = 8.4$ mV. Verify the bridge equation and compute the **force** if the cell constant is $K_c = 500$ N per microstrain.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Theoretical output voltage of a full bridge:**

For a full active Wheatstone bridge, the differential output voltage is:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$V_{out} = 10 \text{ V} \times 2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}$$

Compute step by step:
$$V_{out} = 10 \times 2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} = 16{,}800 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0168 \text{ V} = 16.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with measured value:**

Measured $V_{out} = 8.4$ mV. The theoretical is **2× the measured** because two gauges are in tension and two in compression in a typical full-bridge bending configuration, but the sign convention may yield a net of $GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$:

$$V_{out, \text{net}} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{GF \cdot \varepsilon}{2} = 10 \times \dfrac{2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 8.4 \text{ mV} \;\; \checkmark$$

The measurement matches.

**Step 4 — Compute the force:**

$$F = K_c \cdot \varepsilon = 500 \text{ N}/\mu\varepsilon \times 800 \; \mu\varepsilon = 400{,}000 \text{ N} = 400 \text{ kN}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world use:** Such a 400 kN load cell is typical in hydraulic press monitoring, automotive crash testing, and heavy forging inspection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Sensor

| Application | Best Sensor | Why? | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detecting metal gear teeth passing | **Inductive proximity** | Cheap, reliable, robust to oil | Range only ~5 mm |
| Detecting transparent bottle on conveyor | **Ultrasonic / Capacitive** | Light passes through; sound/electric field doesn't | Slower than optical |
| Measuring car-body panel flatness | **Laser line scanner / 3D vision** | Full-field, no contact | Expensive, sensitive to vibration |
| Inspecting PCB solder height | **Confocal chromatic sensor** | Sub-µm vertical resolution | Slow point measurement |
| Verifying pharmaceutical label text | **2D Vision + OCR** | Fast, contactless | Needs controlled lighting |
| Measuring engine block bore diameter | **CMM with scanning probe** | Highest accuracy, full GD&T | Slow, requires climate control |
| Checking engine torque at assembly | **Strain-gauge torque cell** | Direct, real-time | Needs slip-ring or telemetry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Architecture of an Automated Inspection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece on Conveyor] --> B[Position Trigger Sensor]
    B --> C{Inspection Station}
    C --> D[Lighting Source]
    C --> E[Vision / Laser / Probe]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    F --> G[ADC and Noise Filter]
    G --> H[Industrial PC / PLC]
    H --> I{Decision Logic}
    I -- PASS --> J[Accept Diverter]
    I -- FAIL --> K[Reject Diverter]
    H --> L[SCADA / MES Data Log]
    L --> M[Cloud / Digital Twin]
    M --> N[Predictive Analytics Engine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow proceeds from physical part entry → trigger → multi-modal sensing → signal chain → decision → physical sorting → data logging → cloud analytics. This is the canonical **Industry 4.0 inspection loop**.

## 4.2 Machine Vision System Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Illumination
        L1[LED Ring Light]
        L2[Backlight Panel]
        L3[Structured Laser Line]
    end

    subgraph Optics
        OP1[Lens with Variable Aperture]
        OP2[Polarizing Filter]
    end

    subgraph Sensor
        S1[CMOS / CCD Image Sensor]
        S2[Frame Grabber Card]
    end

    subgraph Processing
        P1[Image Pre-processing]
        P2[Edge Detection and Blob Analysis]
        P3[Pattern Matching and OCR]
        P4[Decision Algorithm]
    end

    L1 --> OP1
    L2 --> OP1
    L3 --> OP1
    OP1 --> S1
    OP2 --> OP1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> Q1[GO Signal]
    P4 --> Q2[NO-GO Signal]
    P4 --> Q3[Statistical Process Control Log]
```

## 4.3 CMM Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Ruby Stylus Probe] --> X1[X Axis Linear Encoder]
    R --> Y1[Y Axis Linear Encoder]
    R --> Z1[Z Axis Linear Encoder]
    X1 --> CTRL[Motion Controller and Servo Drive]
    Y1 --> CTRL
    Z1 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DMU[Granite Bridge Structure]
    CTRL --> DPU[Data Processing Unit]
    DPU --> CAD[CAD Model Comparator]
    CAD --> REP[GD and T Report Generator]
    REP --> PRN[Hard Copy Report]
    REP --> NET[Network Upload to MES]
```

## 4.4 Sensor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Inspect a Part] --> B{Contact Allowed?}
    B -- No --> C[Non-Contact Sensors]
    B -- Yes --> D[Contact Sensors]

    C --> E{Material Type?}
    E -- Metal --> F[Inductive / Eddy-Current]
    E -- Non-Metal --> G[Capacitive]
    E -- Any --> H[Ultrasonic / Vision / Laser]

    H --> I{Measurement Type?}
    I -- 1D Distance --> J[Laser Triangulation]
    I -- 2D Profile --> K[Laser Line Scanner]
    I -- 3D Full Shape --> L[3D Vision / CMM Scanner]
    I -- Surface Defect --> M[2D Vision with Lighting]

    D --> N{Accuracy Required?}
    N -- Ultra High --> O[CMM with Touch Probe]
    N -- Moderate --> P[LVDT / Strain Gauge]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"How will you select a sensor for an inspection task?"*, use this decision tree as a written answer. Examiners love structured flow-logic in 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define an automated inspection sensor system. List any **four** key performance parameters used to evaluate such a system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An automated inspection sensor system is an integrated combination of **transducers, signal conditioning circuits, processing electronics, and decision algorithms** that automatically measure physical, dimensional, or functional attributes of a manufactured part without continuous human intervention.

Four key performance parameters:
1. **Resolution** — smallest detectable change.
2. **Accuracy** — closeness to true value.
3. **Repeatability** — consistency under identical conditions.
4. **Response time / Bandwidth** — speed of measurement.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for four parameters = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between **contact** and **non-contact** inspection methods. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact | Non-Contact |
|---|---|---|
| Physical touch | Yes | No |
| Risk of damage to soft parts | Possible | None |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Examples | **CMM touch probe, LVDT, dial gauge** | **Vision system, laser scanner, ultrasonic sensor** |

*(1.5 marks for comparison + 0.75 each for examples = 3 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** *"Answer any one of the following. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7."*

---

### 🔹 **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
*Explain the working principle of a **machine vision system** used for automated inspection. With a neat block diagram, describe its major components and their functions.*

#### **Model Answer (Part a):**

A **machine vision system** mimics human visual inspection using cameras, optics, and software.

**Working Principle:**
1. The **workpiece** is positioned in the field of view, often with a position-trigger sensor.
2. **Lighting** (LED ring, backlight, or structured light) illuminates the part uniformly.
3. A **lens** focuses the reflected/transmitted light onto a **2D image sensor** (CCD or CMOS).
4. The **frame grabber / camera interface** digitizes the image into pixel arrays.
5. **Image-processing software** performs:
   - Pre-processing (noise reduction, thresholding, edge detection),
   - Feature extraction (blobs, holes, edges, OCR characters),
   - Pattern matching against a reference template.
6. A **decision algorithm** outputs **PASS / FAIL** signals and logs data to SCADA/MES.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2):**
*Lighting → Optics → Image Sensor → Frame Grabber → Processor → Decision / Output.*

*(2 marks principle + 3 marks components + 2 marks diagram = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
*A machine-vision camera has a sensor of horizontal width **8.8 mm** and **1280 pixels** in a row. It views a workpiece of width **60 mm**. A **10 mm** feature is to be detected. Determine the **magnification**, the **pixel resolution on the object**, and whether the feature is detectable. Assume the feature requires at least **2 pixels**.*

#### **Model Answer (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Magnification:**
$$M = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Width}}{\text{Object Width}} = \dfrac{8.8}{60} \approx 0.1467$$

**Step 2 — Pixel resolution on object:**
$$r_{px} = \dfrac{60 \text{ mm}}{1280} = 0.0469 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 3 — Pixels covered by the 10 mm feature:**
$$N_{feat} = \dfrac{10}{0.0469} \approx 213 \text{ pixels}$$

Since 213 ≫ 2 pixels, **the feature is easily detectable**.

**Conclusion:** The system has a pixel resolution of **46.9 µm/pixel**, and a 10 mm feature spans about 213 pixels — well above the 2-pixel minimum. ✔️

*(2 + 3 + 2 = 7 marks with full credit for stepwise working)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1 (Part b):** Students often confuse the magnification formula — remember it is **M = (Image size) / (Object size)**, not the reverse. Also, do not forget to **convert units consistently** (mm throughout, NOT mixing mm and cm).

---

### 🔹 **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
*Describe the working of a **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)**. Explain the role of the **probe**, the **bridge structure**, and **scanning capability** in modern CMMs.*

#### **Model Answer (Part a):**

A **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** is a high-precision 3D metrology device used to measure the **geometric features** of a workpiece to micrometric accuracy.

**Working Principle:**
1. The workpiece is placed on the **granite table** (temperature-stable and vibration-damped).
2. A **ruby-tipped stylus probe** is mounted on a **movable bridge** that travels along X, Y, and Z axes.
3. Each axis carries a **linear optical/laser encoder** providing real-time position feedback.
4. The probe either **touches** specific points (touch-trigger mode) or continuously **scans** the surface (scanning mode).
5. The **controller** records (x, y, z) coordinates and compares them with the nominal CAD model to compute deviations.

**Role of the probe:**
- The **ruby tip** is hard, low-friction, and ball-perfect; it touches the surface and triggers a contact event.
- **Scanning probes** use analogue force-balance systems to maintain continuous contact and gather thousands of points per second.

**Role of the bridge structure:**
- The **bridge** moves in X and Y; the **quill** moves in Z.
- A **ceramic or granite bridge** ensures dimensional stability and minimum thermal drift.
- A **counterbalanced air-bearing system** allows frictionless motion.

**Role of scanning:**
- Scanning reduces measurement time by 5–10×.
- Enables measurement of complex free-form surfaces (turbine blades, car body panels).
- Generates dense point clouds that can be reverse-engineered into CAD.

*(2 marks principle + 2 marks probe + 2 marks bridge + 1 mark scanning = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
*A **laser triangulation sensor** has a baseline $b = 40$ mm and lens focal length $f = 25$ mm. The spot on the PSD moves by $\Delta x = 1.6$ mm when the target is displaced by $\Delta z$. Compute the **original distance $z$** and the **displacement $\Delta z$** using the small-displacement approximation $z = f \cdot b / x$.*

#### **Model Answer (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Original distance:**
$$z_1 = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x_1} = \dfrac{25 \times 40}{1.6} = \dfrac{1000}{1.6} = 625 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Assume a small displacement of 0.1 mm on PSD ($\Delta x = 0.1$ mm);**

New PSD position $x_2 = 1.6 + 0.1 = 1.7$ mm.

**Step 3 — New distance:**
$$z_2 = \dfrac{25 \times 40}{1.7} = \dfrac{1000}{1.7} \approx 588.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Displacement of target:**
$$\Delta z = z_1 - z_2 = 625 - 588.24 = 36.76 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity (optional):**
$$\dfrac{dz}{dx} = -\dfrac{f \cdot b}{x^{2}} = -\dfrac{1000}{(1.6)^2} = -390.6 \text{ mm/mm}$$

So a 1 mm PSD shift → 390 mm target shift, confirming the triangulation's high displacement amplification near the focal plane.

*(2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks with full stepwise work)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2 (Part b):** A very common mistake is to forget that the spot **direction reverses** when the target moves. Always re-derive the **sign convention** before calculating. KTU examiners specifically test the student's awareness that **displacement of the spot is inversely related to target distance**.

---

## 🔥 High-Yield Revision Checklist (Topic Recap)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Things You MUST Remember Before the Exam:**

- ✅ Automated inspection sensors replace **manual quality checks** for speed, consistency, and data logging.
- ✅ **Contact sensors** = LVDT, CMM probe, dial gauge, load cell. **Non-contact** = vision, laser, ultrasonic, capacitive.
- ✅ **LVDT** is theoretically frictionless with **infinite resolution** — a KTU favourite.
- ✅ **Inductive proximity** = only detects **metals**; **Capacitive** = any material.
- ✅ **Vision system components** = Lighting + Lens + Sensor (CCD/CMOS) + Frame Grabber + Software.
- ✅ **Pixel resolution** formula: $r_{px} = FoV_x / N_x$ — this decides *what is visible*.
- ✅ **Laser triangulation** = spot position on PSD ∝ target distance; **sensitivity increases near the focal plane**.
- ✅ **Time-of-flight** = $d = c \cdot t / 2$ (laser) or $d = v_{sound} \cdot t / 2$ (ultrasonic).
- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge** balance condition: $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$.
- ✅ **Strain gauge** equation: $\varepsilon = (\Delta R / R) / GF$, with $GF \approx 2$ for constantan.
- ✅ **Hall-effect** sensors give non-contact position/magnetic field measurement.
- ✅ **CMM** is the **gold standard** for GD&T; modern scanning CMMs are 5–10× faster.
- ✅ **Repeatability ≤ Accuracy ≤ Range** — keep this hierarchy in mind for definitions.
- ✅ For **transparent objects**, prefer **ultrasonic or capacitive**, not optical.
- ✅ In the exam, always **state the units, the formula, substitute, and box the final answer** — KTU examiners reward stepwise work, not just final values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Gauging and Size Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Gauging and Size Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automatic Gauging** refers to the use of self-actuating measurement transducers, signal conditioning electronics, and computational systems to measure the geometric dimensions (diameter, length, taper, flatness, roundness) of a workpiece *without* manual operator intervention, and to convert these measurements into electrical / pneumatic / optical signals suitable for indication, recording, or closed-loop process control.

A **Size Control System** is a closed-loop feedback architecture in which the output of an automatic gauge is compared against a preset tolerance window, and the resulting error signal is used to drive an actuator (typically a tool-slide servo or a tool-offset compensator) so that the *next* part produced is automatically corrected toward the nominal dimension.

Mathematically, the closed-loop objective is to drive the dimensional error

$$e(t) = x_{measured}(t) - x_{nominal}$$

toward **zero** in steady-state, while maintaining the part within the prescribed **upper specification limit (USL)** and **lower specification limit (LSL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Automatic gauging is the *measurement* subsystem, while size control is the *regulation* subsystem. Together they form the **Measure–Decide–Actuate (MDA)** triad of computer-integrated manufacturing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robotic Biryani Chef"

Imagine a robotic chef making biryani. The chef must ensure every serving weighs **exactly 250 g** (±2 g).

1. **Automatic Gauge** = A digital weighing scale that instantly reads the mass of rice falling on the plate.
2. **Size Control System** = The chef's brain, which compares the live reading with 250 g. If it reads 245 g, the chef's hand opens the ladle valve a little *more* on the next scoop; if 253 g, a little *less*.
3. **Tolerance** = The allowed ±2 g window.

In machining, the **scale** is replaced by a pneumatic, LVDT, or optical gauge, the **brain** is a PLC / micro-controller, and the **hand** is a servo-driven tool slide.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Class | Principle | Typical Resolution | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pneumatic Gauge** | Back-pressure / flow vs gap | **0.5 µm – 2 µm** | Bearing rings, piston pins |
| **Electrical (LVDT) Gauge** | Inductance change | **0.1 µm – 1 µm** | Shafts, bores |
| **Capacitive Gauge** | Capacitance vs gap | **0.01 µm** | Thin films, fine bores |
| **Optical / Laser Gauge** | Light intensity / triangulation | **1 µm – 10 µm** | Non-contact diameters |
| **Eddy-Current Gauge** | Impedance change | **0.1 µm** | Conductive surfaces |

> [!NOTE]
> The **2 µm** and **0.5 µm** numbers above are standard precision-grade resolutions — these are the values students must memorize for numerical problems and MCQs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an Automatic Gauging Station

A complete gauging cell contains the following logical blocks:

1. **Workpiece Locator / Fixture** — kinematic or repeatable positioning so that the gauge reads *the same feature* every cycle.
2. **Transducer Head** — converts the dimensional quantity (linear displacement) into a low-level signal (pressure, voltage, capacitance).
3. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, linearises, and filters the raw transducer output.
4. **A/D Converter** — digitises the signal (typically **12-bit** or **16-bit** resolution in modern cells).
5. **Comparator / Decision Logic** — compares the digitised value with USL and LSL.
6. **Display / Data Logger** — operator HMI and SPC database.
7. **Output Interface** — drives the actuator or sorts parts (Go / No-Go).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the *order* of the signal chain. Memorise: **Transducer → Amplifier → Filter → A/D → Comparator**.

## 2.2 Pneumatic Gauging — The Dominant Method

Pneumatic gauging uses the relationship between the **back-pressure** developed across a small orifice–jet gap and the physical size of the workpiece.

### Working Principle

Compressed air at supply pressure $P_s$ (typically **2 bar** gauge) flows through a fixed orifice and escapes through a small gap $h$ between the jet nozzle and the workpiece surface. As $h$ decreases, the resistance to flow increases, raising the back-pressure $P_b$ inside the jet.

For a circular jet of diameter $d_j$ and a flat workpiece, the mass flow rate $Q$ through the annular gap is

$$Q = C_d \, A_{gap} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (≈ 0.6 – 0.95)
- $A_{gap} = \pi d_j h$ (annular area for a circular jet)
- $\Delta P = P_s - P_b$
- $\rho$ = density of air (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP)

The back-pressure sensitivity to gap is **highly non-linear** (roughly inverse-square), but in the operating region of **10 µm – 100 µm**, the characteristic can be linearised to excellent accuracy.

### Why Pneumatic?

- **No physical contact** → zero wear on gauge or workpiece.
- Self-cleaning jet → suitable for oily, dirty shop-floor environments.
- High magnification (**10 000 : 1** to **50 000 : 1** easily achieved).
- Inherently **averaging** measurement → insensitive to micro-roughness.

## 2.3 Electrical (LVDT) Gauging

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core attached to the gauging probe.

The differential output voltage is

$$V_{out} = V_s \left( \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1 + N_2} \right) \cdot f(x)$$

For a symmetrically wound transformer, this reduces to the textbook form

$$V_{out} = k \cdot (x_a - x_b)$$

where $k$ is the sensitivity constant in **V/mm**, and $x_a$, $x_b$ are the displacements of the core into the two secondaries.

Key advantages:
- Infinite resolution (limited only by the readout electronics).
- Excellent linearity over ±0.5 mm to ±25 mm stroke.
- Robust to temperature and humidity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ | Orifice flow equation | m³/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P \propto 1/h^2$ | Pneumatic back-pressure law | Pa |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta D}$ | Magnification ratio (gauge) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $e(t) = x_m(t) - x_{nom}$ | Control error signal | µm |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | V or counts |
| 6 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability index | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Centred capability | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Sampling frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $V_{out} = k(x_a - x_b)$ | LVDT linear output | V |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance for capacitive probe | Farads |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $C_{pk}$ or any "absolute value" expression in a KTU answer book, write $\vert x \vert$ using a clear bracket — but in your *typed* notes always use `\vert` inside markdown tables so the pipe does not break the table layout.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing Manufacturing** — every inner race is 100 % gauged with pneumatic probes; out-of-spec parts are auto-sorted at rates of 3–6 parts/second.
- **Engine Block Machining** — adaptive size control compensates for **thermal drift** of the casting and **tool wear**, maintaining bore diameter within ±5 µm over a 1000-part run.
- **Rolling Mills** — laser / capacitive thickness gauges feed back to the roll-gap hydraulic servo to maintain strip thickness within ±10 µm at speeds of 20 m/s.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — continuous gauge data populates $\bar{x}$–R control charts in real time, enabling predictive rather than reactive quality control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pneumatic Back-Pressure Characteristic

We model the air gap between the nozzle and the workpiece as a thin annular orifice. By continuity, the mass flow into the gap equals the mass flow out.

**Step 1 — Mass flow through the annular gap.**
The annular area is

$$A = \pi d_j h$$

Applying Bernoulli's equation for compressible flow across the gap, the velocity is

$$v = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho} }$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate.**
Substituting $v$ and $A$:

$$Q = C_d \, A \, v = C_d \, (\pi d_j h) \, \sqrt{ \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho} }$$

**Step 3 — Continuity through the fixed inlet orifice.**
The same mass flow must pass through the fixed inlet orifice of area $A_0$:

$$Q = C_{d0} \, A_0 \, \sqrt{ \frac{2 (P_s - P_b)}{\rho} }$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $P_b$.**
For a steady reading, the back-pressure self-adjusts so that both flows are equal:

$$C_d \pi d_j h \sqrt{\frac{2(P_s - P_b)}{\rho}} = C_{d0} A_0 \sqrt{\frac{2(P_s - P_b)}{\rho}}$$

Canceling the common square-root term and rearranging yields

$$P_b = P_s \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{C_{d0} A_0}{C_d \pi d_j h} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity to gap.**
Differentiating $P_b$ with respect to $h$:

$$\frac{dP_b}{dh} = 2 P_s \left( \frac{C_{d0} A_0}{C_d \pi d_j} \right)^2 \frac{1}{h^3}$$

This is the celebrated **inverse-cube** sensitivity law — it explains why pneumatic gauges are so *sensitive to micrometre-level* changes yet become *sluggish* at gaps above 200 µm. The operating point is therefore chosen in the **10 – 100 µm** region.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Gauge Selection Decision Table

| Application | Required Tolerance | Best Gauge Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bearing inner race ID** | **±2 µm** | Pneumatic (column-type) | Non-contact, self-cleaning, high magnification |
| **Crankshaft journal diameter** | **±5 µm** | LVDT in-process | Robust, can be mounted on tool slide |
| **Aluminium sheet thickness (rolling mill)** | **±10 µm** | X-ray / Laser triangulation | Non-contact, high speed |
| **Capacitor dielectric thickness** | **±0.5 µm** | Capacitive | Sub-micron resolution |
| **Brake disc runout** | **±20 µm** | Eddy-current | Conductive, immune to oil |
| **Engine block bore** | **±7 µm** | Pneumatic + adaptive control | Combines gauging with tool offset |

## 3.3 Closed-Loop Size Control — Block Diagram Reduction

Consider a turning operation where the actual diameter $D$ is being controlled. The simplified plant model is a first-order system:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

The gauge, amplifier, and actuator each contribute gain; their combined transfer function is

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{(Ts + 1)^n}$$

Using a **PI controller** $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$, the closed-loop transfer function is

$$\frac{D(s)}{D_{ref}(s)} = \frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{(K_p s + K_i)\, K_p / [s(\tau_p s + 1)]}{1 + (K_p s + K_i)\, K_p / [s(\tau_p s + 1)]}$$

After algebraic simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(\tau_p s + 1)$):

$$\frac{D(s)}{D_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_p (K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_p s + 1) + K_p (K_p s + K_i)}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$s(\tau_p s + 1) + K_p (K_p s + K_i) = \tau_p s^2 + s + K_p K_p s + K_p K_i$$

$$\Rightarrow \tau_p s^2 + (1 + K_p K_p) s + K_p K_i$$

So the final closed-loop form is

$$\frac{D(s)}{D_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_p K_p s + K_p K_i}{\tau_p s^2 + (1 + K_p K_p) s + K_p K_i}$$

This is a standard second-order system, allowing the use of the familiar relations

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p K_i}{\tau_p}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{1 + K_p K_p}{2 \sqrt{\tau_p K_p K_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"What is the steady-state error for a step input with a PI controller?"* The answer is **zero** because of the integral term. With a purely P-controller it would be $1 / (1 + K_{loop})$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A pneumatic gauge has a jet diameter of **1.5 mm**, the workpiece is at a gap of **25 µm**, and the back-pressure sensitivity in the operating region is $\dfrac{dP_b}{dh} = -8 \times 10^{9}$ Pa/m. The supply pressure is **2 bar**. Find the percentage change in output signal when the workpiece diameter changes by **4 µm**.

**Solution:**

The change in gap, assuming the gauge reads from one side only, is

$$\Delta h = \Delta D = 4 \; \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-6} \; m$$

The corresponding change in back-pressure is

$$\Delta P_b = \frac{dP_b}{dh} \cdot \Delta h = (-8 \times 10^{9}) \times (4 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\Delta P_b = -3.2 \times 10^{4} \; Pa = -0.32 \; bar$$

The percentage change with respect to the supply pressure is

$$\% \Delta = \frac{\vert \Delta P_b \vert}{P_s} \times 100 = \frac{0.32}{2.0} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \Delta = 16 \; \%}$$

This 16 % electrical signal swing for a 4 µm dimensional change corresponds to a **magnification** of approximately 75 000 : 1, illustrating the extreme sensitivity of pneumatic gauging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automatic Gauging & Size Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REF Setpoint Nominal Dimension] --> B[Comparator Subtractor]
    C[Workpiece on Fixture] --> D[Pneumatic or LVDT Gauge Head]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Filter]
    E --> F[ADC 12 bit or 16 bit]
    F --> B
    B --> G[PID Controller in PLC]
    G --> H[DAC and Power Amplifier]
    H --> I[Servo Driven Tool Slide]
    I --> J[Turning or Grinding Process]
    J --> C
    E --> K[HMI Display and SPC Logger]
    F --> K
    K --> L[Go No Go Sorting Actuator]
```

## 4.2 Functional State Machine for Decision Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Measure : Part In Position
    Measure --> Compare : ADC Reading Ready
    Compare --> Accept : LSL less than x less than USL
    Compare --> Reject : x greater than USL
    Compare --> Reject : x less than LSL
    Compare --> Correct : Drift Detected Within Window
    Accept --> Idle : Sort to Go Bin
    Reject --> Idle : Sort to No Go Bin
    Correct --> Adjust : Send Offset to Tool Slide
    Adjust --> Idle : Wait Next Part
```

## 4.3 Comparison of In-Process vs Post-Process Gauging

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IP[In Process Gauging]
        IP1[Workpiece in Machine] --> IP2[Probe on Tool Slide]
        IP2 --> IP3[Live Dimension Read]
        IP3 --> IP4[Real Time Tool Offset]
    end
    subgraph PP[Post Process Gauging]
        PP1[Workpiece Leaves Machine] --> PP2[Stand Alone Gauge Station]
        PP2 --> PP3[Sort and Log]
        PP3 --> PP4[Statistical Feedback to Machine]
    end
    IP4 --> Result1[100 Percent In Line Control]
    PP4 --> Result2[Off Line Audit and SPC]
```

## 4.4 Adaptive Control vs Fixed-Offset Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Offset] --> B[Operator Sets Tool Position Once]
    B --> C[Drift Over Time]
    C --> D[Out of Spec Parts]
    D --> E[Manual Re Set]
    E --> A
    F[Adaptive Control] --> G[Gauge Reads Every Part]
    G --> H[Controller Computes New Offset]
    H --> I[Tool Slide Auto Repositions]
    I --> J[Drift Continuously Compensated]
    J --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **in-process** and **post-process** gauging. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **In-process gauging** measures the workpiece *while* it is still mounted in the machine tool. The gauge probe is integrated with the tool slide, so measurement and machining happen in the same cycle. Example: a pneumatic probe checking bore diameter *during* an engine block honing operation.
- **Post-process gauging** measures the workpiece *after* it has been removed from the machine, typically on a dedicated gauging station. Example: a bench-type LVDT gauge inspecting the diameter of a finished shaft in a quality lab.
- Key difference: in-process enables *real-time* feedback control, whereas post-process is used for *sorting* and *SPC logging* but cannot correct the part that was just produced.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State **two advantages** and **one limitation** of pneumatic gauging over LVDT gauging.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Advantage 1:** Non-contact operation — no probe wear, suitable for soft / finished surfaces.
- **Advantage 2:** Self-averaging measurement — insensitive to surface micro-roughness; ideal for honed or ground bores.
- **Limitation:** Requires a clean, dry, regulated compressed-air supply; the relationship is non-linear and the dynamic response is slower than LVDT, so it is unsuitable for high-speed (>10 Hz) dynamic measurements.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Question Choice A]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **automatic gauging and size control system** used in a CNC turning centre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic gauge has a jet diameter of **2.0 mm** and a back-pressure sensitivity of $\dfrac{dP_b}{dh} = -5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa/m in its working region. The supply pressure is **2.5 bar**. Calculate the percentage change in output signal when the workpiece diameter changes by **6 µm**. Comment on the magnification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The functional architecture consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Workpiece** mounted on the turning centre spindle, located by a 3-jaw chuck.
2. **Pneumatic gauge head** (or LVDT probe) mounted on a retractable arm.
3. **Signal conditioner** — amplifies the low-level transducer signal and linearises it.
4. **A/D converter** with 12-bit or 16-bit resolution.
5. **Comparator** in the CNC / PLC — compares reading with stored USL, LSL, and nominal.
6. **PID controller** calculates a corrective offset.
7. **Servo amplifier and tool-slide actuator** repositions the X-axis.
8. **Display / SPC database** for logging.
9. **Sorting mechanism** for the finished part (Go / No-Go).

The closed-loop action compensates for **tool wear**, **thermal expansion of the workpiece**, and **machine drift**, holding the diameter within ±5 µm even after 8 hours of continuous production.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given:
- $\dfrac{dP_b}{dh} = -5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa/m
- $\Delta D = 6 \; \mu m = 6 \times 10^{-6} \; m$
- $P_s = 2.5 \; bar = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \; Pa$

Change in back-pressure:

$$\Delta P_b = \frac{dP_b}{dh} \cdot \Delta D = (-5 \times 10^{9}) \times (6 \times 10^{-6}) = -3 \times 10^{4} \; Pa$$

Convert to bar:

$$\Delta P_b = -0.30 \; bar$$

Percentage change:

$$\% \Delta = \frac{0.30}{2.50} \times 100 = 12 \; \%$$

**Comment on magnification:**
A 6 µm dimensional change produces a 12 % electrical signal swing. The magnification ratio is

$$M = \frac{\% \Delta_{signal}}{\Delta D} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \; \% \; per \; \mu m$$

Equivalently, the full-scale (2.5 bar) signal swing corresponds to about **125 µm** of dimensional change, giving a **magnification of 50 000 : 1** when the readout column is graduated over 2.5 bar. This is characteristic of precision pneumatic gauging.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Question Choice B]**

### Question B — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **pneumatic back-pressure gauge** with a labelled schematic. Derive the relationship between back-pressure and the gap between the nozzle and the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how **adaptive control** differs from **fixed-offset control** in a size-control loop. Why is adaptive control preferred in high-precision batch manufacturing? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle:** Compressed air at supply pressure $P_s$ flows through a fixed inlet orifice into a chamber, then escapes through the small annular gap $h$ between the measuring jet (diameter $d_j$) and the workpiece. As $h$ decreases, flow resistance increases and the back-pressure $P_b$ inside the chamber rises. $P_b$ is read by a precision manometer or pressure transducer and is the gauge output.

**Schematic layout (textual):**
- Air supply → regulator → fixed inlet orifice → measurement chamber → jet (nozzle) → gap $h$ → workpiece.
- The chamber is connected to a pressure readout column or electronic transducer.

**Derivation:**
Mass flow through the fixed inlet orifice:

$$Q_1 = C_{d0} A_0 \sqrt{\frac{2(P_s - P_b)}{\rho}}$$

Mass flow through the annular gap:

$$Q_2 = C_d (\pi d_j h) \sqrt{\frac{2 P_b}{\rho}}$$

At steady state $Q_1 = Q_2$. Equating and squaring:

$$C_{d0}^2 A_0^2 (P_s - P_b) = C_d^2 \pi^2 d_j^2 h^2 \, P_b$$

Solving for $P_b$:

$$P_b = \frac{C_{d0}^2 A_0^2 P_s}{C_{d0}^2 A_0^2 + C_d^2 \pi^2 d_j^2 h^2}$$

This is the **inverse-square** relationship between $P_b$ and $h$ that gives pneumatic gauges their extreme sensitivity. For small variations around a working point, linearisation yields the sensitivity

$$\frac{dP_b}{dh} \approx -\frac{2 C_{d0}^2 A_0^2 P_s \, C_d^2 \pi^2 d_j^2}{[C_{d0}^2 A_0^2 + C_d^2 \pi^2 d_j^2 h^2]^2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Fixed-Offset Control | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Offset updating** | Done manually at scheduled intervals | Automatic, on every part |
| **Compensation** | Tool wear only (operator estimate) | Tool wear + thermal drift + material hardness variation |
| **Response to drift** | Slow, batch-level | Real-time, part-to-part |
| **Operator skill** | High (must judge when to offset) | Low (controller does it) |
| **SPC integration** | Off-line | Built-in, continuous |

**Why adaptive is preferred in high-precision batch manufacturing:**
- Modern tolerances (e.g., ±5 µm) are tighter than the inherent drift of a turning centre over an 8-hour shift.
- Real-time compensation eliminates the need to stop the machine for periodic re-calibration, raising **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**.
- Closed-loop feedback reduces **scrap rate** from ~3 % to <0.2 % in typical bearing manufacture.
- Continuous logging builds an SPC database that can predict tool-life and trigger replacement *before* failure.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing magnification with resolution.** Magnification $M = \Delta_{signal} / \Delta_{dimension}$ is dimensionless; resolution is the *smallest detectable* change. Many students write $M$ in µm — instant **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $dP_b/dh$. As $h$ *decreases*, $P_b$ *increases*. Omitting the sign loses one mark in derivations.
> 3. **Mixing units** — bar, Pa, psi. Always convert to SI Pa in derivations, then quote the answer in bar if the question allows.
> 4. **Writing "automatic gauging is better than manual"** — examiners want *engineering justification* (speed, accuracy, SPC integration), not vague statements.
> 5. **Skipping the steady-state condition** $Q_1 = Q_2$ in pneumatic derivations — this is the *core* of the model and carries 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Treating the LVDT output as a single-coil** — it is *differential*; $V_{out} = k(x_a - x_b)$. Forgetting the difference loses full credit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition trio:** Automatic gauging (measurement) ≠ Size control (regulation) ≠ SPC (analysis). KTU often asks to separate these.
- **Pneumatic law:** $P_b \propto 1/h^2$ in the operating region; sensitivity $dP_b/dh \propto 1/h^3$.
- **LVDT law:** $V_{out} = k(x_a - x_b)$, linear, infinite resolution, *requires* A/D conditioning.
- **Closed-loop objective:** drive $e(t) = x_m - x_{nom} \to 0$ within tolerance band USL–LSL.
- **PID form:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$; PI alone gives zero steady-state error.
- **Sampling rule:** $f_s \geq 10 \times$ bandwidth of the dominant process mode to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.
- **Capability indices:** $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the industry-accepted minimum; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ accounts for off-centring.
- **Pneumatic advantages to memorise:** non-contact, self-cleaning, high $M$ (10 000 – 50 000 : 1), averaging.
- **Adaptive vs fixed-offset:** adaptive corrects *every* part using live gauge feedback; fixed-offset relies on operator judgement at intervals.
- **Typical resolutions to memorise:** Pneumatic = **0.5 – 2 µm**, LVDT = **0.1 – 1 µm**, Capacitive = **0.01 µm**, Eddy-current = **0.1 µm**, Optical = **1 – 10 µm**.
- **Standard supply pressure** for pneumatic gauges: **2 bar (gauge)** unless otherwise specified.
- **Density of air** at NTP: **1.2 kg/m³**.
- **Three-block recall:** Transducer → Signal Conditioner → A/D → Comparator → Controller → Actuator → Process. (Reverse this in viva and the examiner will know you understood it.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thickness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thickness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Thickness Measurement** is the process of quantifying the perpendicular distance between two parallel surfaces of a material (solid, liquid, or coated substrate) using contact or non-contact sensing techniques, with the objective of providing closed-loop feedback for process control in continuous manufacturing systems such as rolling mills, paper machines, film extruders, and semiconductor fabrication lines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, thickness measurement is classified as a **primary process variable** under dimensional metrology within the broader umbrella of **Industrial Process Instrumentation**. It forms an essential feedback parameter in SCADA/DCS architectures for quality assurance (ISO 9001 compliance) and Six-Sigma driven process optimization.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):**  
> - **Gauge (gauging):** The continuous, in-process measurement of thickness.  
> - **Stand-off distance:** The fixed gap between the sensor head and the nominal surface reference.  
> - **Accuracy vs. Repeatability:** Accuracy is the closeness to the true value; Repeatability is the ability to yield the same reading under identical conditions (more critical in on-line gauging).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are rolling out **chapati dough** in a kitchen. As you press down with the rolling pin, you constantly peek to check whether the dough is *too thick* (under-cooked centre) or *too thin* (tears easily). A modern steel rolling mill faces the **exact same problem**, but at speeds of 20 m/s, with tolerances of **± 5 micrometres**, and on steel strips kilometres long. 

A human cannot "peek" — so we deploy **electronic "eyes" (sensors)** that sit above and/or below the strip, mathematically deduce the thickness, and instantly command the hydraulic roll gap to open or close. **Thickness gauging is, quite literally, the rolling mill's sense of touch.**

## 1.3 Classification of Thickness Measurement Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Two Broad Families:**  
> 1. **Contact Methods** — Sensor physically touches the material (LVDT, capacitive contact probe, mechanical micrometer).  
> 2. **Non-Contact Methods** — Sensor infers thickness via a physical field (laser, ultrasonic, X-ray, beta radiation, eddy current, capacitive proximity).

**Why this matters in KTU exams:** The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus specifically expects you to contrast *contact vs. non-contact* with respect to **measurement range, accuracy, environment, and effect on the process (destructive vs. non-destructive).**

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Resolution:** The smallest detectable change in thickness (e.g., **1 µm**).  
- **Linearity:** The maximum deviation of the output curve from a best-fit straight line, usually expressed as **% of Full Scale (FS)**.  
- **Hysteresis:** The difference in output when the same thickness is approached from a higher value vs. a lower value.  
- **Response Time (τ):** Time taken for the sensor to reach 63.2 % of its final output, typically **10 ms – 1 s** for industrial gauges.  
- **Calibration Block:** A reference standard (e.g., gauge blocks, optical flats) traceable to **NABL/NPL standards**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Thickness is a "Hard" Process Variable

Unlike temperature or pressure, thickness has a peculiar challenge: **it is a geometric dimension that demands reference to a datum**. The material itself is often moving, vibrating, and temperature-drifting. Therefore, every practical thickness gauge uses **either**:
- A **fixed reference frame** (e.g., a C-frame holding a sensor on each side of the strip), **or**
- A **single-sided reference** (e.g., ultrasonic pulse-echo from the back surface).

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Common Techniques

### A. LVDT-Based Contact Thickness Gauge
- Two LVDT probes are mounted on a **C-shaped yoke** on opposite sides of the moving strip.
- The algebraic **sum** of the displacements gives the thickness: $t = d_1 + d_2 - K$, where $K$ is the constant C-frame gap.
- **Used for:** Cold rolling of non-ferrous metals, plastics.

### B. Capacitive Proximity Thickness Gauge
- The moving sheet acts as the **dielectric** between two capacitor plates (one on each side).
- Capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ → as $t$ increases, $C$ decreases.
- **Used for:** Thin films, paper, foil.

### C. Laser Triangulation Thickness Gauge
- Two laser diodes project spots onto the top and bottom surfaces.
- Each spot is imaged onto a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** at a known triangulation angle $\theta$.
- Distance $d = \dfrac{f \cdot L}{x}$, where $x$ is the spot displacement on the PSD.
- **Sum of two triangulated distances = thickness.**
- **Used for:** Cold-rolled steel, aluminium strip, glass.

### D. X-Ray and Beta-Ray Transmission Gauges
- A radioactive source (e.g., **Kr-85** for β-rays, **Fe-55 / Am-241** for X-rays) emits radiation through the material.
- Detected intensity follows the **Beer–Lambert Law**:
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho t}$$
where $\mu$ is the mass attenuation coefficient (cm²/g), $\rho$ is density (g/cm³), and $t$ is thickness.
- Rearranging: $t = \dfrac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$
- **Used for:** Hot strip mills (radiation is unaffected by glow of red-hot steel).

### E. Ultrasonic Pulse-Echo Thickness Gauge
- A single transducer sends a pulse, which reflects from the far surface.
- Time-of-flight $t = \dfrac{2 d}{v_{long}}$, where $v_{long}$ is the longitudinal wave velocity in the material.
- **Used for:** Coatings, pipelines, single-sided access scenarios.

### F. Eddy Current Thickness Gauge
- A high-frequency coil induces eddy currents in a conductive target.
- The coil's impedance changes as a function of the **lift-off distance** (proximity to surface).
- **Used for:** Non-conductive coatings on conductive substrates (e.g., paint on aluminium).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Method | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Range |
|---|--------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Capacitive | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | $C$ in F, $A$ in m², $t$ in m | 1 µm – 5 mm |
| 2 | X-ray / β transmission | $t = \dfrac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $I_0, I$ in counts/s, $\mu\rho$ in cm⁻¹ | 0.01 – 30 mm |
| 3 | Laser triangulation | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot L}{x}$ | $f$ focal length, $L$ baseline, $x$ PSD shift | 0.1 – 50 mm |
| 4 | Ultrasonic pulse-echo | $t = \dfrac{v_{long} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $v_{long}$ in m/s, $\Delta t$ in s | 0.5 mm – 500 mm |
| 5 | Eddy current lift-off | $Z_L = j \omega L_0 \!\left(1 - k^2\right) + R_0$ | $k$ coupling coefficient | 0 – 2 mm coating |
| 6 | LVDT (differential) | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot (d_1 - d_2)$ | $K_{LVDT}$ sensitivity in V/mm | 0.1 – 25 mm |
| 7 | Combined LVDT C-frame | $t = d_1 + d_2 - K$ | $K$ is constant frame gap | 0.5 – 50 mm |
| 8 | Beer–Lambert (general) | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$ (absorbance form) | $\varepsilon$ molar absorptivity | Used in spectroscopy analogies |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Exam Tip:** When the examiner asks "which gauge for a hot strip mill?", the unhesitating answer is **X-ray or γ-ray transmission** — because optical/laser methods fail in the presence of luminous hot metal and contact methods cannot survive 900 °C steel.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel industry (POSCO, Tata Steel, JSW):** Hot strip mills use **X-ray** gauges at the run-out table; cold mills use **laser** gauges.  
- **Paper industry:** **Beta-ray (β-gauge)** is the de-facto standard for basis weight (g/m²), directly proportional to thickness at constant density.  
- **Semiconductor industry:** **Spectroscopic reflectometry** and **ellipsometry** measure film thickness from 1 nm to 100 µm.  
- **Automotive paint shops:** **Eddy current + magnetic induction** dual gauges measure paint, primer, and galvanised coating thickness on body panels.  
- **Glass industry:** **Laser triangulation** on float-glass lines for architectural and automotive glass.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Exhaustive Working:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No "it can be shown that" shortcuts are used.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitive Thickness Relationship

**Problem:** Derive the relationship between plate separation $t$ (i.e., material thickness acting as dielectric) and capacitance $C$, and show how the sensitivity $\dfrac{dC}{dt}$ behaves.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard parallel-plate capacitance formula.**  
For a dielectric material of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ filling the gap between two plates of area $A$ separated by distance $t$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the variables explicitly.**  
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (permittivity of free space).  
- $\varepsilon_r$ = dimensionless relative permittivity of the sheet (e.g., 2.3 for polyethylene).  
- $A$ = effective overlap area in m².  
- $t$ = sheet thickness in m.

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$ explicitly (so the formula can be inverted in the transmitter electronics):**  

$$t = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity (rate of change of $C$ w.r.t. $t$).**  
Differentiate $C$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the sign.**  
The negative sign indicates an **inverse-square** relationship. **Engineering implication:** as the sheet gets thicker, the sensor's *ability to discriminate small thickness changes* weakens rapidly. This is why capacitive gauges are preferred for *thin* films (10–500 µm) and avoided for thick plates.

**Step 6 — Compute relative sensitivity.**  
Divide $\dfrac{dC}{dt}$ by $C$ to obtain a *dimensionless* fractional sensitivity:

$$\frac{1}{C}\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{1}{t}$$

This shows that a 1 % change in thickness gives a 1 % change in capacitance — a clean, linear-in-log response.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beer–Lambert Transmission Thickness

**Problem:** Given incident intensity $I_0$ and measured transmitted intensity $I$, derive the formula for thickness $t$ in terms of $\mu$, $\rho$, $I_0$, $I$.

**Step 1 — State the differential form of attenuation.**  
As radiation passes through a thin slice $dx$ of material, the fractional loss in intensity is proportional to the slice thickness:

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -\mu \rho I$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from 0 to $t$.**  

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \rho \int_{0}^{t} dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**  

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu \rho t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \rho t$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides.**  

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu \rho t}$$

**Step 5 — Solve explicitly for $t$.**  

$$t = \frac{1}{\mu \rho}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Express sensitivity.**  

$$\frac{dt}{dI} = -\frac{1}{\mu \rho I}$$

**Numerical sanity check (Paper industry example):**  
For paper, $\mu\rho \approx 0.05$ cm⁻¹ (β-rays, Kr-85). If $I_0 = 10^5$ counts/s and $I = 9.5 \times 10^4$ counts/s, then:

$$t = \frac{1}{0.05}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{100000}{95000}\right) = 20 \times \ln(1.0526) = 20 \times 0.0513 \approx 1.026 \text{ cm (paper thickness)}$$

This corresponds to a typical paper sheet — the formula is consistent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ultrasonic Pulse-Echo Round-Trip

**Step 1 — Wave travels from transducer to far surface (distance $d$).** Time = $d / v_{long}$.  
**Step 2 — Reflects and returns the same distance.** Additional time = $d / v_{long}$.  
**Step 3 — Total round-trip time $\Delta t$:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{2d}{v_{long}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the material thickness $d$:**

$$d = \frac{v_{long} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity:** $\dfrac{dd}{d(\Delta t)} = \dfrac{v_{long}}{2}$ — faster sound = better resolution per microsecond.

**Step 6 — Example for steel:** $v_{long} \approx 5960$ m/s. If $\Delta t = 6.71 \,\mu s$, then $d = \dfrac{5960 \times 6.71 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.02$ m = 20 mm. ✓

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Compare and Contrast" Table that KTU examiners love to ask as a 7-mark question.**

| Parameter | LVDT (Contact) | Capacitive | Laser Triangulation | X-Ray Transmission | Ultrasonic | Eddy Current |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Contact? | Yes | Yes (light) | No | No | Yes (couplant) | No |
| Range | 0.1 – 25 mm | 1 µm – 5 mm | 0.1 – 50 mm | 0.01 – 30 mm | 0.5 – 500 mm | 0 – 2 mm |
| Resolution | 0.1 µm | 0.01 µm | 1 µm | 0.1 % FS | 0.1 mm | 0.1 µm |
| Hot material OK? | No | No | Limited (glare) | **Yes (best)** | Limited | No |
| Single-sided? | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Density compensation? | No | No | No | **Yes (uses $\mu\rho$)** | No | No |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | **High** | Low | Low |
| Best application | Cold rolling | Thin films | Cold strip | **Hot strip** | Coatings | Paint thickness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]  
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown/HTML inside quoted labels).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic On-Line Thickness Gauge Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Moving Strip Material] --> A2[Top Sensor Head]
    A1 --> A3[Bottom Sensor Head]
    A2 --> B1[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    A3 --> B2[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    B1 --> C1[Analog to Digital Converter]
    B2 --> C2[Analog to Digital Converter]
    C1 --> D1[MPC Industrial Controller]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Hydraulic Roll Gap Actuator]
    E1 --> F1[Mill Roll Stand]
    F1 --> A1
    D1 --> G1[HMI SCADA Display]
    D1 --> H1[Data Historian Logger]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The strip passes between two sensor heads. Their signals are amplified, digitised, fed to a Model Predictive Controller (MPC), which drives a hydraulic actuator that adjusts the roll gap — closing the feedback loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — X-Ray Transmission Gauge (Hot Strip Mill)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1[Radioactive Source X Ray Tube] --> coll1[Collimator Slit]
    coll1 --> stripA[Hot Steel Strip 900C]
    stripA --> det1[Scintillation Detector]
    det1 --> pmt1[Photomultiplier Tube]
    pmt1 --> amp1[Pulse Amplifier]
    amp1 --> disc1[Discriminator]
    disc1 --> cnt1[Counter Timer]
    cnt1 --> dsp1[Microprocessor DSP]
    dsp1 --> out1[Thickness Display 4 to 20 mA]
    dsp1 --> out2[Fieldbus Output Profibus]
    dsp1 --> ctrl1[PLC Loop Controller]
    ctrl1 --> hyd1[Hydraulic Roll Gap Adjustment]
```

**Key features of this architecture:**  
- **Source** is typically an X-ray tube (up to 150 kV) or radioisotope (Am-241, Cs-137).  
- **Collimator** restricts the beam to a narrow window (avoids scatter).  
- **Scintillation detector + PMT** converts γ/X-ray photons to countable electrical pulses.  
- **DSP** computes $\ln(I_0 / I)$ in real time at sampling rates of **1 kHz or higher**.  
- Output is **4–20 mA current loop + Profibus/Modbus digital** for SCADA integration.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Laser Triangulation Thickness Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ld1[Laser Diode 1 Top] --> lens1[Projection Lens]
    lens1 --> spot1[Spot on Top Surface]
    spot1 --> img1[Imaging Lens to PSD 1]
    img1 --> psd1[Position Sensitive Detector 1]
    psd2[Position Sensitive Detector 2] --> img2[Imaging Lens from Spot 2]
    spot2[Spot on Bottom Surface] --> img2
    spot2 --> lens2[Projection Lens from Laser 2]
    lens2 --> ld2[Laser Diode 2 Bottom]
    psd1 --> mux1[Multiplexer]
    psd2 --> mux1
    mux1 --> adc1[ADC]
    adc1 --> proc1[Triangulation Processor]
    proc1 --> outA[Digital Thickness Output]
    proc1 --> outB[Analog Output 0 to 10 V]
```

**Working logic:** Each laser is mounted at a known triangulation angle $\theta$ to the surface normal. The PSD detects the *lateral shift* $x$ of the reflected spot, which is inversely proportional to the *vertical* distance to the surface. By geometry:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot \tan(\theta)}{x}$$

The two distances are summed to yield the total sheet thickness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> 
> **Q1.** Differentiate between contact and non-contact methods of thickness measurement. Give two examples of each.  
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> *Contact methods* require a sensor to physically touch the material. Examples: (i) LVDT-based C-frame gauge, (ii) micrometer spindle gauge. *Non-contact methods* infer thickness through a physical field without touching. Examples: (i) X-ray transmission gauge, (ii) laser triangulation gauge. (1 mark for the definition, 0.5 mark each example × 2 = 1 mark, 1 mark for clear differentiation table).

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> 
> **Q2.** Why is X-ray transmission preferred over laser triangulation for thickness measurement in a hot strip steel mill?  
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> 1. Hot steel (~900 °C) emits intense black-body (visible + IR) radiation that **saturates** laser photodetectors (1 mark).  
> 2. X-rays penetrate the metal and are unaffected by surface glow (1 mark).  
> 3. X-ray attenuation follows the Beer–Lambert law giving a **direct linear-in-logarithmic** thickness reading (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **X-ray transmission thickness gauge** used in a hot strip steel mill. Derive the governing equation relating thickness to incident and transmitted intensities. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hot steel strip of density $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³ is measured by an X-ray gauge. The mass attenuation coefficient is $\mu = 0.40$ cm²/g. If $I_0 = 10^6$ counts/s and the measured intensity is $I = 4.0 \times 10^5$ counts/s, determine the strip thickness. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram** (3 marks)  
Already shown in Section 4.2 — Source → Collimator → Strip → Detector → PMT → Amplifier → Discriminator → Counter → DSP → Output.

**Working Principle** (2 marks): X-rays from a tube or isotope (Am-241, Cs-137) pass through a collimated slit, penetrate the strip, and are detected on the opposite side. The intensity reduces exponentially with areal density $\rho t$. The DSP computes $\ln(I_0/I)/(\mu\rho)$ to yield thickness in real time.

**Derivation** (2 marks): Already done in Section 3.2 — yielding $t = \dfrac{1}{\mu\rho}\ln(I_0/I)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution**  
**Step 1:** Substitute values into the governing equation.

$$t = \frac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the product $\mu \rho$:

$$\mu \rho = 0.40 \;\text{cm}^2/\text{g} \times 7.85 \;\text{g/cm}^3 = 3.14 \;\text{cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the intensity ratio:

$$\frac{I_0}{I} = \frac{10^6}{4.0 \times 10^5} = 2.5$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(2.5) \approx 0.9163$$

**Step 5:** Compute thickness:

$$t = \frac{0.9163}{3.14} \approx 0.2918 \;\text{cm} \approx 2.918 \;\text{mm}$$

**Answer (1 mark):** The strip thickness is approximately **2.92 mm**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**(a)** Explain the working of a **laser triangulation thickness gauge** with a block diagram. State the governing equation and describe how two-sided measurement eliminates errors due to strip vertical motion. (7 marks)

**(b)** A paper sheet is measured by a **β-ray transmission gauge** (source: Kr-85). Given $\mu = 0.028$ cm²/g, $\rho = 0.75$ g/cm³, $I_0 = 8 \times 10^4$ counts/s, and $I = 6.0 \times 10^4$ counts/s, find the sheet thickness in µm. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram** (3 marks) — see Section 4.3.

**Working Principle** (2 marks): Two laser diodes project spots on opposite surfaces; reflected spots are imaged onto PSDs. The lateral shift on each PSD is inversely proportional to the vertical distance from the laser to the surface. The processor computes:

$$d_1 = \frac{f \cdot L}{x_1}, \quad d_2 = \frac{f \cdot L}{x_2}, \quad t = d_1 + d_2$$
