<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a position sensor and a proximity sensor. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *position sensor* provides an output proportional to the absolute or relative displacement (linear or angular) of a target from a reference datum, e.g., an **LVDT** measuring hydraulic cylinder stroke. A *proximity sensor* gives a binary (present/absent) output when a target enters a defined sensing zone, without contact, e.g., an **inductive proximity switch** detecting a metal gear tooth on a conveyor. The key difference is that position sensors give *quantitative displacement information*, whereas proximity sensors give *qualitative presence information*. (1 mark for distinction, 1 mark each for examples — Total 3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the working principle of a Doppler radar motion sensor. Write the expression for Doppler frequency shift.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Doppler radar motion sensor emits a continuous microwave signal (typically $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz). When the signal reflects off a moving target, its frequency is shifted by an amount $f_d$ proportional to the target's radial velocity (1 mark). The Doppler frequency is given by:

$$f_{d} = \frac{2\,v\,f_{0}}{c}\,\cos\theta$$

where $v$ is target velocity, $c$ the speed of light, and $\theta$ the angle between motion and beam (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for working explanation).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*(a)* Classify position sensors with suitable examples. Explain the working principle of an **LVDT** with a neat sketch and state its advantages. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With a block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **optical rotary encoder**. Compare incremental and absolute encoders. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Position Sensor Classification (3 marks):**

| Sub-class | Examples |
|-----------|----------|
| Contact / Resistive | Potentiometric (linear, rotary) |
| Contact / Inductive | LVDT, RVDT |
| Non-contact / Magnetic | Hall effect, Magnetostrictive |
| Non-contact / Optical | Laser triangulation, Optical encoders |
| Non-contact / Eddy Current | Eddy current displacement probe |
| Non-contact / Capacitive | Capacitive linear encoder |

**LVDT Working Principle (3 marks):**

An LVDT consists of one primary winding and two secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core. The primary is excited by an AC voltage ($V_p$, typically 1–5 V RMS at 1–10 kHz). As the core moves, the mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ with the two secondaries change in opposite directions. The differential output is:

$$V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = K_{LVDT}\,x$$

After phase-sensitive rectification, $V_{out}$ is a linear function of core displacement $x$ and its polarity reverses at the null.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Infinite resolution, frictionless operation, excellent linearity (±0.1 %), robust against vibration and shock, works in harsh environments, no hysteresis.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A rotary optical encoder consists of:
- A disc mounted on the rotating shaft, patterned with radial slots/code tracks.
- A light source (LED) on one side.
- A photodetector array on the other side.
- Signal conditioning electronics.

For an **incremental encoder**, the disc has a single track with equally spaced slots. As the disc rotates, the photodetector outputs a pulse train. An index pulse provides one reference per revolution.

For an **absolute encoder**, the disc has multiple concentric tracks, each with a unique Gray-coded pattern, allowing every shaft angle to be represented by a unique digital word.

**Working (2 marks):**
Each track alternately allows or blocks light from reaching the photodetector. The resulting binary pattern is decoded to yield either a pulse count (incremental) or a unique angular position (absolute).

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Incremental | Absolute |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| Output | Pulse train | Unique binary/gray code per angle |
| Position after power-off | Lost — needs homing | Retained |
| Resolution | Set by CPR | Set by number of tracks |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Speed capability | Higher | Limited by bit rate |
| Noise immunity | Lower | Higher (Gray code) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**
*(a)* Classify proximity sensors based on operating principle. Explain the working of an **inductive proximity sensor** with a suitable diagram. State any four selection criteria. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the construction and working of an **ultrasonic proximity sensor**. Derive the expression for distance measurement using time-of-flight. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of Proximity Sensors (2 marks):**

1. **Inductive** — detect metallic targets via eddy-current losses.
2. **Capacitive** — detect any material via change in capacitance.
3. **Magnetic (Reed / Hall)** — detect magnetic targets.
4. **Optical** — detect any reflective/opaque target via light beam.
5. **Ultrasonic** — detect any sound-reflective target via acoustic echo.

**Inductive Proximity Sensor Working (3 marks):**

The sensor contains an LC oscillator with a coil wound around a ferrite core, with the coil faces forming the active sensing area. When no target is present, the oscillator runs freely. When a metallic target enters the electromagnetic field, eddy currents are induced in the target, drawing energy from the oscillator and damping its amplitude below a threshold. This triggers a Schmitt trigger that switches the output transistor (PNP or NPN) ON, indicating target detection.

**Selection Criteria (any 4, 2 marks):**
1. **Target material** — inductive for metals, capacitive for non-metals.
2. **Sensing range** — typically 0.8 mm to 80 mm.
3. **Switching frequency** — for high-speed counting.
4. **Environmental rating** — IP67/IP68/IP69K for washdown zones.
5. **Output type** — PNP vs NPN, NO vs NC.
6. **Mounting** — shielded vs unshielded.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
An ultrasonic proximity sensor consists of a piezoelectric ceramic transducer (operating in the 40–200 kHz band), a driver/pulse-generation circuit, a receiver amplifier, a time-measurement (TOF) circuit, and an output stage. The transducer acts as both emitter and receiver in most compact designs.

**Working (2 marks):**
The driver excites the piezoelectric crystal with a short electrical pulse, which converts it into an ultrasonic burst that propagates through air. On striking a target, part of the energy is reflected back as an echo. The same (or a second) transducer receives the echo and converts it into a small electrical signal that is amplified and time-stamped.

**Derivation of Distance (3 marks):**
Let $t_{TOF}$ be the measured round-trip time. Since the wave traverses the distance $d$ twice (to the target and back) at speed $c$:

$$t_{TOF} = \frac{2\,d}{c}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{c\,t_{TOF}}{2}\,}$$

with temperature-compensated sound velocity $c(T) = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ (in °C) m/s.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "position" with "proximity"** — Examiners specifically test the student's ability to distinguish *quantitative displacement* from *binary presence*. Do *not* describe a limit switch as a position sensor without qualification.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Doppler / TOF equations** — One-way vs round-trip is the most common derivation error; lose 2 marks if missed.
> 3. **Confusing shielded vs unshielded inductive sensors** — Shielded sensors can be flush-mounted; unshielded require a clearance zone. Examiners test this directly.
> 4. **LVDT polarity reversal** — Students often forget to mention that the *sign* of $V_{out}$ indicates direction; this is essential for a full 7-mark answer.
> 5. **Incremental vs absolute encoder** — Stating that absolute encoders retain position after power-off is the single most-tested point; miss it and lose 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Position sensors** give *quantitative* displacement (linear or angular); **proximity sensors** give *binary* presence; **motion sensors** give *dynamic* state.
- ✅ **LVDT** output is $V_{out} = K_{LVDT}\,x$ after phase-sensitive demodulation; linearity ±0.1 %, infinite resolution.
- ✅ **Hall voltage** is $V_H = R_H\,(IB/t)$; used in BLDC commutation and contactless switching.
- ✅ **Inductive proximity sensors** detect only metallic targets via eddy-current damping of an LC oscillator; sensing range $S_n \le D/2$.
- ✅ **Capacitive proximity sensors** detect any material via change in capacitance; ideal for liquid-level and plastic-pellet sensing.
- ✅ **Optical encoders** may be incremental (pulse count) or absolute (unique Gray code per angle); absolute encoders retain position after power-off.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic distance** is $d = c\,t_{TOF}/2$ with $c \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C; independent of colour, works in dusty/steam environments.
- ✅ **Doppler shift** for microwave motion sensors is $f_d = (2 v f_0 \cos\theta)/c$; produces audio-band beat signals (~100 Hz).
- ✅ **MEMS accelerometers** use $a = k\,\Delta x / m$ with capacitive sensing; **MEMS gyroscopes** use the Coriolis force $F = 2 m v \Omega$.
- ✅ Selection rule: ferrous → inductive; non-metal → capacitive; long range / dirty air → ultrasonic; human presence → PIR/Doppler; rotary shaft → optical encoder.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromechanical Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electromechanical Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electromechanical Switch (EMS)** is a binary sensing and control device that uses the *mechanical motion* of one or more electrical contacts, actuated by an external physical force (manual, mechanical, magnetic, or thermal), to make (close) or break (open) an electrical circuit. These devices are the fundamental interface elements between the physical world and electrical control systems in industrial automation.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, electromechanical switches form the first tier of the *Input Subsystem* of any Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), and they are categorized based on the **actuation method**, **number of poles**, **number of throws**, and **contact configuration** (Normally Open / Normally Closed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus**
> Electromechanical switches are the *physical layer* of industrial control. While PLCs handle logic, the actual **field-level signalling** (machine ON/OFF, limit reached, emergency stop, part present) flows through these switches. Their reliability directly determines Mean Time Between Failures (**MTBF**) of the automation cell.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **doorbell at your home**:
- You *push* a mechanical button (physical force).
- Inside, a spring-loaded contact **slaps** against another contact (electromechanical motion).
- Current flows, and the bell rings (circuit completed).

Now scale this same idea up to a **conveyor belt in a factory**:
- A box slides along and physically *bumps* into a lever (instead of your finger).
- The lever inside the **Limit Switch** rotates and forces two metal contacts together.
- A signal is sent to the PLC input → "Box has arrived at position 3."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Electro-Mechanical"?**
> The word itself is the operational truth:
> - **Electro** — the *electrical* contacts that switch the load current.
> - **Mechanical** — the *physical* motion (lever, plunger, rotary cam) that drives the contacts.
> The marriage of these two physical domains gives the switch its tactile, robust, and easily serviceable nature.

### 1.3 Key Standard Physical Metrics

The following metrics are universally used in industrial datasheets and must be memorized:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact Resistance** | R_c | **≤ 50 mΩ** (milliohms) |
| **Insulation Resistance** | R_i | **≥ 100 MΩ** at 500 V DC |
| **Mechanical Life** | N_mech | **10⁶ to 10⁷ operations** |
| **Electrical Life** | N_elec | **10⁵ to 10⁶ operations** |
| **Dielectric Strength** | V_d | **2 kV AC for 1 minute** |
| **Operating Temperature** | T_op | **−25 °C to +85 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical Life vs Electrical Life** is a critical distinction. Mechanical life counts pure *actuations* (no load). Electrical life counts *actuations under load*, where arcing slowly welds, pits, and erodes the contact surfaces — hence it is always an order of magnitude lower.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an Electromechanical Switch

Every electromechanical switch, regardless of type, contains these structural and functional sub-units:

- **Actuator / Operator** — the physical input (push-button cap, lever, plunger, cam follower).
- **Operating Mechanism** — the linkage, spring, and snap-action mechanism that translates actuator motion into contact motion.
- **Contact Block** — the moving and stationary electrical contacts made of silver alloy (AgCdO, AgNi, AgSnO₂).
- **Terminal Block** — screw or solder terminals connecting internal contacts to external wiring.
- **Enclosure / Housing** — protective casing rated by **IP (Ingress Protection)** code (e.g., IP65, IP67).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The contact transition from open to closed (or vice versa) follows this deterministic sequence:

1. **External Force Application** — An operator or machine applies force *F* to the actuator.
2. **Mechanical Linkage Transmission** — The actuator transmits force through a spring-loaded lever or cam.
3. **Snap-Action Transition** — Once *F* exceeds the **Operating Force (OF)** threshold, a *snap-action* spring rapidly drives the moving contact against the stationary contact. This snap prevents slow contact closure (which causes arcing).
4. **Electrical Circuit Completion/Interruption** — The contact faces touch (or separate), and current flow begins (or ceases).
5. **Contact Bounce** — For 1–5 ms after first contact, the contacts mechanically bounce. This is *unwanted* in digital circuits.
6. **Force Removal & Reset** — When *F* drops below the **Release Force (RF)**, the return spring resets the actuator to the **Free Position (FP)**.

### 2.3 Contact Configurations — The Foundation Vocabulary

This is the most heavily tested classification in KTU ESE:

| Code | Full Form | Schematic Function | Industrial Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPST** | Single Pole Single Throw | ON / OFF only | Wall light switch |
| **SPDT** | Single Pole Double Throw | Routes one input to two outputs | Selector switch (Auto/Man) |
| **DPST** | Double Pole Single Throw | Two simultaneous ON/OFF circuits | Mains + Control phase cut-off |
| **DPDT** | Double Pole Double Throw | Reverses polarity across two circuits | Motor forward/reverse |
| **NO** | Normally Open | Closes when actuated | Start push button |
| **NC** | Normally Closed | Opens when actuated | Emergency Stop push button |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are the verified standard equations for switching-circuit analysis. Memorize them with their units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Contact bulk resistance (Rho-L-A form) | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_c$ | Power dissipated as heat in closed contacts | W |
| 3 | $E_{arc} = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in inductive load that must be dissipated as arc | J |
| 4 | $V_{kick} = -L \, \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive voltage spike across opening contacts | V |
| 5 | $V_{arc} \approx V_{min}$ | Sustained arc voltage (for Ag contacts ≈ 12 V) | V |
| 6 | $t_{arc} = \dfrac{L \cdot I}{V_{sup} + V_{arc}}$ | Arc duration during contact opening | s |
| 7 | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Relay coil pull-in current | A |
| 8 | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}$ | Continuous coil holding power | W |
| 9 | $MTBF = \dfrac{N_{elec}}{f_{op}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures for switch | hours |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{n_{turns} \cdot d\Phi}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in relay coil (Faraday's Law) | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Formulas 3, 4, and 6 explain *why DC inductive loads* (solenoids, DC motor fields) are far more destructive to contacts than AC loads — there is no natural current zero crossing in DC to extinguish the arc. This is why **RC snubbers** or **flyback diodes** are mandatory across DC relay coils and solenoid valves.

### 2.5 Why "Snap-Action" is Genetically Engineered into the Design

If contacts close slowly, the gap voltage *V* across a small gap *x* creates an enormous electric field $E = V/x$ that ionizes the air and **strikes an arc** well before metallic contact is made. A snap-action mechanism accelerates the moving contact to > 1 m/s in < 5 ms, ensuring that arcing time is *minimized*. This is the design genius behind microswitches and limit switches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arc Energy Dissipated Across Opening Contacts of an Inductive Load

We consider the worst-case industrial scenario — a relay coil or solenoid being switched OFF by a contact. We will derive the arc energy step-by-step.

**Scenario Setup:**
- Coil inductance: $L$
- Coil current just before contact opens: $I_0$
- Supply voltage: $V_s$
- Arc voltage across opening contacts (assumed constant): $V_{arc}$

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:**

The supply voltage, inductor voltage, and arc voltage form a closed series loop.

$$V_s = V_L(t) + V_{arc}(t)$$

The inductor voltage is defined by Faraday's law for a constant-inductance element:

$$V_L(t) = L \, \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting this into the KVL equation:

$$V_s = L \, \frac{di(t)}{dt} + V_{arc}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the first-order linear ODE:**

Rearranging to isolate the derivative term:

$$L \, \frac{di(t)}{dt} = V_s - V_{arc}$$

Dividing both sides by L:

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{L}$$

Integrating both sides with respect to time (with initial condition $i(0) = I_0$):

$$i(t) = I_0 + \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{L} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Find the arc extinction time:**

The arc extinguishes when current drops to zero, i.e., $i(t_{arc}) = 0$:

$$0 = I_0 + \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{L} \cdot t_{arc}$$

Solving for $t_{arc}$:

$$t_{arc} = \frac{L \cdot I_0}{V_{arc} - V_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Insight**: In a typical DC inductive circuit, $V_{arc} > V_s$ (because arc voltage of silver contacts ≈ 12 V is often greater than supply voltage of 5 V, 12 V logic rails). Thus $t_{arc}$ is positive, confirming physical reality. If $V_{arc} < V_s$, the arc *cannot self-extinguish* in DC — this is the famous "DC arc problem" solved by zero-crossing AC or active snubbers.

**Step 4 — Compute the total arc energy:**

Arc power at any instant is the product of arc voltage and instantaneous current:

$$P_{arc}(t) = V_{arc} \cdot i(t)$$

Total energy is the integral of power over the arc duration:

$$E_{arc} = \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} V_{arc} \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

Substituting our derived expression for $i(t)$:

$$E_{arc} = \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} V_{arc} \left[ I_0 + \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{L} \cdot t \right] dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral term by term:**

Pulling constants outside:

$$E_{arc} = V_{arc} \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} I_0 \, dt + V_{arc} \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{L} \cdot t \, dt$$

$$E_{arc} = V_{arc} \cdot I_0 \cdot t_{arc} + \frac{V_{arc}(V_s - V_{arc})}{L} \cdot \frac{t_{arc}^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $t_{arc}$ from Step 3:**

After algebraic substitution and simplification (assuming $V_s \ll V_{arc}$, which holds for low-voltage DC control circuits):

$$E_{arc} \approx \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Compact Result**: The arc energy dissipated across the switch contacts during opening is approximately equal to the magnetic energy originally stored in the inductor. **This is why contact wear is proportional to $L I^2$** — doubling the load current *quadruples* the contact erosion per switching cycle.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Common EMS Types

| Switch Type | Actuator | Force Range | Best Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Push Button (Momentary)** | Finger press | 2 – 5 N | Start / Stop commands | Not position-sensing |
| **Push Button (Maintained)** | Finger press + latch | 2 – 5 N | Power ON / OFF | Mechanical wear of latch |
| **Toggle Switch** | Lever flip | 1 – 3 N | Manual mode selection | Slow actuation → arcing |
| **Selector Switch** | Knob rotation | 0.5 – 2 N·cm | Auto/Man/Off selection | Limited contact positions |
| **Limit Switch** | Cam / object contact | 5 – 15 N | End-of-travel detection | Requires mechanical linkage |
| **Micro Switch** | Plunger / leaf spring | 0.5 – 2 N | Precision limit detection | Small contact rating |
| **Electromechanical Relay** | Coil energization | (magnetic) | Signal-level switching | Slow (10–20 ms) |
| **Contactor** | Coil energization | (magnetic) | Motor / heater switching | Bulk, needs arc chute |

### 3.3 Quantitative Example — Relay Coil Switching

**Given:**
- 24 V DC relay coil, $L = 200$ mH, $R_{coil} = 480 \, \Omega$.
- Steady-state coil current: $I_0 = V_s / R_{coil} = 24 / 480 = 0.05$ A.

**Find:** Arc energy if contact opens.

**Solution:**

$$E_{arc} = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (0.2) \cdot (0.05)^2$$

$$E_{arc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.0025 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{J}$$

This tiny 0.25 mJ is enough to pit a silver contact over thousands of cycles — confirming why **snubber diodes** are mandatory across relay coils.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart — Electromechanical Switch Universe

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Electromechanical Switches]
    rootA --> cat1[Manual Actuation]
    rootA --> cat2[Mechanical Actuation]
    rootA --> cat3[Electromagnetic Actuation]

    cat1 --> man1[Push Button NO]
    cat1 --> man2[Push Button NC]
    cat1 --> man3[Toggle Switch]
    cat1 --> man4[Selector Switch]

    cat2 --> mech1[Limit Switch]
    cat2 --> mech2[Micro Switch]
    cat2 --> mech3[Pressure Switch]
    mech1 --> mech1a[Plunger Type]
    mech1 --> mech1b[Roller Lever Type]
    mech1 --> mech1c[Rotary Shaft Type]

    cat3 --> em1[Electromechanical Relay]
    cat3 --> em2[Contactor]
    cat3 --> em3[Solenoid Switch]
    em1 --> em1a[SPDT Relay]
    em1 --> em1b[DPDT Relay]
    em1 --> em1c[Latching Relay]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Limit Switch Internal Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    extInput[External Cam or Object] --> actuator1[Actuator Plunger]
    actuator1 --> springMechanism[Snap Action Spring]
    springMechanism --> movingContact[Moving Contact]
    movingContact --> stationaryA[Stationary Contact A NC]
    movingContact --> stationaryB[Stationary Contact B NO]
    springMechanism --> resetSpring[Return Spring]
    resetSpring --> freePos[Free Position Reset]
    stationaryA --> termA[Terminal COM]
    stationaryB --> termB[Terminal COM]
    termA --> plcIn1[PLC Digital Input 1]
    termB --> plcIn2[PLC Digital Input 2]
```

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram — SPDT Selector Switch

```mermaid
graph TD
    pos1[Position 1 Left] -->|Operator Rotates Knob| pos2[Position 2 Center]
    pos2 -->|Clockwise Rotation| pos3[Position 3 Right]
    pos3 -->|Counter Clockwise| pos2
    pos2 -->|Counter Clockwise| pos1
    pos1 -.->|Contact Closed| load1[Load A Energized]
    pos2 -.->|Both Contacts Open| loadOff[All Loads OFF]
    pos3 -.->|Contact Closed| load2[Load B Energized]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Signal Flow from Field Switch to PLC

```mermaid
graph TD
    fieldDev[Field Device Sensor] --> ems[Electromechanical Switch Contact]
    ems --> wire[Shielded Cable]
    wire --> termBlock[PLC Terminal Block]
    termBlock --> optoIso[Optical Isolation Barrier]
    optoIso --> filt[RC Input Filter]
    filt --> ledInd[LED Status Indicator]
    filt --> latch[Input Latch Register]
    latch --> cpu[PLC CPU Scan Cycle]
    cpu --> programExec[Ladder Logic Execution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram**: The optical isolation stage is the boundary that protects the delicate 5 V PLC logic from the **dirty industrial 24 V DC** world. The filter removes the contact bounce signature, and the latch freezes the signal for the duration of one PLC scan (typically 10–20 ms). Without this architecture, contact bounce would cause false triggering of high-speed counters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define an electromechanical switch. Differentiate between Normally Open (NO) and Normally Closed (NC) contacts with one industrial example each.

**Model Answer:**
An *electromechanical switch* is a device that opens or closes an electrical circuit through the mechanical motion of one or more contacts, driven by an external force.

- **NO (Normally Open):** Contacts are *open* in the de-energized / unactuated state. They *close* only when actuated. *Example: Start push button on a motor starter.*
- **NC (Normally Closed):** Contacts are *closed* in the de-energized / unactuated state. They *open* when actuated. *Example: Emergency Stop push button (must break the circuit when pressed).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is *snap-action* in a limit switch? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer:**
Snap-action is a mechanism in which the moving contact jumps rapidly from one position to the other once the actuating force crosses a threshold (Operating Force). It is *essential* because rapid contact transition minimizes the time spent in the *intermediate arcing region* between the contacts. This dramatically reduces contact erosion, prevents welding of contacts, and gives a clean, bounce-suppressed digital signal to the PLC input.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **roller lever type limit switch** with a neat functional diagram. List any four industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of circuit diagrams, explain the following contact configurations: **SPST, SPDT, DPST, and DPDT**. Give one application for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

A roller lever type limit switch consists of:
1. **Roller Lever** — Rotates about a pivot when struck by a moving cam.
2. **Shaft** — Transmits lever rotation to the internal mechanism.
3. **Snap-Action Spring** — Stores energy and releases it abruptly at a set angular position.
4. **Contact Block** — Contains NO and/or NC contact pairs with silver alloy faces.
5. **Terminal Housing** — Sealed enclosure rated IP65/IP67 for industrial environments.

**Working:**
- A moving machine part (e.g., a conveyor pallet) physically contacts the roller.
- The lever rotates by an angle θ, stretching the snap-action spring.
- When the lever passes the *operating angle*, the spring snaps the moving contact from NC to NO (or vice versa) in < 5 ms.
- A hard electrical signal is sent to the PLC: "Pallet has reached position X."
- When the part retracts, the return spring pulls the lever back to the Free Position, and the contact reverts to its normal state.

**Industrial Applications:**
- End-of-travel detection on CNC machine tool slides.
- Over-travel safety cut-off on overhead cranes.
- Pallet indexing verification on assembly conveyors.
- Door interlock on industrial enclosures (preventing operation while open).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**SPST (Single Pole Single Throw):** Two terminals, one contact pair. Acts as a simple ON/OFF switch.
*Application:* Wall light switch, basic machine power cut-off.

**SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw):** Three terminals (COM, NO, NC). Routes the common terminal between two outputs.
*Application:* Auto/Manual selector on a control panel.

**DPST (Double Pole Single Throw):** Four terminals, two independent ON/OFF contact pairs ganged together.
*Application:* Simultaneous switching of Line and Neutral in a single-phase supply.

**DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw):** Six terminals, two independent SPDT switches ganged together. Used for *polarity reversal*.
*Application:* Forward/Reverse motor direction switching, battery polarity swap.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **electromechanical relay** with a labeled diagram. Derive the relationship between coil voltage and pull-in current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **electromechanical switches** with **solid-state switches** under the following heads: switching speed, contact bounce, electrical isolation, life span, heat dissipation, and overload capability. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

An **electromechanical relay** consists of:
- **Coil** (copper wire wound on a soft iron core)
- **Armature** (movable ferromagnetic lever)
- **Spring** (returns armature when coil is de-energized)
- **Contacts** (NO and/or NC)
- **Yoke / Core** (completes the magnetic flux path)

**Working Principle:**
- When coil voltage $V_{coil}$ is applied, current $I_{coil} = V_{coil} / R_{coil}$ flows.
- This current produces magnetomotive force (MMF) = $N \cdot I_{coil}$.
- The MMF creates magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the iron core.
- The flux attracts the armature against the spring force.
- The armature mechanically moves the contact pair, closing the NO contact (or opening the NC contact).

**Pull-in Current Derivation:**

$$I_{pull\text{-}in} = \frac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$$

This is the minimum coil current that produces sufficient flux density $B$ to overcome the spring restoring force $F_s$:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} \geq F_s$$

For pull-in to occur:

$$B \geq \sqrt{\frac{2 \mu_0 F_s}{A}}$$

Since $B \propto I_{coil}$ in the linear region of the B-H curve:

$$I_{pull\text{-}in} \geq \frac{N \cdot \Phi_{min}}{\Re_{mag}}$$

where $\Re_{mag}$ is the magnetic reluctance of the core.

---

**Solution to (b):**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Switch | Solid-State Switch (SSR, Transistor) |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching Speed** | 10 – 50 ms (slow, mechanical) | 1 – 100 µs (very fast) |
| **Contact Bounce** | 1 – 5 ms (unwanted) | Zero (no moving parts) |
| **Electrical Isolation** | 4 kV galvanic (inherent) | Opto-isolated, ~2.5 kV |
| **Life Span** | 10⁵ – 10⁶ operations | > 10⁹ operations |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Negligible (low R_on) | Significant (V × I loss) |
| **Overload Capability** | High surge (10× rating short term) | Limited, sensitive to dv/dt |

**Conclusion:** Electromechanical switches dominate in *low-speed, high-isolation, high-surge* applications (motor starters, E-stops, safety interlocks). Solid-state switches dominate in *high-speed, high-cycle* applications (PWM drives, fast counters, semiconductor manufacturing).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Electromechanical Switch questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting NO/NC conventions** — A 3-mark answer that draws the contact but does not state "in the unactuated state" loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **No mention of IP rating or contact material** — Examiner expects industrial-grade vocabulary (AgCdO, IP65).
> 3. **Confusing Relay with Contactor** — Relays handle signal-level loads (≤ 5 A); Contactors handle power loads (≥ 9 A) with arc chutes.
> 4. **Skipping the snap-action explanation** — A limit switch answer without "snap-action = rapid contact transfer = reduced arcing" is considered incomplete.
> 5. **In derivation questions, students often jump from KVL to final answer** — always show all 5–6 integration steps to claim full marks.
> 6. **Block diagrams must have arrows showing signal flow direction** — undirected arrows are treated as missing.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Electromechanical switch = *mechanical motion* of contacts to make/break a circuit.
- **Three big families**: Manual (push button, toggle, selector), Mechanical (limit, micro, pressure), Electromagnetic (relay, contactor, solenoid).
- **Poles and Throws**:
  - **SPST** = 1 circuit, ON/OFF only.
  - **SPDT** = 1 circuit, choice of 2 outputs (selector).
  - **DPST** = 2 simultaneous ON/OFF (line + neutral cut-off).
  - **DPDT** = 2 simultaneous SPDT (motor reverse).
- **NO vs NC**:
  - **NO** = closed *only when actuated*. Example: Start button.
  - **NC** = open *only when actuated*. Example: Emergency Stop.
- **Key metrics to memorize**: R_c ≤ 50 mΩ, R_i ≥ 100 MΩ, dielectric 2 kV, mech life 10⁶, elec life 10⁵.
- **Snap-action** = essential for arc suppression and bounce-free digital signals.
- **Critical formula**: $E_{arc} = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ — arc energy scales with the *square* of current.
- **Pull-in current** of relay: $I_{pull} = V_{coil} / R_{coil}$.
- **DC arc problem**: DC inductive loads need **snubber diodes or RC networks**; AC has natural zero-crossing.
- **Contact materials**: AgCdO (general), AgSnO₂ (motor loads, welding-resistant), AgNi (low load, long life).
- **IP rating** of industrial EMS: minimum IP65 for washdown environments, IP67 for submerged.
- **Architecture rule**: Field Switch → Cable → Opto-Isolation → Filter → Latch → CPU → Ladder Logic.
- **Examiner trick**: When asked to "compare EMS vs SSR", always use a *tabular* format — never a paragraph. Tables get full marks; paragraphs lose structure marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT and RVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** LVDT and RVDT # Module 3 — LVDT and RVDT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) > [!NOTE] > **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive, inductive displacement transducer that converts **linear mechanical displacement** into a corresponding **electrical (AC) voltage signal**. It operates on the principle of mutual induction between a primary winding and two secondary windings, with a movable soft-iron core controlling the magnetic coupling. The term *differential* refers to the use of **two identical secondary windings** connected in **series opposition**, whose output is the *algebraic difference* of the two individual induced EMFs. **Working Frequency (Excitation Source):** An AC excitation in the range of **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (typically **5 kHz** or **10 kHz**) is supplied to the primary. **Range of Measurement:** ±0.1 mm up to ±250 mm depending on construction. --- ### 1.2 Rotary Variable Differential Transformer (RVDT) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer)** is the angular-displacement counterpart of the LVDT. It converts **rotational (angular) displacement** of a shaft into a proportional AC voltage. The construction is identical to the LVDT, except the soft-iron core is shaped like a **cam (or specially profiled rotor)** to maintain a *linear output–angle relationship* over a limited angular range (typically **±40°**). --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **LVDT — The "Magnetic Tug-of-War" Analogy** Imagine two identical speakers (the *secondary coils*) facing each other across a small gap. A microphone (the *primary*) sends out a continuous musical tone. A **metal plate (the core)** slides between them. - When the plate sits **exactly in the middle**, the music reaching both speakers is equally loud → they cancel each other out (because they are wired in *opposition*). The output is **silent (0 V)**. - Slide the plate slightly **to the right** → the right speaker hears louder music, the left speaker hears weaker music → the *difference* in loudness is converted to a voltage, whose **sign** tells you the *direction* of motion, and **magnitude** tells you *how far* it moved. This "loudness difference" is exactly what the two secondaries of an LVDT produce in voltage form. **RVDT** is the same game, except the metal plate is **shaped like a cam** and rotates instead of sliding — used for measuring shaft rotation in control systems and servo feedback loops. > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway for the board exam:** LVDT/RVDT are *electrical analogues* of a *wheatstone bridge* for AC — null-balance is achieved at the central/reference position, and any deviation produces a *differential output* proportional to the displacement. --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Operating excitation frequency:** 1 kHz – 25 kHz - **Standard null voltage (residual):** < **0.5 %** of full-scale output (a quality figure) - **Linearity:** typically **±0.25 %** of full scale - **Resolution:** effectively **infinite** (limited only by the readout voltmeter) - **Hysteresis:** typically **< 0.1 %** of full scale - **Sensitivity (k):** measured in **mV per mm per volt of excitation** (mV/mm/V) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction of the LVDT The LVDT consists of the following components assembled coaxially: 1. **One primary winding (P)** — wound at the **centre** of a hollow cylindrical former (bobbin). 2. **Two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** — wound symmetrically on either side of the primary, each having **equal number of turns (N_s1 = N_s2)**. 3. **A movable soft-iron core (armature)** — slides freely inside the hollow former along the axis. 4. **A non-magnetic, non-conductive shield** — surrounds the assembly to provide electrostatic and magnetic shielding from external fields. 5. **End plates** — mechanical stops that limit the travel of the core to within the linear range. > [!NOTE] > The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (also called *anti-series*). This means their induced EMFs **subtract**, so: > $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ --- ## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step) | Step | Action | Magnetic / Electrical Consequence | |:----:|:-------|:----------------------------------| | 1 | Apply AC excitation $V_p$ of frequency $f$ to primary **P** | An alternating flux $\phi_p$ is set up in the core region | | 2 | Flux links with **S₁** and **S₂** through the movable armature | Induces EMFs $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$ in the secondaries | | 3 | When armature is at **null (centre)** position | Flux linked to S₁ = Flux linked to S₂ → $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ → net output $e_o = 0$ | | 4 | When armature moves **towards S₁** by a displacement $x$ | Flux coupling to S₁ increases and to S₂ decreases → $e_{s1} > e_{s2}$ → $e_o$ is **positive** | | 5 | When armature moves **towards S₂** by a displacement $x$ | Flux coupling to S₂ increases and to S₁ decreases → $e_{s2} > e_{s1}$ → $e_o$ is **negative** (180° phase shift) | | 6 | For small displacements, the relationship is **linear** | $e_o = k \cdot x$, where $k$ = sensitivity (V/mm) | --- ## 2.3 Why a Differential (Two-Section) Design? A single secondary would give a voltage that depends on the *absolute* position and is **non-linear**. The differential arrangement provides: - **Null reference** at centre → the natural zero point of the transducer. - **Linearity** over a useful range. - **Phase-sensitive output** → the *phase* of $e_o$ (with respect to $V_p$) indicates the *direction* of motion, which is detected by a **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)**. - **Common-mode rejection** of external magnetic interference and temperature drift. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Remarks | |:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:------|:--------| | 1 | Net output EMF of LVDT | $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ | V (RMS) | Series-opposition connection | | 2 | Linear (small-displacement) output | $e_o = k_s \cdot x$ | V | $k_s$ = sensitivity in V/mm | | 3 | Sensitivity definition | $k_s = \dfrac{d e_o}{dx}$ | V/mm | Slope of transfer curve | | 4 | Normalised sensitivity | $S_n = \dfrac{e_o}{V_p \cdot x}$ | $\dfrac{\text{mV}}{\text{mm} \cdot \text{V}}$ | Independent of excitation | | 5 | Induced EMF in a secondary (ideal) | $e_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_{\max}$ | V | Standard transformer EMF equation | | 6 | Mutual inductance variation | $e_{s1} \propto M_1, \quad e_{s2} \propto M_2$ | — | $M_1, M_2$ depend on core position | | 7 | Net output (mutual-inductance form) | $e_o = j \omega \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) I_p$ | V | $I_p$ = primary current | | 8 | Total displacement range | $X_{FS} = 2 \, X_{max}$ | mm | From $-X_{max}$ to $+X_{max}$ | | 9 | RVDT transfer function | $e_o = k_\theta \cdot \theta$ | V | Valid for $\vert \theta \vert \le 40^\circ$ typically | | 10 | Residual (null) voltage | $e_{null} < 0.005 \, e_{FS}$ | V | Manufacturing / winding imbalance | | 11 | Phase angle w.r.t. primary at null | ideally $0^\circ$ (or $180^\circ$ jump) | degrees | Used for direction detection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units convention (KTU exams):** Sensitivity is frequently expressed in $\dfrac{\text{mV}}{\text{mm} \cdot \text{V}}$ to make it independent of the supply voltage $V_p$. Always quote sensitivity with the **excitation voltage** clearly stated. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Aerospace** — feedback in flight-control actuators (LVDT reports ram/actuator extension). - **CNC machine tools** — tool-tip displacement measurement. - **Civil engineering** — structural elongation, crack-width monitoring. - **Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders** — position feedback of piston rod. - **Robotics** — joint angle measurement (RVDT). - **Process valves** — RVDT feedback on the valve-stem rotation. > [!TIP] > **Why LVDT is preferred over potentiometric transducers in industry:** *Frictionless contact* (no wear), *infinite resolution*, *high linearity*, *robustness*, and *no electrical noise from wiper* — making it ideal for long-term industrial use. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of LVDT Output Voltage from Transformer EMF Equation Let the primary winding have $N_p$ turns and be supplied by an AC voltage $V_p$ at frequency $f$, producing a primary current $I_p$ and an alternating flux $\phi_p$ in the core. The induced EMF in secondary **S₁** (which has $N_{s1}$ turns and is linked to flux $\phi_{s1}$ through the armature): $$e_{s1} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{s1} \, \phi_{s1,\max}$$ Similarly, for secondary **S₂**: $$e_{s2} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{s2} \, \phi_{s2,\max}$$ Since the two secondaries are **identical in construction**: $N_{s1} = N_{s2} = N_s$. **Net differential output (series opposition):** $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= e_{s1} - e_{s2} \\ &= 4.44 \, f \, N_s \left( \phi_{s1,\max} - \phi_{s2,\max} \right) \end{aligned} $$ The flux linked to each secondary depends on the **position of the soft-iron core** through the mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_{s1,\max} &= \dfrac{M_1 \, I_{p,\max}}{N_s} \\ \phi_{s2,\max} &= \dfrac{M_2 \, I_{p,\max}}{N_s} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= 4.44 \, f \, N_s \cdot \dfrac{I_{p,\max}}{N_s} \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) \\ &= 4.44 \, f \, I_{p,\max} \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) \end{aligned} $$ In **phasor form** (since $V_p$ drives $I_p$ through primary impedance, and for an ideal transformer at low frequency): $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= j \, \omega \, I_p \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) \\ &= j \, 2 \pi f \, I_p \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) \end{aligned} $$ where $\omega = 2 \pi f$ is the angular frequency in rad/s. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Linear Transfer Function $e_o = k_s \, x$ **Assumption:** For *small displacements* of the core from the null position, the change in mutual inductance $\Delta M$ is **linearly proportional to displacement $x$**. Let the rate of change of $M_1$ and $M_2$ with respect to displacement be: $$ \dfrac{dM_1}{dx} = +k_1, \quad \dfrac{dM_2}{dx} = -k_2 $$ For a **symmetrically wound** LVDT, the two rates are equal in magnitude: $k_1 = k_2 = k_m$ (a constant for the given design). Therefore, for a small displacement $x$ from centre: $$ \begin{aligned} M_1(x) &= M_0 + k_m \cdot x \\ M_2(x) &= M_0 - k_m \cdot x \end{aligned} $$ where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at the centred (null) position. Substituting into the phasor expression: $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= j \, 2 \pi f \, I_p \left[ (M_0 + k_m x) - (M_0 - k_m x) \right] \\ &= j \, 2 \pi f \, I_p \left( 2 k_m x \right) \\ &= j \, 4 \pi f \, k_m \, I_p \cdot x \end{aligned} $$ Recognising the lumped constant $k_s = 4 \pi f \, k_m \, I_p$ (which depends on excitation frequency, current, and the core/coil geometry): $$ \boxed{\,e_o = k_s \cdot x\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Magnitude interpretation:** The output is a *complex phasor*. The factor $j$ indicates a **90° phase shift** between $e_o$ and the primary current $I_p$. The *phase* of $e_o$ (relative to $V_p$) reverses by **180°** when the core crosses the null — this is what enables **direction sensing**. --- ## 3.3 Sensitivity in Practical Units If the primary current is driven from a *voltage source* $V_p$ through the primary winding (with primary winding impedance $Z_p$): $$ I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{Z_p} $$ The output voltage becomes: $$ e_o = \left( j \, 2 \pi f \, \dfrac{2 k_m}{Z_p} \right) V_p \, x $$ Therefore, the **sensitivity (in V/mm)** is: $$ k_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f \, \vert 2 k_m \vert}{\vert Z_p \vert} \cdot V_p $$ **Normalised sensitivity** (per volt of excitation): $$ S_n = \dfrac{e_o}{V_p \cdot x} = \dfrac{2 k_s}{V_p} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{V}}{\text{mm} \cdot \text{V}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{mm}} \right] $$ Typical values: **0.5 to 5 mV / (mm · V)**. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **2 mV per (mm · V)**. The excitation voltage is **5 V (RMS)** at **5 kHz**. The core is displaced by **+3 mm** from the null position. **(a)** Find the RMS output voltage. **(b)** If the displacement reverses to **−3 mm**, what is the new output? **(c)** What is the output frequency? **Solution (a):** $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= S_n \cdot V_p \cdot x \\ &= 2 \,\dfrac{\text{mV}}{\text{mm}\cdot\text{V}} \cdot 5\,\text{V} \cdot 3\,\text{mm} \\ &= 30\,\text{mV (RMS)} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution (b):** The *magnitude* is the same (30 mV), but the **phase** reverses by 180°. $$ e_o = -30\,\text{mV (RMS)} \quad \text{(with respect to the primary voltage reference)} $$ **Solution (c):** The output frequency is **identical to the excitation frequency**: $f = 5\,\text{kHz}$ (the LVDT is a *passive* device — it does not generate a new frequency). --- ## 3.5 RVDT — Transfer Characteristic The RVDT uses a **cam-shaped core** whose effective overlap area with each secondary winding varies **linearly with angle $\theta$** over a limited range. By identical reasoning to the LVDT: $$ \boxed{\,e_o = k_\theta \cdot \theta\,} \quad \text{for } \vert \theta \vert \le \theta_{max} $$ Typical $\theta_{max} = \pm 40^\circ$ (some designs extend to $\pm 60^\circ$ with reduced linearity). **Null position:** the rotor sits where the cam profile is **symmetric** between the two secondaries. **Phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD):** converts the AC differential signal into a **DC voltage** whose polarity indicates direction, typically feeding the input of a closed-loop controller (e.g., a servo valve positioner). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LVDT — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Excitation Source
1 to 25 kHz] -->|Vp applied| B[Primary Winding P
centred on former] B <-->|Magnetic flux through core| C1[Soft-Iron Core
movable armature] C1 -->|Flux coupling depends on x| D1[Secondary S1
left half] C1 -->|Flux coupling depends on x| D2[Secondary S2
right half] D1 -->|+ e_s1| E[Series-Opposition
Summation Node] D2 -->|- e_s2| E E --> F[Phase-Sensitive
Demodulator PSD] G[Reference Signal
same Vp] --> F F --> H[DC Output Voltage
proportional to x
sign indicates direction] H --> I[Display or
Controller Input] ``` > [!NOTE] > The block `E` performs the algebraic subtraction $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ in hardware via the *series-opposition* winding connection. The phase-sensitive demodulator `F` then resolves both *magnitude* and *sign* of the displacement. --- ## 4.2 LVDT — Core Position vs Output Voltage (Transfer Characteristic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REGION_NEG [" "] direction LR N1[Core at far left] --> N2[Large negative e_o
180 deg out of phase] end subgraph REGION_NULL [" "] direction LR Z1[Core at centre] --> Z2[e_o = 0
Null position] end subgraph REGION_POS [" "] direction LR P1[Core at far right] --> P2[Large positive e_o
in phase with Vp] end REGION_NEG --> REGION_NULL REGION_NULL --> REGION_POS ``` **Graphical interpretation:** - **X-axis:** Core displacement $x$ (negative on the left, zero at centre, positive on the right). - **Y-axis:** Output voltage $e_o$ (RMS). - **Slope** of the linear region = sensitivity $k_s$. - At extreme displacements, the curve **flattens** as the core leaves the coil — this is the *non-linear* saturation region (KTU exams often ask students to identify the linear working range). --- ## 4.3 LVDT — Internal Construction Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FORMER [Hollow Cylindrical Former] direction TB S1[Secondary S1
N turns
left half] --> P[Primary P
N_p turns
centre] P --> S2[Secondary S2
N turns
right half] end CORE[Soft-Iron Armature
slides along axis] -.->|Position x| FORMER SHIELD[Magnetic and
Electrostatic Shield] --- FORMER ENDS[End Stops
limit travel] --- FORMER ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **primary is sandwiched between the two secondaries**. This is a deliberate design choice: it ensures **symmetric flux distribution** when the core is centred, and gives a *true null* output. --- ## 4.4 RVDT — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Excitation Vp] --> B[RVDT Primary Winding] B <--> C[Cam-Shaped
Soft-Iron Rotor
rotates with shaft] C --> D1[Secondary S1
fixed] C --> D2[Secondary S2
fixed at 180 deg] D1 --> E[Series Opposition
Output Node] D2 --> E E --> F[Phase-Sensitive
Demodulator] A -->|Reference| F F --> G[DC Output
proportional to theta] G --> H[Servo / PLC
Controller] ``` > [!TIP] > Notice the only structural difference from the LVDT: the **straight cylindrical core** is replaced by a **rotor with a cam-shaped iron profile**, and the secondary windings are mounted at **180° apart** on a circular stator. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Define LVDT. List any four advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement transducer.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage, using a movable soft-iron core and a primary plus two series-opposed secondary windings. **Advantages (any four):** 1. **Frictionless operation** — no mechanical contact between core and windings, hence no wear and long life. 2. **Infinite resolution** — output is a continuous function of displacement; limited only by the readout. 3. **High sensitivity and linearity** — sensitivity typically 0.5 to 5 mV/(mm·V); linearity ±0.25 % FS. 4. **No hysteresis** in the mechanical sense (very low magnetic hysteresis). 5. **Electrical isolation** between input (primary) and output (secondaries) — inherent transformer action. 6. **Robust** to vibrations and harsh environments when properly sealed. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Explain the term *null position* in an LVDT. What is the *residual (null) voltage*, and what causes it?** **Model Answer:** **Null Position:** The mechanical position of the soft-iron core at which the two secondary EMFs are **equal in magnitude**, and being wired in series opposition, the **net differential output $e_o = 0$**. **Residual / Null Voltage:** In a *real* LVDT, even at the null position, a small non-zero voltage (typically **< 0.5 % of full-scale output**) is observed at the output terminals. This is the **residual (or null) voltage**. **Causes:** 1. **Winding asymmetry** — the two secondaries are not perfectly identical in turns, wire gauge, or layer distribution. 2. **Magnetic asymmetries** in the core or path. 3. **Stray capacitance and mutual coupling** between primary and each secondary. 4. **Harmonics** in the excitation source causing unequal responses. > [!NOTE] > In a *phase-sensitive demodulator*, the residual voltage manifests as a *quadrature component* (90° out of phase) and can be nulled out electronically. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [Module-3, KTU Pattern] **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the mutual inductances and the primary current. **(b) [7 Marks]** An LVDT has a primary winding excited by a **5 V, 5 kHz** AC source. The mutual inductances vary with displacement as $M_1 = (0.9 + 0.4 x)\,\text{mH}$ and $M_2 = (0.9 - 0.4 x)\,\text{mH}$, where $x$ is in mm. The primary current is **10 mA (RMS)**. (i) Find the **net output voltage** when $x = +2\,\text{mm}$. (ii) Find the output voltage when $x = -1.5\,\text{mm}$. (iii) Comment on the **direction sensitivity**. --- #### Model Solution **Part (a):** *(Diagram is the standard LVDT block schematic already given in Section 4.1.)* **Construction:** Hollow cylindrical former. **Primary** $P$ wound at the centre with $N_p$ turns. **Two secondaries** $S_1$ and $S_2$ — each with $N_s$ turns — wound symmetrically on either side of $P$ and connected in **series opposition**. A movable **soft-iron core** slides inside the former. **Working:** AC excitation $V_p$ applied to $P$ produces alternating flux which links to $S_1$ and $S_2$ through the core. If the core is at the centre, the flux linkages to $S_1$ and $S_2$ are equal, so the differential output is zero. Any displacement $x$ of the core changes the relative flux linkage, producing a net EMF whose **magnitude** is proportional to $x$ and whose **phase** indicates direction. **Derivation:** The induced EMFs in the secondaries depend on the mutual inductances: $$ \begin{aligned} e_{s1} &= j \, 2 \pi f \, M_1 \, I_p \\ e_{s2} &= j \, 2 \pi f \, M_2 \, I_p \end{aligned} $$ Series-opposition output: $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= e_{s1} - e_{s2} \\ &= j \, 2 \pi f \, (M_1 - M_2) \, I_p \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $M_1 - M_2 = 2 k_m x$ for the linear region: $$ e_o = j \, 4 \pi f \, k_m \, I_p \, x = k_s \, x $$ **Part (b) — Solution:** Given: $V_p = 5\,\text{V}, \quad f = 5\,\text{kHz}, \quad I_p = 10\,\text{mA} = 0.01\,\text{A}$. **Step 1 — Compute $(M_1 - M_2)$ at $x = +2$ mm:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_1 - M_2 &= (0.9 + 0.4 \times 2) - (0.9 - 0.4 \times 2) \\ &= (0.9 + 0.8) - (0.9 - 0.8) \\ &= 1.7 - 0.1 \\ &= 1.6 \,\text{mH} \\ &= 1.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute angular frequency:** $$ \omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 5000 = 31415.93\,\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute output voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= \omega \, (M_1 - M_2) \, I_p \\ &= 31415.93 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01 \\ &= 0.5027\,\text{V (RMS)} \\ &\approx 502.7\,\text{mV (RMS)} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) (ii) — at $x = -1.5$ mm:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_1 &= 0.9 + 0.4 \times (-1.5) = 0.9 - 0.6 = 0.3\,\text{mH} \\ M_2 &= 0.9 - 0.4 \times (-1.5) = 0.9 + 0.6 = 1.5\,\text{mH} \\ M_1 - M_2 &= 0.3 - 1.5 = -1.2\,\text{mH} = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} e_o &= 31415.93 \times (-1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.01 \\ &= -0.3770\,\text{V (RMS)} \\ &\approx -377.0\,\text{mV (RMS)} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) (iii) — Direction sensitivity:** The **sign** of $e_o$ reverses when the core crosses the null position. A positive displacement produces a *positive* output (in phase with $V_p$), and a negative displacement produces a *negative* output (180° out of phase). The **magnitude** gives the *distance* moved, and the **phase** gives the *direction* — enabling full bidirectional measurement when read by a **phase-sensitive demodulator**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of an **RVDT**. Compare LVDT and RVDT on the basis of (i) core shape, (ii) measured variable, (iii) typical measurement range, and (iv) one industrial application. **(b) [7 Marks]** Draw the **output voltage vs displacement** characteristic of an LVDT. Mark the **linear range**, the **null point**, and the **non-linear (saturation) regions**. Explain why the output is *non-zero* at the null in a practical LVDT, and how a **phase-sensitive demodulator** eliminates this residual voltage and provides a signed DC output. --- #### Model Solution **Part (a):** **Construction of RVDT:** Identical to LVDT — primary winding at the centre, two secondary windings on either side (on a circular stator), all wound on a common former. The **difference** is the **rotor (core)**: a soft-iron element shaped like a **cam (asymmetric profile)** that rotates with the input shaft. **Working:** When the rotor is in the *symmetrical position*, equal flux is linked to both secondaries → net output = 0. Rotating the shaft by an angle $\theta$ within the design range unbalances the flux linkage, producing $e_o = k_\theta \cdot \theta$ (linear in $\theta$ for small angles). The phase of $e_o$ relative to the excitation indicates the **sense of rotation**. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | LVDT | RVDT | |:------|:-----|:-----| | (i) Core shape | Straight cylindrical soft-iron rod | Cam-shaped (asymmetric) soft-iron rotor | | (ii) Measured variable | Linear displacement $x$ (mm) | Angular displacement $\theta$ (degrees) | | (iii) Typical range | ±0.1 mm to ±250 mm | ±40° to ±60° (linear range) | | (iv) Industrial application | Hydraulic cylinder piston position feedback | Servo valve stem rotation feedback | **Part (b):** **Transfer Characteristic (sketch description):** - **X-axis:** Core displacement $x$ (negative on the left, zero at centre, positive on the right). - **Y-axis:** Output voltage $e_o$ (RMS). - The plot is a **straight line** through the origin for $-X_{max} \le x \le +X_{max}$ (the *linear range*). - Beyond $\pm X_{max}$, the curve **bends and saturates** — this is the *non-linear region* where the core has moved out of effective overlap with the secondaries. - At the **null** ($x = 0$), ideally $e_o = 0$; in practice a small **residual (null) voltage** appears. **Why output is non-zero at null (in practice):** 1. Asymmetry between the two secondaries (slightly different turns, layer pattern). 2. Stray capacitances causing direct primary-to-secondary coupling. 3. Magnetic circuit imperfections and end-effect asymmetry. 4. Harmonic content in the excitation source. These contribute a *fundamental-frequency residual* (in-phase) and a *quadrature residual* (90° out of phase) with the excitation. **How a Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) eliminates residual voltage and gives signed DC output:** The PSD uses the **primary excitation as a reference**. It multiplies the LVDT output by a square-wave (or sine) reference, then low-pass-filters the result. - The **fundamental in-phase component** of the LVDT signal (proportional to displacement) is **rectified and retained** → produces a DC voltage whose magnitude and sign match the displacement. - The **quadrature residual component** is **rejected** because its average over a full reference cycle is zero. - The reference phase is set so that a positive $x$ gives a **positive DC output**, and a negative $x$ gives a **negative DC output** — i.e., the polarity indicates *direction*. A subsequent **offset null adjustment** trims out any remaining residual to give a true **0 V at the mechanical null**. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** when computing $M_1 - M_2$ from expressions like $M_1 = M_0 + kx$ and $M_2 = M_0 - kx$, students often write $kx$ instead of $2 kx$. **Cost: 1 mark.** > 2. **Mixing up units:** $I_p$ must be in **Amperes** and $M$ in **Henries** for the formula $e_o = \omega (M_1 - M_2) I_p$ to give Volts. The factor $\omega (M_1 - M_2) I_p$ is sometimes incorrectly quoted as $2 \pi f (M_1 - M_2) V_p$ — remember $V_p$ is the **primary voltage**, not the primary current. > 3. **Sign convention:** always state the *reference* for the sign (e.g., "positive $x$ is towards $S_1$"). Examiners penalise ambiguous sign statements. > 4. **Range of linearity:** the LVDT output is linear **only over a limited displacement**. Do not write $e_o = k_s x$ for the entire mechanical range. > 5. **Confusing RVDT linearity:** an RVDT is linear only over a small angular range (typically $\le 40°$); outside this the cam profile is non-linear by design. > 6. **No diagrams:** KTU part-(a) of 7 marks always expects a *neat labelled diagram*. Skip it and you risk losing 2–3 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — LVDT and RVDT** - ☐ LVDT = **Linear** Variable Differential Transformer → measures **linear** displacement. - ☐ RVDT = **Rotary** Variable Differential Transformer → measures **angular** displacement. - ☐ Both are **passive inductive** transducers requiring **AC excitation** (1–25 kHz). - ☐ Two **identical secondaries** are connected in **series opposition** → differential output $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$. - ☐ **Core (armature) position** controls the **mutual inductances** $M_1$ and $M_2$ of the two secondaries. - ☐ **Null position** = mechanical centre → ideal $e_o = 0$; practical residual voltage $< 0.5\,\%$ of FS. - ☐ **Linear transfer function:** $e_o = k_s \, x$ (LVDT) and $e_o = k_\theta \, \theta$ (RVDT). - ☐ **Direction sensing** is achieved via **phase reversal** (180°) of $e_o$ on crossing the null. - ☐ A **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** converts the AC differential output into a signed DC voltage proportional to displacement. - ☐ Typical sensitivity: **0.5 to 5 mV per (mm · V)** of excitation. - ☐ Output EMF formula: $\boxed{e_o = j\, 2\pi f\, (M_1 - M_2)\, I_p}$. - ☐ RVDT rotor is **cam-shaped** (asymmetric iron profile) to linearise the output over the design angular range. - ☐ Advantages: frictionless, infinite resolution, high linearity, robust, electrically isolated. - ☐ Limitations: require AC excitation + signal conditioning; sensitive to external magnetic fields unless shielded. - ☐ Applications: hydraulic cylinder position (LVDT), servo valve feedback (RVDT), CNC, aerospace actuators, robotics, process control. - ☐ The **operating frequency is the same** on both primary and output — the LVDT does *not* change the frequency. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute memory anchor:** "**P in the middle, S's on the sides, core slides, output is the *difference* of the two S-emfs, *null* is at the centre, *sign* tells you *which way*, *size* tells you *how far*."** ---

topics.title: Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators and Their Comparison (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators and Their Comparison (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuator? (Foundation Concept)

An **Actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a physical motion, typically linear (translational) or rotational. It is the "muscle" of any automated industrial system, executing the final command issued by the controller (PLC, DCS, or microcontroller).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Industrial Automation**, actuators form the final control element in the classic closed-loop automation pyramid:
- **Sensor** → **Controller** (PLC/DCS) → **Actuator** → **Process/Load**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Actuators are the physical interface between the digital control world and the real industrial process. Without actuators, automation is merely a "brain with no hands."

---

## 1.2 The Three Prime Movers of Industrial Automation

Industrial automation broadly relies on **three principal actuator families**, each driven by a distinct energy domain:

| Energy Domain | Actuator Type | Energy Source | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | Solenoids, DC/AC Motors, Servo, Stepper | Electricity | Linear or Rotary |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic Cylinders, Hydraulic Motors | Pressurized Oil (incompressible) | High Force Linear / Rotary |
| **Pneumatic** | Pneumatic Cylinders, Pneumatic Motors | Compressed Air (compressible) | Medium Force Linear / Rotary |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Workers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine three workers tasked with moving a heavy object in a factory.
> - **The Pneumatic Worker (Air-Powered):** Light, fast, slightly "springy," inexpensive, but cannot lift extremely heavy loads. Best for repetitive, light assembly tasks (e.g., pushing small boxes on a conveyor).
> - **The Hydraulic Worker (Oil-Powered):** Extremely strong, slow, powerful, but bulky and messy. Best for heavy-duty lifting (e.g., pressing car bodies, lifting steel plates).
> - **The Electrical Worker (Electricity-Powered):** Precise, clean, controllable, and intelligent. Best for tasks requiring exact positioning (e.g., robotic arm joints, CNC machine tool axes).

Just as no single worker is perfect for every job, **no single actuator type is universally superior**. The choice depends on the application's force, speed, precision, environment, and cost requirements.

---

## 1.4 Formal KTU Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Actuator:** A device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (linear or rotary motion) using electromagnetic principles such as Lorentz force (motors) or magnetic field interaction (solenoids).
>
> **Hydraulic Actuator:** A device that converts the pressure energy of an incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) into mechanical motion, capable of generating very high forces/torques.
>
> **Pneumatic Actuator:** A device that converts the pressure energy of a compressible gas (typically atmospheric air) into mechanical motion, typically used for medium-force, high-speed applications.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Electrical Actuators — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Solenoid (Linear Electrical Actuator)
A **solenoid** is the simplest linear electrical actuator. It consists of a helical coil wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger. When energized, the magnetic field pulls the plunger into the coil, producing linear motion.

- **Operating Principle:** Energized coil → magnetic flux → axial force on plunger.
- **Stroke:** Short (typically 10–30 mm).
- **Force:** Small to medium (a few N to several hundred N).
- **Typical Use:** Valve actuation, door locks, relay switching, pin ejection.

### 2.1.2 DC Motors
Operate on **DC supply**. Use Lorentz force ($F = BIL$) interaction between current-carrying conductors and a magnetic field.
- **Advantages:** Simple speed control via armature voltage, high starting torque, easy reversal.
- **Disadvantage:** Brush wear, commutator maintenance.

### 2.1.3 AC Motors
- **Induction Motors:** Work on AC supply, robust, low maintenance, used for constant-speed applications (conveyors, pumps, fans).
- **Synchronous Motors:** Run at synchronous speed ($N_s = 120f/P$), used where precise constant speed is needed.

### 2.1.4 Servo Motors
Closed-loop rotary/linear actuators with **encoder feedback**. Provide high-precision position, velocity, and torque control. Backbone of CNC machines, robotics, and pick-and-place systems.

### 2.1.5 Stepper Motors
Open-loop position-control motors that rotate in discrete **steps** (e.g., 1.8°/step). Each pulse advances the rotor by one step. No feedback required for basic positioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Servo = Closed-loop (accurate but costly). Stepper = Open-loop (cheaper but can lose steps under overload).

---

## 2.2 Hydraulic Actuators — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Hydraulic Cylinder
A **hydraulic cylinder** converts fluid pressure into linear force. The governing physics:

$$F = P \times A$$

where $F$ is the thrust force (N), $P$ is the fluid pressure (Pa), and $A$ is the piston cross-sectional area (m²).

- **Single-Acting Cylinder:** Fluid pressure acts on one side only; return stroke achieved by spring or gravity.
- **Double-Acting Cylinder:** Fluid pressure acts on both sides; provides controlled force in both directions.

### 2.2.2 Hydraulic Motor
Converts hydraulic pressure into rotary motion. Based on **positive displacement** (gear, vane, piston) principles. Used where very high torque at low speed is required (e.g., excavator slewing, crane drives).

### 2.2.3 Why Hydraulic? — The Power Density Argument
Hydraulic systems can generate forces **10–50 times greater** than pneumatics of the same cylinder size because liquids are **incompressible** and operate at very high pressures (typically **100–350 bar**).

---

## 2.3 Pneumatic Actuators — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Pneumatic Cylinder
Operates on the same principle as hydraulic cylinders ($F = P \times A$), but uses **compressed air** at low pressure (typically **4–8 bar**).

- **Compressibility** of air makes pneumatic systems inherently "soft" and slightly springy.
- **Force** is much lower than hydraulic for the same bore diameter.
- **Speed** is much higher (pneumatic cylinders can cycle 100+ times per minute).

### 2.3.2 Pneumatic Motor
Rotary version, typically vane-type or piston-type. Used in portable tools, drills, nut-runners. Inherently safe in explosive environments (no sparks).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder Thrust Force | $F = P \times A$ | N (Newton) | Hydraulic & Pneumatic |
| 2 | Piston Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | Cylinders |
| 3 | Annular (Rod-side) Area | $A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | m² | Double-Acting Cylinders |
| 4 | Motor Power (Hydraulic) | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \times p}{600}$ | kW | Hydraulic |
| 5 | Motor Power (Pneumatic) | $P_{pneu} = Q \times p$ | W (Watts) | Pneumatic |
| 6 | Motor Torque (Hydraulic) | $T = \dfrac{\Delta p \times D_{disp}}{2\pi}$ | N·m | Hydraulic Motor |
| 7 | Synchronous Speed (AC Motor) | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | Electrical |
| 8 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Fluid Power |
| 9 | Solenoid Pull Force (approx.) | $F \approx \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | N | Electrical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Hydraulic pressure range: **100 – 350 bar**
> - Pneumatic pressure range: **4 – 8 bar**
> - Force ratio (hydraulic vs. pneumatic) for equal bore: ~ **20 : 1**
> - Speed ratio (pneumatic vs. hydraulic) for equal bore: ~ **10 : 1**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Dominant Actuator | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| CNC Machining | **Servo Motors** | Sub-micron precision |
| Injection Molding | **Hydraulic Cylinders** | High clamping force (1000+ tons) |
| Food Packaging | **Pneumatic Cylinders** | Clean, fast, low-cost |
| Automotive Painting | **Pneumatic + Explosion-proof Motors** | Spark-free environment |
| Excavators / Cranes | **Hydraulic Motors & Cylinders** | High power-to-weight ratio |
| Robotic Arms | **Servo Motors + Harmonic Drives** | Precision + torque |
| Valve Automation | **Solenoids** | Compact, on/off control |
| Textile Machines | **Stepper Motors** | Open-loop synchronization |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Thrust Force Developed by a Hydraulic/Pneumatic Cylinder

### Given:
- Piston diameter = $D$ (m)
- Rod diameter (if double-acting) = $d$ (m)
- Supply pressure = $P$ (Pa or N/m²)

### To Derive:
- Thrust force $F$ in the **extension stroke** (full piston area exposed to pressure).
- Thrust force $F_{ret}$ in the **retraction stroke** (annular area exposed to pressure).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Statement of the Physical Law**

Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to a confined incompressible (or compressible) fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. The force on the piston is therefore pressure multiplied by the effective area.

$$F = P \times A_{eff}$$

**Step 2 — Computing the Full Piston Area (Extension Stroke)**

The piston face is a circle of diameter $D$. Its geometric area is:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Substituting into the force equation:

$$F_{ext} = P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Computing the Annular (Rod-Side) Area (Retraction Stroke)**

During retraction, pressure acts on the **annulus** between the piston and the rod. The annular area is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{rod} &= A_{piston} - A_{rod} \\
        &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
        &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Retraction Force Expression**

$$F_{ret} = P \times \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)**

> A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm and rod diameter $d = 50$ mm. The pump supplies $P = 100$ bar. Calculate the extension and retraction forces. Take $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa.

**Solution:**

Conversion: $P = 100 \times 10^5 = 10^7$ Pa, $D = 0.1$ m, $d = 0.05$ m.

Extension area:
$$A = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Extension force:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ext} &= P \times A \\
        &= 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \\
        &= 7.854 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \\
        &= 78.54 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Retraction area:
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} \\
        &= \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.0025)}{4} \\
        &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0075}{4} \\
        &= 5.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Retraction force:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ret} &= 10^7 \times 5.89 \times 10^{-3} \\
        &= 5.89 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \\
        &= 58.9 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** The retraction force is **lower** than the extension force because the effective area is reduced by the rod.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power of a Hydraulic Motor

### Given:
- Displacement volume per revolution = $D_{disp}$ (m³/rev)
- Pressure drop across motor = $\Delta p$ (Pa)
- Rotational speed = $N$ (rev/s or RPM)

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Work Done per Revolution**

The motor receives a volume $D_{disp}$ of fluid per revolution at pressure $\Delta p$. Work done = Pressure × Volume:

$$W_{rev} = \Delta p \times D_{disp}$$

**Step 2 — Torque (Work per radian)**

One revolution = $2\pi$ radians. So:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{W_{rev}}{2\pi} \\
  &= \frac{\Delta p \times D_{disp}}{2\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic Power**

Power is work per unit time. With $N$ revolutions per second:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{hyd} &= W_{rev} \times N \\
        &= \Delta p \times D_{disp} \times N
\end{aligned}$$

In practical units, using flow rate $Q$ (L/min) and pressure $p$ (bar):

$$P_{hyd} \text{ (kW)} = \frac{Q \times p}{600}$$

because $1$ kW = $600$ bar·L/min.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Actuator Excels (Engineering Reasoning)

| Engineering Requirement | Best Actuator | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Force (1000s of kN)** | Hydraulic | Incompressible fluid at very high pressure |
| **High-Speed Cycling (>100/min)** | Pneumatic | Compressed air exhausts fast, low mass |
| **Sub-millimeter Precision** | Servo Motor | Closed-loop encoder feedback |
| **Explosive / Hazardous Area** | Pneumatic | No electrical spark risk |
| **Clean Room (Pharma / Food)** | Pneumatic | No oil leakage, dry air |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Electrical | ~90%+ efficiency vs. ~50% for fluid power |
| **Open-Loop Position Control** | Stepper Motor | Discrete steps, no feedback needed |
| **Heavy Mobile Machinery** | Hydraulic | High power-to-weight ratio |
| **Low-Cost Repetitive Task** | Pneumatic | Cheapest actuator family |
| **Variable Speed Constant Load** | AC Induction Motor | Simple, robust, reliable VFD control |

    DCV -->|Port B| ROD[Rod End of Cylinder]:::cyl

**Functional Flow Explanation:**

- The **piston** converts pressure energy into linear force, driving the **output rod**.

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an actuator. List any four types of industrial actuators with one application each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **actuator** is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic) into physical motion — typically linear or rotary — to perform a mechanical task in an automated system.

**Four types of industrial actuators with applications:**

1. **Solenoid** → Used in solenoid valves for fluid control.
2. **DC Motor** → Used in conveyor belt drives.
3. **Hydraulic Cylinder** → Used in injection molding machine clamping.
4. **Pneumatic Cylinder** → Used in pick-and-place packaging machines.
