## 3.2 CASE STUDY 2 — Automatic Bottle Filling Plant (Mid-Level Problem)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> *A conveyor carries empty bottles. A proximity sensor detects the bottle under the filling nozzle. A solenoid valve opens for **2 s** to fill the bottle. After filling, the conveyor runs for **3 s** to move the bottle to the capping station, where a capping solenoid operates for **1 s**. Count the total bottles filled; when **12** bottles are done, the line stops and a **"Batch Complete"** lamp turns ON. A reset pushbutton restarts the line. Provide a START/STOP and an EMERGENCY-STOP that drops all outputs.*

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Process Description

1. **Idle** → conveyor OFF, waiting for START.
2. **Bottle at nozzle** (sensor `PB` TRUE) → stop conveyor, open fill valve, run 2 s timer.
3. **Fill done** → close valve, start conveyor, run 3 s timer.
4. **Bottle at capper** → stop conveyor, energise capper, run 1 s timer.
5. **Cap done** → start conveyor again (cycle restarts).
6. **Counter** increments at every fill completion.
7. **When count = 12** → all outputs OFF, batch lamp ON.
8. **RESET** → counter cleared, batch lamp OFF, line ready.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — I/O Address Assignment

| Tag (AB) | Description |
|---|---|
| `I:1/0` | START (NO PB) |
| `I:1/1` | STOP (NC PB) |
| `I:1/2` | E-STOP (NC PB) |
| `I:1/3` | Bottle-at-nozzle sensor (PB) |
| `I:1/4` | Bottle-at-capper sensor (PC) |
| `I:1/5` | RESET (NO PB) |
| `O:2/0` | Conveyor motor starter |
| `O:2/1` | Fill valve solenoid |
| `O:2/2` | Capper solenoid |
| `O:2/3` | "Batch Complete" lamp |
| `T4:0` | Fill timer (2 s) |
| `T4:1` | Conveyor travel to capper (3 s) |
| `T4:2` | Capping timer (1 s) |
| `C5:0` | Bottle counter (CTU) |

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Boolean Logic

Let `Run` be the master seal-in bit. Then:

$$
\text{Conveyor} = \text{Run} \cdot \overline{\text{PB}} \cdot \overline{\text{PC}} \cdot \overline{\text{BatchDone}}
$$

$$
\text{FillValve} = \text{Run} \cdot \text{PB} \cdot \overline{T4:0.DN}
$$

$$
\text{Capper} = \text{Run} \cdot \text{PC} \cdot \overline{T4:2.DN}
$$

$$
\text{BatchLamp} = C5:0.ACC \geq 12 \quad (\text{or } C5:0.DN)
$$

The counter increments on the rising edge of `T4:0.DN` (fill-done edge).

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Ladder Rungs

**Rung 1 — Master Run seal-in** with STOP, E-STOP, BatchDone XIOs.

**Rung 2 — Conveyor run logic** (Run AND NOT PB AND NOT PC AND NOT BatchDone).

**Rung 3 — Fill timing** (Run AND PB → TON T4:0, PRE = 200 with 0.01 s base = 2 s).

**Rung 4 — Fill valve output** (Run AND PB AND T4:0 DN (XIO)).

**Rung 5 — Counter** (T4:0/DN rising edge → CTU C5:0, PRE = 12).

**Rung 6 — Conveyor travel timer** (Run AND T4:0/DN → TON T4:1, PRE = 300 = 3 s).

**Rung 7 — Capper timing** (Run AND PC AND T4:1/DN → TON T4:2, PRE = 100 = 1 s).

**Rung 8 — Capper solenoid** (Run AND PC AND T4:2/DN XIO).

**Rung 9 — Batch Complete lamp** (C5:0/DN OTE).

**Rung 10 — Reset** (RESET PB → RES C5:0; OTU BatchLamp; OTU Run).

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Validation

| $t$ (s) | Run | PB | PC | Conveyor | FillV | Capper | C5:0.ACC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | ON | OFF | OFF | 0 |
| 1.5 | 1 | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | OFF | 0 |
| 3.5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | ON (T4:1 starts) | OFF | OFF | 1 |
| 6.5 | 1 | 0 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 7.5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | ON | OFF | OFF | 1 |
| 12 cycles later | 1 | 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | OFF | 12, lamp ON |

---

## 3.3 CASE STUDY 3 — Conveyor with Part Counting and Sorting (Higher Order)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> *A conveyor moves parts past two sensors. Sensor **S1** (proximity, reflective) detects any part. Sensor **S2** (optical, height-measuring) detects only "tall" parts. After **every 10 parts** counted by S1, the conveyor must stop for **4 s** while a red "Batch Index" lamp flashes. Any time a **tall part** passes S2 while S1 is also active, a pneumatic **diverter** must fire for **1.5 s** to push the part onto a reject chute. Provide START/STOP and a manual diverter RESET.*

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — I/O List

| Tag | Description |
|---|---|
| `I:1/0` | START |
| `I:1/1` | STOP |
| `I:1/2` | S1 (any part) |
| `I:1/3` | S2 (tall part) |
| `I:1/4` | Diverter RESET (NO PB) |
| `O:2/0` | Conveyor motor |
| `O:2/1` | Diverter solenoid |
| `O:2/2` | Batch Index lamp |
| `T4:0` | Diverter ON timer (1.5 s) |
| `T4:1` | Batch stop timer (4 s) |
| `T4:2` | Flash timer (0.5 s on) |
| `T4:3` | Flash timer (0.5 s off) |
| `C5:0` | Part counter (CTU, PRE = 10) |

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Critical Boolean Expression for Diverter

The diverter must fire **only when both S1 and S2 are simultaneously TRUE** (coincident event). The PLC executes a continuous AND:

$$
\text{Diverter} = \text{Run} \cdot S1 \cdot S2 \cdot \overline{T4:0.DN}
$$

To avoid double-firing on a long part (which might occlude both sensors for many scans), the diverter is **latched by its own seal-in contact and broken only by T4:0.DN**. This converts a level-sensitive AND into a one-shot 1.5 s pulse.

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Ladder Rungs (Exhaustive)

**Rung 1** — Master Run seal-in: `Start —|/Stop—|/|E-Stop—|/|BatchStop—( Run OTL )` with `Run` contact in parallel as seal-in.

**Rung 2** — Conveyor enable: `Run —|/BatchStop—( Conveyor )`. The XIO of `BatchStop` halts the conveyor during the 4 s batch-pause.

**Rung 3** — Diverter enable: `Run — S1 — S2 —|/T4:0.DN—( DiverterOTL )` with seal-in `Diverter` in parallel.

**Rung 4** — Diverter timer: `Diverter —( TON T4:0, PRE = 150, base = 0.01 s )`.

**Rung 5** — Diverter auto-reset: `T4:0.DN —( OTU Diverter )`.

**Rung 6** — Counter: `S1` rising edge (use a one-shot `OSR` or `T4:0.DN` of a 0.1 s pulse on `S1`) → `CTU C5:0, PRE = 10`.

**Rung 7** — Batch stop timer: `C5:0.DN —( TON T4:1, PRE = 400, base = 0.01 s )`. While `T4:1` is timing, `BatchStop` is TRUE.

**Rung 8 — Counter reset:** `T4:1.DN —( RES C5:0 )`.

**Rungs 9–12** — Flash oscillator for the Batch Index lamp identical to §3.1.5 (two out-of-phase 0.5 s timers, OR-ed with `C5:0.DN` for the 4 s duration).

**Rung 13** — Manual Diverter RESET: `I:1/4 —( OTU Diverter )`.

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Cycle Time & Throughput Calculation

Suppose conveyor runs at $v = 0.5$ m/s and parts are spaced $0.4$ m apart. Then the **inter-part arrival time** is:

$$
T_{arrival} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/s}} = 0.8 \text{ s}
$$

After 10 parts the line halts for 4 s. The **average throughput** is:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{10 \text{ parts}}{10 \times 0.8 \text{ s} + 4 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{10}{12} \approx 0.833 \text{ parts/s}
$$

$$
BPH = 0.833 \times 3600 \approx 3000 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This calculation-style sub-question appears frequently in KTU 14-mark problems. Always carry the units and the numerical substitution explicitly.

| KTU Cognitive Level | Verb in QP | How the case study is phrased | What examiner awards marks for |

| **L1 – Remember** | "Define", "List" | Recall I/O tags, instruction mnemonic | Symbol table, basic definitions |
| **L2 – Understand** | "Explain", "Describe" | Explain why seal-in is needed | Conceptual paragraph |
| **L3 – Apply** | "Develop", "Construct" | Write ladder rungs for a new process | I/O list, logic table, complete rungs |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a seal-in (latching) circuit and a momentary START/STOP pushbutton arrangement in a PLC ladder program. Why is the seal-in contact essential in motor control applications? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- In a **momentary** arrangement, the output stays ON only as long as the START pushbutton is physically pressed. Releasing it turns the output OFF.
- In a **seal-in (latching) arrangement**, a NO contact of the output coil is wired in **parallel** with the START pushbutton. Once the coil energises, this parallel contact closes and keeps the rung true even after the START button is released.
- The output drops only when the **STOP** (XIO) or **overload** (XIO) breaks the rung.
- **Why essential for motors:** Industrial pushbuttons are momentary for operator safety; a continuous-running motor must be held ON by the PLC (or contactor) itself, not by the operator's finger. Seal-in mimics the **auxiliary NO contact of a physical contactor** and provides a single, predictable reset path (the STOP button), which is critical for emergency stop logic.

> **Marking note:** Award 1 mark for seal-in concept, 1 mark for momentary contrast, 1 mark for motor-control justification.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three timer instructions available in Allen-Bradley PLCs and state one industrial application for each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Timer | Function | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TON (Timer On-Delay)** | Starts timing when rung goes TRUE; output bit `DN` becomes TRUE only after the accumulated value equals the preset. | Conveyor cool-down delay; lubricating oil cycle |
| 2 | **TOF (Timer Off-Delay)** | Output stays TRUE as long as rung is TRUE, then begins counting after rung goes FALSE; output drops only after preset elapses. | Lamp that must stay lit for 30 s after a part leaves a station (operator visibility) |
| 3 | **RTO (Retentive Timer On)** | Like TON but the accumulator is **not** reset when the rung goes FALSE; requires a separate RES instruction. | Totalising machine run-time in hours; tracking cumulative chemical addition |

> **Marking note:** Each correct instruction + application = 1 mark. Vague answers like "timing" fetch 0.5.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Develop a complete PLC ladder program for an **automatic bottle-filling and capping system** with the following specification: *(7 marks)*

- A conveyor runs continuously.
- A **proximity sensor S1** detects a bottle under the fill nozzle; the conveyor stops, and a **fill valve** opens for **2 seconds**.
- After filling, the conveyor restarts and runs for **3 seconds** to carry the bottle to the capping station where sensor **S2** is placed.
- The capper solenoid operates for **1 second** on detecting the bottle at S2.
- A **counter** tallies the filled bottles. After **12** bottles, a **"Batch Complete"** lamp turns ON, the line stops, and the system awaits a **RESET** pushbutton.
- A **START** (NO), **STOP** (NC), and **EMERGENCY STOP** (NC) pushbutton are provided; E-STOP must drop all outputs.

**(b)** With reference to the above system, explain the role of the **two-hand anti-repeat interlock** in press-safety applications. Compare it with the **emergency stop** logic used in part (a). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Process Description (1 mark):**
The line cycles through *Conveyor → Stop+Fill (2 s) → Conveyor (3 s) → Stop+Cap (1 s)* continuously until 12 bottles are counted. An E-STOP forces all outputs OFF.

**Step 2 — I/O Table (2 marks):**

| Tag | Description |
|---|---|
| `I:1/0` | START NO |
| `I:1/1` | STOP NC |
| `I:1/2` | E-STOP NC |
| `I:1/3` | S1 bottle-at-nozzle |
| `I:1/4` | S2 bottle-at-capper |
| `I:1/5` | RESET NO |
| `O:2/0` | Conveyor |
| `O:2/1` | Fill Valve |
| `O:2/2` | Capper |
| `O:2/3` | Batch Lamp |
| `T4:0` | Fill (2 s) |
| `T4:1` | Travel (3 s) |
| `T4:2` | Cap (1 s) |
| `C5:0` | Bottle counter |

**Step 3 — Ladder Rungs (4 marks):**

- **Rung 1:** `Start —|/Stop—|/|E-STOP—|/BatchDone—( Run, OTL )` with `Run` parallel seal-in.
- **Rung 2:** `Run —|/S1—|/S2—( Conveyor )`.
- **Rung 3:** `Run — S1 —( TON T4:0, PRE = 200, base 0.01 s )`.
- **Rung 4:** `Run — S1 —|/T4:0.DN—( FillValve )`.
- **Rung 5:** `T4:0.DN rising —( CTU C5:0, PRE = 12 )`.
- **Rung 6:** `Run — T4:0.DN —( TON T4:1, PRE = 300, base 0.01 s )`.
- **Rung 7:** `Run — PC (S2) — T4:1.DN —( TON T4:2, PRE = 100, base 0.01 s )`.
- **Rung 8:** `Run — S2 —|/T4:2.DN—( Capper )`.
- **Rung 9:** `C5:0.DN —( BatchLamp )`.
- **Rung 10:** `RESET —( RES C5:0; OTU BatchLamp; OTU Run )`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **put the E-STOP XIO in series with EVERY output rung**, not just the master Run. Full marks require E-STOP to drop outputs **directly and immediately**, bypassing the scan cycle logic. A rung-level E-STOP XIO in each output branch (or a master `Run` reset by E-STOP combined with a hardware contactor) is mandatory for safety credit.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Two-Hand Anti-Repeat Interlock (3.5 marks):**

- A stamping press is dangerous because a single hand on the press can be crushed.
- **Two palm buttons** (PB1 and PB2) must both be pressed **within 0.5 seconds** of each other; releasing either **immediately** retracts the press ram.
- **Ladder implementation:**
  - Rung A: `PB1 — PB2 —( Seal-in TON T4:0, PRE = 5 = 0.5 s with 0.1 s base )` — if both are pressed within 0.5 s, T4:0 times out and latches a "press-enable" bit.
  - Rung B: `PressEnable —|/PB1—|/PB2—( Press Down Solenoid )` — the XIOs on PB1 and PB2 are wired such that **either** button release **drops** the press. The press cannot be re-cycled by a second press of one button alone (anti-repeat).
- **Why anti-repeat:** Without the XIOs, an operator could press-and-release repeatedly to inch the press down — defeating the safety intent.

**Comparison with E-STOP (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | E-STOP | Two-Hand Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Single NC mushroom button, pressed in panic | Two simultaneous palm presses |
| **Action** | **Hard-wired** removal of power (relay/contactor) and software reset | Software-enable bit for one press cycle |
| **Reset** | Twist-to-release + PLC RESET | Automatic reset after both buttons released |
| **Latching?** | Yes — must be deliberately reset | No — releases when buttons are released |
| **Position in IEC 60204** | Category 0 or 1 stop | Category 1 controlled stop with hold-to-run |
| **Hardware vs Software** | Both — final element is a contactor | Purely software; can be defeated by PLC fault |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Design a PLC ladder program for a **two-way traffic light controller** for a North–South / East–West junction. Each direction must display Green for **25 s**, Yellow for **5 s**, and Red for the remaining **30 s** (Red of one road = Green + Yellow of the other). Include a **START/STOP** and an **EMERGENCY** button that flashes **all four red lamps at 0.5 s on / 0.5 s off**. List the I/O table and draw the rungs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A conveyor is used in a bottling plant. The conveyor runs at **0.5 m/s** and bottles are spaced **0.4 m** apart on it. A sensor counts bottles; after every **10 bottles** the conveyor halts for **4 s** and a red "Batch Index" lamp flashes. Calculate the **average throughput in bottles per hour** and the **duty cycle** of the conveyor. Justify the use of a CTU counter over multiple TON timers in this scenario. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — I/O List (1.5 marks):** `I:1/0` START, `I:1/1` STOP, `I:1/2` EMERGENCY, outputs `O:2/0`–`O:2/5` for N–S RYG and E–W RYG, timers T4:0 (25 s Green, preset 250 with 0.1 s base), T4:1 (5 s Yellow, preset 50), T4:2 and T4:3 (0.5 s flash oscillators, preset 5).

**Step 2 — Boolean Equations (1.5 marks):**
- `N–S Green = Run · /Emergency · /T4:0.DN · /T4:1.DN`
- `N–S Yellow = Run · /Emergency · T4:0.DN · /T4:1.DN`
- `N–S Red = Run · /Emergency · /N–S Green · /N–S Yellow`
- `E–W Green = N–S Red` (and so on, inverse logic).

**Step 3 — Rungs (3 marks):** Provide the 5–6 main rungs exactly as enumerated in §3.1.5. The seal-in of the Run bit, the cascade of TON timers, and the emergency flash oscillator must all be present.

**Step 4 — Flash Bit (1 mark):** `F = Emergency · (T4:2.DN OR T4:3.DN)` and every output is OR-ed with `Emergency · F` so all lamps flash red.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget the **Time-Base conversion** (preset 250 with 0.1 s base ⇒ 25 s) and write "preset = 25". Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** per wrong preset and **1 mark** if no base is mentioned.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Part 1 — Inter-part arrival time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{arrival} = \dfrac{\text{spacing}}{v} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/s}} = 0.8 \text{ s/bottle}
$$

**Part 2 — Active run-time per batch (2 marks):**
For 10 bottles, the conveyor runs for $10 \times 0.8 = 8$ s, then halts for 4 s. Therefore each batch cycle is:

$$
T_{batch} = 10 \cdot T_{arrival} + T_{halt} = 8 \text{ s} + 4 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ s}
$$

**Part 3 — Throughput (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{N_{bottles}}{T_{batch}} = \dfrac{10}{12 \text{ s}} \approx 0.8333 \text{ bottles/s}
$$

$$
BPH = 0.8333 \times 3600 \approx 3000 \text{ bottles/hour}
$$

**Part 4 — Duty Cycle (1 mark):**

$$
D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \dfrac{8}{12} = 0.6667 = 66.67 \text{ \%}
$$

**Part 5 — Justification of CTU over multiple TON (0.5 mark):**
A single **CTU with preset = 10** replaces the need for 10 cascaded TON timers or a long-delay TON (which would require an unstable 8 s preset on a 0.01 s base and increase the accumulator size). The CTU is **scalable, reset-able, retentive on demand, and immune to the resolution limits of a single TON**. It also frees up the timer-word memory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to compute throughput as $1/T_{arrival} = 1/0.8 = 1.25$ bottles/s = 4500 BPH, **ignoring the 4 s batch-pause**. This inflates throughput by 50 % and loses 1 mark. The 4 s stop **must** be included.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks on PLC Case Study questions:**
> 1. **No I/O table** — Examiners award 1.5–2 marks exclusively for a clear I/O table. Omitting it costs those marks outright.
> 2. **Wrong timer preset** — Writing `PRE = 25` instead of `PRE = 250` with a **0.1 s base** is a half-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 3. **Missing seal-in** — A motor rung with only a START contact and no parallel coil-contact is a serious logical error (the motor would drop the moment the button is released). Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting E-STOP** — Safety is a *mandatory* part of the rubric. A ladder program that does not drop outputs on E-STOP loses 1–1.5 marks.
> 5. **Mixing NO/NC convention** — Labelling a NC stop pushbutton as XIC instead of XIO. Worth 0.5 mark deduction.
> 6. **Not validating** — Skipping the "walk-through" or timing diagram costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Cross-wired rung logic** — Placing the STOP XIO *after* the coil (in series with the seal-in branch in a way that the coil's own contact cannot re-energise the rung) breaks the latch. Avoid this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ Always start a case study answer with the **5-step KTU framework**: Process Description → I/O Table → Boolean Logic → Ladder Rungs → Validation.
- ✅ Every **motor/actuator** rung needs a **seal-in (latching) contact** of its own coil in parallel with the START input.
- ✅ Every **STOP and E-STOP** pushbutton is **NC** in the field and **XIO** in the ladder.
- ✅ **Overload relay** contact is **NC in the field** and **XIO** in the rung; place it in series with the motor output, not in the master seal-in rung alone.
- ✅ **Timer base** rules: $0.01$ s base × $100$ = $1$ s; $0.1$ s base × $10$ = $1$ s. For 25 s on a 0.1 s base, preset = 250.
- ✅ **Counters** are pulse-triggered; use a one-shot or a timer-DN edge if the input could remain TRUE for multiple scans.
- ✅ For **emergency flash** logic, build a two-timer oscillator (T_a on, T_b off, mutually reset) and AND with the emergency bit.
- ✅ For **diverter/sorter** logic, the AND of two coincident sensors must be **latched and timed out** to avoid bounce and double-fire.
- ✅ For **batch counting**, increment the counter on the **process-completion edge** (e.g., `T4:0.DN`), not on the sensor itself.
- ✅ For **complementary outputs** (e.g., opposing traffic lights), use the **inverse (XIO)** of the first output to drive the second.
- ✅ For **E-STOP**, place an XIO on E-STOP in **every** output rung OR a master seal-in rung that the E-STOP also breaks. Best practice is **both**.
- ✅ **Memory retentive** bits (e.g., `B3:0/0` in AB, `M0.0` in Siemens) are used for flags that must survive a STOP-to-START transition.
- ✅ **Scan time** consideration: a program with 200+ rungs and 20 timers/counters should still complete in $< 20$ ms; if not, segment the program.
- ✅ **Throughput calculations** in conveyor case studies must include **batch pauses**; forgetting them inflates the answer by 30–50 %.
- ✅ **Two-hand anti-repeat** interlock needs **two XIO contacts** on the press-down rung, plus a 0.5 s coincidence timer.
- ✅ Always carry **units** and **explicit numerical substitution** in derivations — examiners reward this.
- ✅ **Latching vs Sealing**: these terms are interchangeable; "seal-in" is the IEEE-preferred phrase in KTU papers.
- ✅ **OTL/OTU** are the correct AB instructions for **latch/unlatch**; do not use the SET/RST Siemens-only terms unless the paper specifies Siemens.
- ✅ For **higher marks** in 14-mark questions, add a **truth table** or **timing diagram** to your answer — these are *displayed* in marking schemes for full credit.
- ✅ **NEVER** use Python, C, or any general-purpose code in a non-CS paper; ladder logic rungs, function blocks, and Boolean equations are the only acceptable artefacts in PCRUT501.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Controllers - VFD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion Controllers - VFD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** is a solid-state electronic power converter used as a **motion controller** in industrial automation to regulate the speed, torque, and rotational direction of a three-phase **AC induction motor** (or synchronous motor) by precisely varying the **frequency (f)** and **amplitude (V)** of the supplied voltage in a controlled, proportional manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A VFD is a *closed-loop/feed-forward power electronic device* that converts a fixed-frequency, fixed-voltage AC mains supply into a *variable-frequency, variable-voltage* output to achieve continuous, stepless control of AC motor speed below and above the base (synchronous) speed.

### 1.2 Component-Level Decomposition of a VFD

A VFD is not a single device but a coordinated assembly of four power-electronic sub-systems operating in cascade:

| Stage | Block Name | Function | Core Power Device |
|:------|:-----------|:---------|:------------------|
| **1** | **Rectifier (Converter)** | Converts fixed AC mains (50/60 Hz) into a **pulsating DC** link | 6-pulse Diode Bridge (uncontrolled) / SCR Bridge (controlled) |
| **2** | **DC Bus (Link)** | Filters and stores the rectified DC; consists of a large **DC link capacitor** | Electrolytic Capacitor Bank |
| **3** | **Inverter** | Chops the DC link into a synthesized AC output using **PWM** | **IGBT** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) switches |
| **4** | **Control Section** | Generates PWM gate signals, performs V/f profiling, handles feedback | Microcontroller / DSP / ASIC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Tagging:** In P&ID diagrams, a VFD is commonly tagged as **VFD-xx** or **ASD-xx** (Adjustable Speed Drive). It is always placed *upstream* of the motor and *downstream* of the Main Distribution Panel (MDP).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Car + Gearbox Analogy:**
> Think of an AC induction motor as a **car engine** running at a fixed "RPM" (synchronous speed) and a VFD as the **gearbox + accelerator pedal combined**.
> - **Accelerator Pedal (Reference Signal):** 4–20 mA or 0–10 V DC from PLC.
> - **Clutch (Rectifier):** Takes the raw power from the wall (AC mains).
> - **Gearbox (Inverter):** Gears down (or up) the rotation by changing the electrical "gear ratio" — which is the **frequency** of the supply.
> - **Speed Limiter (Control Logic):** Prevents stalling (over-current) and overspeeding (over-voltage).
> - **Cruise Control (Closed Loop):** Uses the **tachogenerator/encoder feedback** to lock the car at the *exact* speed you want, regardless of hill slope (load disturbance).
>
> Without a VFD, an AC motor is like a car stuck in **one gear** — full speed or nothing. With a VFD, you have **infinite gears** (stepless control).

### 1.4 Why VFDs are Mandatory in Modern Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Savings Benchmark:** A VFD on a pump/fan (which follows the **Affinity Laws**) can reduce electrical consumption by **30%–60%** in throttled applications, as the load power scales with the *cube* of the speed reduction (P ∝ N³).
>
> Other benefits: **soft starting** (eliminates 6×–8× inrush currents), **reduced mechanical stress** (no water-hammer in pumps), **process precision**, and **power factor correction** (modern IGBT VFDs have PF > 0.95).

### 1.5 Classification of VFDs (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Full Form | PWM Method | Application |
|:-----|:----------|:-----------|:------------|
| **VSI** | Voltage Source Inverter | PWM / SVPWM | General purpose (>95% market) |
| **CSI** | Current Source Inverter | Selective Harmonic Elimination | High-power (>500 kW), regenerative drives |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Principle — EMF Equation of an AC Machine

The complete theoretical foundation of a VFD is derived from the **per-phase induced EMF equation** of an AC induction motor:

$$
E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w
$$

Where every symbol must be memorized for KTU derivations:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|:-------|:----------|:-----|:--------------------|
| $E$ | RMS Induced EMF per phase | Volts (V) | The internal back-EMF of the stator |
| $f$ | Supply Frequency | Hertz (Hz) | The **independent variable** a VFD controls |
| $N$ | Number of turns per phase | dimensionless | Fixed by motor design |
| $\phi$ | Air-gap Flux per pole | Weber (Wb) | Determined by **V/f ratio** |
| $k_w$ | Winding Factor | dimensionless ($\le 1$) | Fixed by winding distribution/pitch |

### 2.2 The Fundamental VFD Control Law: Constant V/f Ratio

This is the **single most important concept** in KTU Module 4.

**Logical Step-by-Step Derivation of the Law:**

1. For a motor to deliver **rated torque** at *any* speed below base speed, the air-gap flux $\phi$ **must be held constant** at its rated value.
2. From the EMF equation: $\phi \propto \dfrac{E}{f}$ (assuming $N$ and $k_w$ are fixed by construction).
3. To a very good engineering approximation, the per-phase **stator terminal voltage V** is nearly equal to the back-EMF **E** (because stator impedance drop is small: ~3–5%).
4. Therefore: $\phi = \text{const} \implies \dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The VFD's Golden Rule:** To maintain **constant flux** and **constant torque capability** from 0 Hz to base frequency (typically 50 Hz), the VFD must ramp up voltage and frequency *proportionally*, keeping the **V/f ratio** fixed at its rated value.

**Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
- Rated motor: 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole
- Rated V/f ratio: $415 / 50 = 8.3$ V/Hz
- At 25 Hz, the VFD outputs: $V = 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5$ V
- At 5 Hz, the VFD outputs: $V = 8.3 \times 5 = 41.5$ V

### 2.3 Three Operating Regions of a VFD

A modern VFD has three distinct operating zones, each with a specific V/f profile:

| Region | Frequency Range | V/f Profile | Torque Capability | Power Capability |
|:-------|:----------------|:------------|:------------------|:-----------------|
| **Constant Torque** | 0 → 50 Hz (Base) | **Linear** (V/f = const) | **100% Rated** | Rises linearly (P ∝ N) |
| **Constant Power** | 50 → 100 Hz (Field Weakening) | **Constant V** (V = 415 V, f rises) | Falls as 1/f | **100% Rated** |
| **Constant Slip Series** | Above 100 Hz | Not recommended | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Weakening Intuition:** Above 50 Hz, the VFD cannot increase voltage further (limited by the DC bus = 415 V rectified peak ≈ 580 V DC). So V is held constant, while f rises. This means $\phi = V/f$ **decreases** → flux weakens → torque falls as **1/f**, but power remains constant.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Pinned)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table — it covers 80% of KTU Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | Synchronous Speed | **rpm** |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (fraction) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Rotor (Shaft) Speed | **rpm** |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const}$ | Constant Flux V/f Law | V/Hz |
| 5 | $E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w$ | Per-Phase EMF | **Volts** |
| 6 | $P_{\text{shaft}} \propto N^3$ | Pump/Fan Affinity Law | Watts |
| 7 | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_r$ | Torque Equation | N·m |
| 8 | $f_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{P \cdot N_{\text{ref}}}{2^k \cdot t_{\text{carrier}}}$ | PWM Frequency Synthesis (Internal) | Hz |
| 9 | $I_{\text{inrush}} \approx 6$ to $8 \times I_{\text{rated}}$ | Direct-on-line starting current (no VFD) | Amps |
| 10 | $\text{Power Savings} \propto 1 - \left(\dfrac{N_{\text{reduced}}}{N_{\text{rated}}}\right)^3$ | Energy savings with VFD | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** When you use the table in your answer sheet, **never** use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ for absolute value. Use $\vert x \vert$ only inside inline math; in markdown table cells, write it as `\vert x \vert` or describe it in words (e.g., "magnitude of x") to prevent table-parsing failures.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Industry:** Chilled-water pumps, AHU fans, cooling-tower fans — savings measured in MWh/year.
- **Process Industries (Chemicals/Oil & Gas):** Centrifugal compressors, mixers, conveyor speed-matching.
- **Cement & Mining:** Kiln drives, crusher feeders, bucket elevators.
- **Water/Wastewater:** Pump stations for level/flow control without mechanical valves.
- **Manufacturing:** CNC spindle drives, textile looms, paper machines.

The VFD is the **universal translator** between the *digital command* from a PLC and the *physical rotation* of the motor — making it the cornerstone of any Industry 4.0 / IIoT architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed from Pole-Pair Geometry

**Given:** A 3-phase induction motor has **P** poles (must be an even number: 2, 4, 6, 8 …) and is supplied at frequency **f** Hz by the VFD.
**Find:** Synchronous speed $N_s$ in rpm.

**Step 1 — Relate mechanical and electrical revolutions.**

In one electrical cycle, the magnetic field of a 2-pole machine completes **one full mechanical revolution**. For a P-pole machine, one electrical cycle corresponds to $\dfrac{2}{P}$ mechanical revolutions.

**Step 2 — Compute the revolutions per second.**

$$
\text{Revolutions per second} = f \cdot \dfrac{2}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm).**

$$
N_s = f \cdot \dfrac{2}{P} \cdot 60
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard KTU form.**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU-board "must-derive" formula.** If the question states "Derive the expression for synchronous speed," you must start from Faraday's law or mechanical revolution logic, *not* just state the result.

**Numerical Verification (4-pole, 50 Hz):**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

For 4-pole, 60 Hz (US standard): $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 60}{4} = 1800$ rpm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — V/f Law from EMF Equation

**Given:** A 3-phase induction motor rated at 415 V, 50 Hz, with full-load flux $\phi_{\text{rated}}$.
**Find:** Why must a VFD keep V/f = constant to maintain rated torque below base speed?

**Step 1 — Start with the per-phase EMF equation.**

$$
E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the flux.**

$$
\phi = \dfrac{E}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $E \approx V$ (small stator impedance drop).**

$$
\phi \approx \dfrac{V}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the constant $K$ for the rest of the motor's life (it depends only on construction).**

$$
K = 4.44 \cdot N \cdot k_w
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting, we get the fundamental relationship.**

$$
\phi = \dfrac{V}{K \cdot f}
$$

**Step 6 — State the condition for constant flux (rated torque).**

$$
\phi = \text{const} \iff \dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{V}{f} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Reduction from a VFD Command

**Problem (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style):**

A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V, 3-phase induction motor is controlled by a VFD. The PLC sends a speed reference of **1200 rpm** to the VFD. Assuming the motor develops rated torque at 3% slip, calculate:
- (a) The required VFD output frequency.
- (b) The required VFD output voltage.
- (c) The new operating slip in rpm and as a percentage.

**Step 1 — Identify known values.**

$$
P = 4, \quad f_{\text{rated}} = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad V_{\text{rated}} = 415 \text{ V}, \quad s = 0.03, \quad N_r = 1200 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Find the actual rotor speed vs. the *synchronous* speed required.**

The relationship between rotor speed and synchronous speed is:
$$
N_r = N_s (1 - s)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required synchronous speed.**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{N_r}{1 - s} = \dfrac{1200}{1 - 0.03} = \dfrac{1200}{0.97}
$$

$$
N_s = 1237.11 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the required VFD output frequency (the "speed reference" in Hz).**

$$
f_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{N_s \cdot P}{120} = \dfrac{1237.11 \times 4}{120}
$$

$$
f_{\text{out}} = 41.24 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the required VFD output voltage using the V/f law.**

$$
\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{f_{\text{out}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = f_{\text{out}} \times \dfrac{415}{50} = 41.24 \times 8.3
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 342.30 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the slip remains 3%.**

At 41.24 Hz, synchronous speed is 1237.11 rpm, rotor runs at 1200 rpm, so slip speed is:

$$
\Delta N = 1237.11 - 1200 = 37.11 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
s = \dfrac{37.11}{1237.11} \times 100\% = 3.0 \% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice the slip in **rpm** (37.11) is the same at both frequencies! This is a *constant-absolute-slip* regime, characteristic of the **constant-torque** region. Slip in *percentage* is held constant.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — VFD Response to Different Reference Scenarios

| PLC Reference | VFD $f_{\text{out}}$ | VFD $V_{\text{out}}$ | Operating Region | Resulting Torque |
|:--------------|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------------|:-----------------|
| 1500 rpm | 50.0 Hz | 415.0 V | Base speed | 100% Rated |
| 1200 rpm | 41.2 Hz | 342.3 V | Constant Torque | 100% Rated |
| 750 rpm | 25.0 Hz | 207.5 V | Constant Torque | 100% Rated |
| 100 rpm | 3.33 Hz | 27.6 V | Low-speed (with boost) | ~100% with **voltage boost** |
| 1800 rpm | 60.0 Hz | 415.0 V (held) | **Field Weakening** | ~83% Rated |
| 2400 rpm | 80.0 Hz | 415.0 V (held) | Field Weakening | ~62% Rated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Boost Phenomenon:** At very low frequencies (below ~5 Hz), the stator IR drop becomes significant compared to E. To compensate, modern VFDs add a small **manual or auto-boost** (typically 5–15 V) to maintain torque. This is a favorite KTU sub-question: *"Why is voltage boost needed at low frequency?"*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a VFD (Functional Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid Compilation Safeguards applied — all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords as standalone nodes, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains["AC Mains 50Hz 415V"]:::power --> Rect["Stage 1 Rectifier Bridge"]:::conv
    Rect --> DCBus["Stage 2 DC Bus Filter Capacitor"]:::dc
    DCBus --> Inv["Stage 3 IGBT Inverter 6 Switch PWM"]:::inv
    Ctrl["Stage 4 Control DSP V F Profile"]:::ctrl --> Inv
    Inv --> Motor["3 Phase AC Motor"]:::load
    Encoder["Tachogenerator or Encoder Feedback"]:::fb --> Ctrl
    PLC["PLC 4 to 20mA or 0 to 10V Reference"]:::ref --> Ctrl
    Brake["Dynamic Braking Chopper and Resistor"]:::brk --> DCBus

    classDef power fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef conv fill:#0e7490,stroke:#164e63,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dc fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inv fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef load fill:#475569,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fb fill:#a16207,stroke:#713f12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ref fill:#be185d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef brk fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top path (Power):** Mains → Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → Motor.
- **Right path (Control):** PLC reference and encoder feedback both feed the DSP-based control block.
- **Control → Inverter (vertical):** The DSP generates PWM gate signals to the IGBT inverter.
- **Braking path (bottom):** During deceleration, the motor acts as a generator, dumping energy through the brake chopper and resistor.

### 4.2 VFD Operation State Machine (Speed Reference vs. Output)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stopped
    Stopped --> Accelerating : Start Command
    Accelerating --> ConstantTorque : V and f ramp proportional
    ConstantTorque --> FieldWeakening : f exceeds 50 Hz V held constant
    FieldWeakening --> ConstantTorque : f reduced below 50 Hz
    ConstantTorque --> Decelerating : Stop or Slowdown Command
    FieldWeakening --> Decelerating : Stop or Slowdown Command
    Decelerating --> DynamicBraking : Regenerative Energy to Brake Resistor
    DynamicBraking --> Stopped : Speed reaches zero
    Decelerating --> Stopped : Natural Coasting
    Stopped --> [*]
```

### 4.3 PWM Modulation Concept (Per Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sine["Reference Sine Wave 50 Hz"]:::ref --> Comp["Comparator"]:::cmp
    Tri["High Frequency Triangle Carrier 2 to 15 kHz"]:::car --> Comp
    Comp --> Gate["IGBT Gate Pulses PWM"]:::gate
    Gate --> Switch["6 IGBT Inverter Bridge"]:::sw
    Switch --> Out["Synthesized Variable V and f Output"]:::out

    classDef ref fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef car fill:#0e7490,stroke:#164e63,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cmp fill:#a16207,stroke:#713f12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gate fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sw fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Insight:** The VFD compares a *low-frequency sine reference* with a *high-frequency triangular carrier*. The intersection points generate the PWM pulses. By simultaneously changing the sine's *amplitude* (V) and *frequency* (f) in proportion, the VFD synthesizes the desired output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). List its four main power stages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** is an electronic power converter that regulates the speed and torque of a 3-phase AC induction motor by varying the **frequency (f)** and **voltage (V)** of the supplied power in a controlled manner, keeping the V/f ratio constant to maintain air-gap flux.

**Four Power Stages:**
1. **Rectifier (Converter):** Converts fixed AC mains into pulsating DC.
2. **DC Bus (Link):** Smooths the DC using a filter capacitor.
3. **Inverter:** Converts DC to variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC using IGBTs and PWM.
4. **Control Section:** Generates gate pulses, handles V/f profiling, and processes feedback.

*(Examiner's note: Mentioning the use of IGBTs in the inverter scores the 3rd mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the V/f law used in VFD control. Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The VFD maintains a **constant V/f ratio** equal to the motor's rated ratio:

$$
\dfrac{V}{f} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}
$$

**Necessity:** From the EMF equation, air-gap flux $\phi \propto V/f$. To deliver **rated torque** at any speed below the base speed, the flux must be held **constant** at its rated value. This is achieved by ramping voltage and frequency proportionally. If V is reduced without reducing f, flux falls → torque falls drastically. If f is reduced without reducing V, flux saturates the core → excessive magnetizing current → motor damage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — VFD Operation & Speed Control**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted from Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) based Variable Frequency Drive**. Describe each stage and the role of PWM in synthesizing the output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V squirrel-cage induction motor is controlled by a VFD. The motor runs at **3% slip** at full load. The VFD receives a reference signal from the PLC to run the motor at **900 rpm**.

Calculate:
1. The synchronous speed at the new operating frequency.
2. The required VFD output frequency.
3. The required VFD output voltage.
4. The new rotor slip in rpm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & PWM Operation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full block diagram. The text answer should describe:

1. **Rectifier Stage:** 6-pulse uncontrolled diode bridge converts 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC into pulsating DC (~580 V DC peak).
2. **DC Bus Stage:** A large electrolytic capacitor bank smooths the DC to a near-constant link voltage (typically 540–620 V DC depending on load).
3. **Inverter Stage:** Six IGBT switches (Q1–Q6) arranged in three legs, switched in a complementary fashion (Q1-Q2, Q3-Q4, Q5-Q6) at high frequency.
4. **PWM Role:** Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) compares a low-frequency sine *reference* (with adjustable amplitude V and frequency f) against a high-frequency triangular *carrier* (2–15 kHz). The intersection points generate the gate pulses. This synthesizes a variable-V, variable-f output that closely approximates a sine wave.
5. **Control Stage:** A DSP/microcontroller computes the V/f profile, implements ramps (acceleration/deceleration), and handles fault conditions (over-current, over-voltage, ground fault).

*(Full marks require explicit mention of IGBTs, SPWM, and the V/f linkage.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $f_{\text{rated}} = 50$ Hz
- $V_{\text{rated}} = 415$ V
- $s = 0.03$
- $N_r = 900$ rpm (new rotor speed)

**Step 1 — New synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{N_r}{1 - s} = \dfrac{900}{0.97} = 927.84 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Required VFD output frequency:**

$$
f_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{N_s \cdot P}{120} = \dfrac{927.84 \times 4}{120} = 30.93 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Required VFD output voltage (V/f law):**

$$
\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{f_{\text{out}}} = \dfrac{415}{50} = 8.3
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 30.93 \times 8.3 = 256.72 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — New slip in rpm:**

$$
\Delta N = N_s - N_r = 927.84 - 900 = 27.84 \text{ rpm}
$$

*(Equivalent to 3% of 927.84 = 27.84 rpm — the absolute slip in rpm is conserved in the constant-torque region.)*

**Final Answer Block:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| $N_s$ | 927.84 rpm |
| $f_{\text{out}}$ | 30.93 Hz |
| $V_{\text{out}}$ | 256.72 V |
| Slip in rpm | 27.84 rpm |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Torque, Field Weakening & Energy Savings**

**(a)** Explain the three operating regions of a VFD-controlled induction motor (**constant torque, constant power, field weakening**). Sketch the **torque-speed characteristic** and label each region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump is driven by a 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V motor. Without a VFD, the pump runs at full speed consuming **25 kW**. A VFD is installed and the speed is reduced to **70%** of rated speed to match the process flow demand.

Calculate:
1. The new power consumed (using the affinity law).
2. The energy savings in kW and as a **percentage**.
3. The annual energy saved in **kWh** if the pump runs **6000 hours/year**.
4. The annual monetary savings if electricity costs **₹7.50 per kWh**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Operating Regions (7 Marks):**

Refer to the analytical matrix in **Section 3.4** and the operating regions in **Section 2.3** for full details. The answer should describe:

- **Region I (0 → 50 Hz) — Constant Torque:** VFD increases V and f proportionally. Flux is held constant. Motor can deliver 100% rated torque. Speed range is full 0 to base.
- **Region II (50 → ~100 Hz) — Constant Power (Field Weakening):** VFD increases f beyond 50 Hz, but V is held constant (limited by DC bus). Flux falls as 1/f, torque falls as 1/f, but power remains constant. Used for high-speed operations like spindles, centrifuges.
- **Region III (Above ~100 Hz) — Series Region:** Mechanical limitations of bearings, rotor dynamics, and inverter current limit further operation. Usually not used.

**Torque-Speed Sketch Description (to draw in exam):**

- A horizontal line at 100% torque from 0 to 50 Hz.
- A hyperbolic decline from 50 Hz onwards: $T \propto 1/f$.
- A vertical line at synchronous speed (1500 rpm) for 50 Hz, shifting right for higher f.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{original}} = 25$ kW at $N_{\text{rated}}$
- $N_{\text{new}} = 0.70 \cdot N_{\text{rated}}$
- Operating hours = 6000 h/year
- Cost = ₹7.50 / kWh

**Step 1 — New power using affinity law ($P \propto N^3$):**

$$
P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{original}} \times \left(\dfrac{N_{\text{new}}}{N_{\text{rated}}}\right)^3
$$

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 25 \times (0.70)^3 = 25 \times 0.343
$$

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 8.575 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy savings:**

$$
P_{\text{saved}} = 25 - 8.575 = 16.425 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{16.425}{25} \times 100 = 65.7\%
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy saved:**

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = P_{\text{saved}} \times t = 16.425 \times 6000
$$

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = 98{,}550 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings:**

$$
\text{Rupees} = 98{,}550 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}7{,}39{,}125
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| $P_{\text{new}}$ | 8.575 kW |
| $P_{\text{saved}}$ | 16.425 kW (65.7%) |
| Annual energy saved | 98,550 kWh |
| Annual monetary savings | **₹7,39,125** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
>
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert 3% slip** into the synchronous speed formula. Many students write $N_s = 120f/P$ and plug in 900 rpm directly into it, then claim $f = 30$ Hz. This loses **2 marks** because they skip the slip correction: $N_s = N_r / (1 - s)$.
> 2. **Ignoring the V/f boost at low frequency** in Region I. Below 5 Hz, if you write $V = 8.3 \times f$ without mentioning voltage boost, you lose 1 mark in part (a) of Question A.
> 3. **Mixing up slip% and slip rpm.** They are related but not equal: $s_{\text{rpm}} = s_{\%} \times N_s / 100$. The 3% gives 3% of *synchronous*, not of rated speed.
> 4. **In the pump problem, using $P \propto N$ instead of $P \propto N^3$.** This is the affinity law for *centrifugal* loads. For constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists), $P \propto N$ — the examiner will deduct **1 mark** for misapplying it.
> 5. **Not labeling units in the final answer block** (e.g., writing "256.72" without "V" or "rpm"). KTU examiners specifically look for unit consistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

- [ ] A **VFD** has 4 stages: **Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → Control**.
- [ ] The inverter uses **IGBTs** with **PWM** (Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation).
- [ ] The **governing equation** is $E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w$.
- [ ] The **golden rule** of VFD is **V/f = constant** to maintain flux and rated torque.
- [ ] **Synchronous speed** is $N_s = 120f / P$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [ ] **Slip** is $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; rotor speed $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$.
- [ ] **Region I (0–50 Hz):** Constant Torque, linear V/f profile.
- [ ] **Region II (50–100 Hz):** Constant Power, V held constant, f rises — **field weakening**.
- [ ] **Region III:** Series region, rarely used.
- [ ] **Low-frequency boost** (~5–15 V) compensates for stator IR drop below 5 Hz.
- [ ] **Pump/Fan Affinity Law:** $P \propto N^3$ — speed reduction yields *cubic* energy savings.
- [ ] **Direct-on-line starting** draws 6×–8× inrush current; VFD limits this to ~1.5×.
- [ ] **Braking chopper + resistor** is needed to dissipate regenerative energy during fast stops.
- [ ] **PLC-to-VFD reference** is usually **4–20 mA** (analog) or **Modbus/Profibus/EtherCAT** (digital).
- [ ] **Encoder/tachogenerator** feedback enables **closed-loop** (vector) control; **open-loop** is V/f only.
- [ ] **Power factor** of a modern IGBT-VFD is > 0.95 (active front-end optional for unity).
- [ ] **Tagging in P&ID:** VFD-xx or ASD-xx — always located between MCC and motor.
- [ ] **Key KTU 14-Mark Sub-Questions to Expect:**
  - Derivation of synchronous speed (4 marks).
  - V/f law derivation (4 marks).
  - Numerical: Given speed reference, find f, V, and slip rpm (6–7 marks).
  - Affinity-law-based energy/₹ savings (7 marks).
- [ ] **Examiner loves** block diagrams, explicit formulas, and unit labels — always include them.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Motion Controllers: VFD**
*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L2–L4: Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Relays and Contactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT501 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** External Relays and Contactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electromagnetic Relay — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Electromagnetic Relay** is an electrically operated, *galvanically isolated* switching device that uses a small control current in a coil to mechanically actuate one or more sets of contacts, thereby switching a separate (and typically much larger) load circuit.

In KTU parlance, a relay is the fundamental *interface element* between a low-power control domain (e.g., a PLC digital output rated **24 V DC / 0.5 A**) and a high-power load domain (e.g., solenoids, motor starters, lamps, heaters).

## 1.2 Contactor — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Language):**
> A **Contactor** is a heavy-duty, *normally-open*, electromagnetically actuated switch, specifically designed for the frequent making and breaking of power circuits — most commonly **three-phase AC induction motors**. It is functionally a relay, but engineered for high inrush currents and arc-quenching duty.

**IEC 60947-1 / IS 13947** classify contactors under *low-voltage switchgear and controlgear*, with main contact ratings typically starting at **9 A (AC-3 duty)** and going up to several hundred amperes.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a relay as an **"electrically controlled seesaw"**:

- Imagine a seesaw in a park. A child sits on one end (the *load spring*). To lift it, a strong hand is needed.
- Now replace the hand with an **electromagnet** and the child's effort with a tiny *control signal*. The electromagnet is the small control coil; the seesaw is the **armature**; and the seesaw's *ends* are the **NO/NC contacts**.
- The moment you send a small current through the coil, the magnet pulls the armature, and the big contacts "swing" — switching a massive motor or heater.

A **contactor** is simply an *industrial-scale seesaw* built from reinforced steel, with arc-shield canopies over the contact tips because each swing is under a thunderstorm of electrical arcing.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Pull-in voltage (V_pickup):** minimum coil voltage to actuate (typically **80 %** of rated).
- **Drop-out voltage (V_dropout):** coil voltage at which contacts reset (typically **10–30 %** of rated).
- **Operate time (t_on):** coil energisation → contact closure = **5–25 ms** for standard relays.
- **Release time (t_off):** coil de-energisation → contact opening = **5–15 ms**.
- **Dielectric withstand:** typically **1.5 kV / 2 kV** between contacts and coil.
- **Contact resistance:** **≤ 50 mΩ** (new), degrades with usage.
- **Mechanical life:** **10⁷ operations** (relays), **10⁶ operations** (contactors).
- **Electrical life:** **10⁵ operations** at full rated load.

---

## 1.5 Role in a PLC-Based Industrial Automation Loop

In a KTU-viva favourite — the **PLC → Relay → Contactor → Motor** chain — the *relay* acts as the **signal amplifier** (interposing relay), while the *contactor* acts as the **power amplifier**. PLC outputs are too feeble to drive motor coils directly; relays and contactors bridge that gap with **galvanic isolation**, ensuring the sensitive electronics of the PLC are never exposed to the brutal transients of the power side.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU Board:**
> **"Relay = RUnner (signal level), Contactor = CArrier (power level)"**
> A relay runs the *signal*, a contactor carries the *power*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Electromagnetic Relay

A standard electromagnetic relay consists of **five functional sub-assemblies**:

1. **Coil (Solenoid):** Wound with thousands of turns of enameled copper wire around a bobbin. Energised by the control voltage (e.g., 24 V DC, 110 V AC, 230 V AC).
2. **Core (Yoke):** Soft-iron / silicon-steel laminated core. Provides a low-reluctance path for magnetic flux. Lamination minimises **eddy-current losses** during AC operation.
3. **Armature:** The pivoted ferromagnetic lever attracted by the core's flux. It carries the moving contact tip(s).
4. **Contact Set:** Comprises:
   - **Common (COM)** — the moving contact terminal.
   - **Normally Open (NO / Form A)** — open when coil is de-energised.
   - **Normally Closed (NC / Form B)** — closed when coil is de-energised.
   - **Changeover (CO / Form C)** — a NO + NC combination on a single armature.
5. **Return Spring:** Counteracts the magnetic pull; ensures armature resets to the *de-energised* position when the coil is switched off.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1:** Control voltage **V_c** is applied across the coil terminals.
- **Step 2:** Coil current **I_c = V_c / R_coil** establishes a magneto-motive force (MMF) **F_m = N · I_c** (where N = number of turns).
- **Step 3:** Flux **Φ = F_m / ℜ_total** (ℜ = total reluctance of the magnetic circuit, including the air gap).
- **Step 4:** Magnetic attractive force **F_mag** on the armature overcomes the spring force **F_spring** → armature pivots.
- **Step 5:** Moving contact transfers from NC to NO (or vice-versa) — the *load circuit* is switched.
- **Step 6:** On coil de-energisation, the spring restores the armature to its *normal* state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept (High KTU Weightage):**
> The force balance equation **F_mag ≥ F_spring** is the fundamental design criterion. If V_c < V_pickup, F_mag < F_spring and the relay *fails to pick up* — a common failure mode during *under-voltage* conditions (brownouts).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coil current | $I_c$ | $I_c = \dfrac{V_c}{R_{coil}}$ | A | Ohm's law; neglects inductance transient |
| 2 | Hot coil resistance | $R_{hot}$ | $R_{hot} = R_{cold} \left(1 + \alpha \, \Delta T \right)$ | Ω | α for copper = **0.00393 / °C** |
| 3 | Magneto-motive force | $\mathcal{F}$ | $\mathcal{F} = N \, I_c$ | AT (ampere-turns) | |
| 4 | Flux in core | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} = \dfrac{N \, I_c \, \mu_0 \, \mu_r \, A}{g}$ | Wb | $\mu_r$ → relative permeability, $g$ → air gap |
| 5 | Flux density | $B$ | $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | T (Tesla) | Typical relays: 0.5 – 1.5 T |
| 6 | Magnetic pull force | $F_{mag}$ | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^2 \, A}{2 \, \mu_0}$ | N | Maxwell stress tensor |
| 7 | Magnetic force (N, I, g) | $F_{mag}$ | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, I_c^2 \, A}{2 \, g^2}$ | N | Key design equation for solenoids |
| 8 | Coil power dissipation | $P_{coil}$ | $P_{coil} = \dfrac{V_c^2}{R_{hot}}$ | W | Steady-state |
| 9 | Coil temperature rise | $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T = P_{coil} \cdot R_{\theta a}$ | K | $R_{\theta a}$ = thermal resistance (K/W) |
| 10 | Arc energy | $E_{arc}$ | $E_{arc} = V_{arc} \, I_{arc} \, t_{arc}$ | J | Lower $E_{arc}$ = longer contact life |
| 11 | Contact life (resistive) | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{K}{I^2}$ | operations | K = material constant (AgSnO₂ ≈ 1.5×10⁸) |
| 12 | Pickup / Dropout ratio | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{V_{dropout}}{V_{pickup}}$ | — | Typical: 0.10 – 0.30 |
| 13 | Snubber design (RC) | $R_s, C_s$ | $C_s = \dfrac{I^2}{10}$ ; $R_s = \dfrac{V}{10 \, I}$ | F, Ω | Empirical; suppresses contact arcing |
| 14 | Time constant (coil) | $\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | s | Determines operate/release times |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Never use the absolute-value bar $\vert x \vert$ inside any table cell — it breaks the markdown table parser. Use the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Why This Engineering System Matters in the Real World

- **Galvanic Isolation:** The control side and load side share **no electrical conductor**, only magnetic coupling. This protects the **PLC's microcontroller** from voltage transients of up to **6 kV** (typical IEC 61000-4-4 burst rating).
- **Signal Multiplication:** A single 24 V / 50 mA PLC output can switch a 415 V / 30 A three-phase motor through a relay + contactor cascade.
- **Logic Implementation:** Ladder-logic rungs in PLC programming physically materialise through relay contacts (NO/NC). This is the historical origin of the *ladder diagram*.
- **System Reliability:** Mechanical relays remain the **most field-serviceable** switching device, a key reason they survive in modern Industry 4.0 plants despite the rise of SSRs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnetic Attractive Force on the Relay Armature

We want to derive the *fundamental* force equation that causes the relay armature to pull in.

### Starting Point — Energy in the Air Gap

The energy stored in the air gap of an electromagnet (per unit volume) is:

$$
w = \frac{B^2}{2 \, \mu_0}
$$

where $B$ is the flux density in Tesla and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$.

### Step 1 — Total Energy in the Air Gap

For an air gap of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $g$:

$$
W = w \cdot A \cdot g = \frac{B^2 \, A \, g}{2 \, \mu_0}
$$

This is the magnetic energy confined in the air gap between the pole face and the armature.

### Step 2 — Apply the Principle of Virtual Work

The mechanical force on the armature is obtained by differentiating the stored energy with respect to the air gap length, holding flux constant (Maxwell's stress approach):

$$
F_{mag} = \frac{\partial W}{\partial g} \bigg|_{\Phi = \text{const}}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate

Since $B = \Phi / A$ and $\Phi$ is held constant, $B$ is constant with respect to $g$:

$$
F_{mag} = \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left[ \frac{B^2 \, A \, g}{2 \, \mu_0} \right] = \frac{B^2 \, A}{2 \, \mu_0}
$$

This is the **Maxwell Stress Equation** — the canonical force expression.

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of N, I, and g

Using $B = \mu_0 \, N \, I / g$ (from Ampère's circuital law, neglecting iron reluctance):

$$
F_{mag} = \frac{1}{2 \mu_0} \left( \frac{\mu_0 \, N \, I}{g} \right)^2 \cdot A = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, I^2 \, A}{2 \, g^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form (Boxed Result)

$$
\boxed{\;F_{mag} = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, I_c^{\,2} \, A}{2 \, g^2}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $F_{mag} \propto I_c^2$ — *doubling* the coil current *quadruples* the pull force.
- $F_{mag} \propto 1 / g^2$ — *halving* the air gap *quadruples* the pull force (this is why relays have a "snap-action" feel: as the armature approaches the core, the force grows explosively).
- $F_{mag} \propto A$ — *larger pole face* → more force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight (for KTU Design Problems):** To *reduce* the operate voltage, the relay designer either *increases* the number of turns $N$ or *decreases* the air gap $g$. Increasing $N$, however, lengthens the wire → increases $R_{coil}$ → reduces $I_c$ at a fixed $V_c$. The optimal design is obtained by minimising the volt-ampere product $V_c \cdot I_c$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coil Temperature Rise at Steady State

### Step 1 — Coil Power Dissipation

By Joule's law, the coil dissipates:

$$
P_{coil} = I_c^2 \, R_{hot} = \frac{V_c^2}{R_{hot}}
$$

### Step 2 — Thermal Model

In steady state, all dissipated power flows out through the relay's outer surface to ambient. The thermal analogue is Ohm's law:

$$
\Delta T = P_{coil} \cdot R_{\theta a}
$$

where $R_{\theta a}$ is the **thermal resistance** from coil to ambient (in K/W).

### Step 3 — Combine

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T = \frac{V_c^2}{R_{hot}} \cdot R_{\theta a}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $V_c = 24 \text{ V DC}$
- $R_{cold} = 1000 \, \Omega$ at $25 \, °C$
- $R_{\theta a} = 50 \text{ K/W}$
- Maximum permissible $\Delta T = 60 \text{ K}$

**Find:** Is the relay thermally safe?

**Solution:**

Hot resistance at 85 °C ($\Delta T = 60 \text{ K}$):
$$
R_{hot} = 1000 \times \left( 1 + 0.00393 \times 60 \right) = 1000 \times 1.2358 = 1235.8 \, \Omega
$$

Coil power:
$$
P_{coil} = \frac{24^2}{1235.8} = \frac{576}{1235.8} = 0.466 \text{ W}
$$

Temperature rise:
$$
\Delta T = 0.466 \times 50 = 23.3 \text{ K}
$$

Since $23.3 \text{ K} \ll 60 \text{ K}$, **the relay is thermally safe**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Relay vs Contactor

| Parameter | Electromagnetic Relay | Power Contactor |
|---|---|---|
| Typical Current Rating | ≤ 10 A | 9 A – 1000+ A |
| Voltage Rating | Up to 250 V AC / 30 V DC | Up to 1000 V AC |
| Main Contact Type | NO / NC / CO (mixed) | Predominantly NO (Form A) |
| Number of Poles | 1, 2, 4 poles common | 3-pole (motor), 4-pole (star-delta) |
| Coil Voltage | 5 V DC – 230 V AC | 24 V DC – 415 V AC |
| Arc Suppression | Optional (RC snubber) | **Mandatory** (arc chutes, de-ion grids) |
| Mechanical Life | 10⁷ operations | 10⁶ operations |
| Electrical Life | 10⁵ operations | 10⁵ – 10⁶ (AC-3 duty) |
| Auxiliary Contacts | Built-in | Add-on sidecar blocks |
| Primary Application | Logic, signal-level switching | Motor starters, power distribution |
| Overload Integration | None | **Thermal OLR** bimetallic pair mounted on contactor |
| Standards | IEC 61810, IS 7325 | IEC 60947-4-1, IS 13947 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU board question is: *"Can a contactor be used in place of a relay?"* The answer is **technically yes, but economically wasteful** — contactors cost 5–10× more and have larger footprints. Conversely, a relay **must never** be used to switch motor inrush current directly, as the contact welding and arcing will destroy it within hours.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Contact Configuration Forms (KTU Favourite)

| Form Code | IEC Symbol | Description | Functional Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Form A** (SPST-NO) | Two parallel bars | Single-Pole Single-Throw, Normally Open | Open at rest, closes on energise |
| **Form B** (SPST-NC) | Bar with diagonal strike | Single-Pole Single-Throw, Normally Closed | Closed at rest, opens on energise |
| **Form C** (SPDT-CO) | COM-NO-NC trio | Single-Pole Double-Throw, Changeover | Transfers between two circuits |
| **Form X** (DPDT-NO×2) | Two Form A coupled | Double-Pole Double-Throw, NO pair | Two simultaneous NO closures |
| **Form Y** (DPDT-NC×2) | Two Form B coupled | Double-Pole Double-Throw, NC pair | Two simultaneous NC openings |
| **Form Z** (DPDT-CO×2) | Two Form C coupled | Double-Pole Double-Throw, CO pair | Two simultaneous transfers |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"A is Always open at rest, B is Blocked (closed) at rest, C is the Changeover (combination)."*

---

## 3.5 Selection Decision Tree — Relay or Contactor?

| Application | Current | Voltage | Recommended Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| PLC indicator lamp | 0.05 A | 24 V DC | **Solid State Relay (SSR)** |
| Solenoid valve (24 V DC) | 0.5 A | 24 V DC | **Miniature PCB relay** |
| 1-phase motor ≤ 0.5 HP | 5 A | 230 V AC | **Industrial relay (8–10 A)** |
| 3-phase motor 1–5 HP | 8–15 A | 415 V AC | **3-pole contactor + OLR** |
| 3-phase motor 10–50 HP | 30–80 A | 415 V AC | **Heavy-duty contactor + CT-operated OLR** |
| DC motor drive | 50 A | 440 V DC | **DC contactor with magnetic blowout** |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of an Electromagnetic Relay

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional architecture* of a generic electromagnetic relay, showing the magnetic flux path, mechanical linkage, and electrical contact grouping.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC AC Coil Input Vc] --> B[Coil Winding N turns]
    B --> C[Yoke and Core laminated Si Fe]
    C --> D[Air Gap g pole face]
    D --> E[Armature pivoted lever]
    E --> F[Return Spring Fs]
    E --> G[Moving Contact common COM]
    G --> H[NO Contact Form A]
    G --> I[NC Contact Form B]
    H --> J[Load Circuit Terminal 1]
    I --> K[Load Circuit Terminal 2]
    L[Pickup Voltage Vp] --> B
    M[Dropout Voltage Vd] --> B
    N[Arc Suppression Snubber RC] -.-> H
    N -.-> I
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Exam:**
- The left half (`A → B → C → D → E`) is the **magnetic circuit**.
- The right half (`E → G → H/I → J/K`) is the **electrical load circuit**.
- The **air gap D** is the critical design variable — its size $g$ appears squared in the force equation.
- **Arc suppression N** is optional for relays but **mandatory for contactors**.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Contactor-Based Direct-On-Line Motor Starter

The most common KTU practical question concerns the *DOL starter*, which uses a contactor + overload relay. The following flowchart shows the *logical sequence* of operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[MCB Closed 3 Phase Supply] --> coil[Contactor Coil Energise via Start Pushbutton]
    coil --> magnetise[Electromagnet Pulls Armature]
    magnetise --> noClose[3 NO Main Contacts Close]
    noClose --> auxNO[Auxiliary NO Contact Latches Holding Circuit]
    auxNO --> motorRun[3 Phase Induction Motor Runs]
    motorRun --> olrCheck{Overload Relay OLR OK?}
    olrCheck -- Yes --> run[Continue Running]
    olrCheck -- No Trip --> trip[OLR NC Contact Opens]
    trip --> coilOff[Contactor Coil De energise]
    coilOff --> spring[Return Spring Pulls Armature Back]
    spring --> noOpen[3 NO Main Contacts Open]
    noOpen --> motorStop[Motor Stops]
    run --> stopPress{Stop Pushbutton Pressed?}
    stopPress -- Yes --> coilOff
    stopPress -- No --> run
```

**Key Reading Points:**
- The **auxiliary NO contact** that *latches* the start button is called the **holding contact** or **seal-in contact** — it is the *physical* implementation of *latching logic* in relay control.
- The **OLR (Overload Relay)** is a *normally-closed* contact in series with the contactor coil; when an overload occurs, it opens and *drops out* the contactor — a classic example of *fail-safe* design.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PLC to Motor Interfacing

This diagram depicts the *complete signal-power chain* typical of a KTU industrial automation lab setup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plc[PLC CPU Digital Output 24V DC 0.5A] -->|Y0 terminal| ir[Interposing Relay Coil 24V DC]
    ir --> irContact[Interposing Relay NO Contact]
    irContact -->|240V AC control circuit| cc[Contactor Coil A1 A2 240V AC]
    cc --> ccMain[3 NO Main Contacts of Contactor]
    ccMain -->|3 Phase 415V AC| olr[Overload Relay Bimetallic Strip]
    olr --> motor[3 Phase Induction Motor M]
    ccAux[Contactor Auxiliary NO Contact] -->|feedback| plc
    style plc fill:#ffd966
    style motor fill:#f4cccc
```

**Engineering Note:** The feedback line `ccAux → plc` represents the *contactor-auxiliary feedback* that the PLC reads on an input terminal to confirm *contactor pickup* — a key technique for *fault diagnostics* in Industry 4.0 systems.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an electromagnetic relay. List any two applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electromagnetic relay** consists of a coil wound around a soft-iron core, an armature pivoted near the pole face, a return spring, and a set of contacts (NO, NC, COM).

- When the **control voltage $V_c$** is applied to the coil, current $I_c = V_c / R_{coil}$ flows and generates an MMF $\mathcal{F} = N I_c$ (N = turns).
- The resulting flux $\Phi$ produces a magnetic force $F_{mag} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I_c^2 A}{2 g^2}$ on the armature.
- When $F_{mag} > F_{spring}$, the **armature pulls in**, and the moving contact transfers from NC to NO — the **load circuit is energised**.
- On coil de-energisation, the spring restores the armature to its rest position.

**Two applications:**
1. Interfacing a PLC output to a high-power load.
2. Implementing logic control in a relay-ladder diagram (e.g., motor starter).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**"Differentiate between a relay and a contactor. Why can't a relay be used for direct switching of a 3-phase induction motor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Relay | Contactor |
|---|---|---|
| Current rating | ≤ 10 A | 9 A – 1000+ A |
| Arc suppression | Optional | Mandatory (arc chutes) |
| Main contacts | NO/NC/CO mix | Predominantly NO, 3-pole |
| Standards | IEC 61810 | IEC 60947-4-1 |

**Why a relay cannot switch a 3-phase motor directly:**
A 3-phase induction motor draws a **starting (inrush) current of 6–8 times** its full-load current. For a 5 HP motor (FLC ≈ 7 A), inrush can reach **42–56 A** for 1–2 seconds. A control relay rated 10 A will experience:
1. **Contact welding** — the high inrush current arcs and fuses the contact tips.
2. **Severe arc erosion** — no arc chute, so the contact material vaporises.
3. **Insulation failure** — relay dielectric is not rated for 415 V AC across open contacts.

Hence, a **contactor with built-in arc chutes** is mandatory for motor switching. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Q7]`

**(a) Explain the construction and working of an electromagnetic contactor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of arc chutes in detail.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

An electromagnetic contactor consists of:

1. **Coil Assembly:** A cylindrical coil of enameled copper wire, wound on a bobbin, rated for the control voltage (24 V DC, 110 V AC, 230 V AC, or 415 V AC). The coil is the only power-consuming part of the contactor.
2. **Magnetic Core and Yoke:** Laminated silicon-steel, shaped as an E-core or T-core, providing a low-reluctance path. Lamination reduces eddy-current losses at 50/60 Hz AC operation.
3. **Armature:** A pivoted ferromagnetic plate that closes the magnetic circuit on coil energisation. Carries the moving contact bridge.
4. **Main Contacts (3 NO poles for 3-phase):** Made of **silver-tin oxide (AgSnO₂)** or **silver-cadmium oxide (AgCdO)** for high arc resistance. Rated for AC-3 duty.
5. **Arc Chutes:** Ceramic (moulded melamine formaldehyde or DMC) chambers surrounding each pole, containing **de-ion grids** — a series of steel plates that split and cool the arc.
6. **Auxiliary Contact Block:** Side-mounted NO/NC contacts for control circuit feedback.
7. **Return Spring:** Restores armature on de-energisation.
8. **Overload Relay Mounting:** DIN-rail socket for thermal OLR.

**Working (2 marks):**

- On coil energisation, the magnetic flux overcomes the spring force and pulls the armature.
- The three main NO contacts close, connecting the 3-phase supply to the motor terminals.
- A built-in auxiliary NO contact closes, latching the start command.
- On coil de-energisation (either by stop button or OLR trip), the spring snaps the contacts open.

**Role of Arc Chutes (2 marks):**

When a heavy inductive load (motor winding) is switched off, the stored magnetic energy in the inductance forces current to continue flowing, *stretching* an **electric arc** between the parting contacts. Arc temperature can exceed **10,000 °C**.

The **arc chute** suppresses this arc by:

1. **Splitting the arc** into multiple smaller arcs using the de-ion grid plates — each smaller arc has higher resistance and dissipates more energy.
2. **Cooling the arc plasma** by forcing it against the cool ceramic walls.
3. **Lengthening the arc path** by magnetic blowout, increasing arc voltage beyond the supply voltage → arc extinguishes at current zero (in AC).
4. **De-ionising the gap** — recombination of ions and electrons re-establishes dielectric strength rapidly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse the role of **arc chutes** with **RC snubbers**. Arc chutes are *passive mechanical* devices that split and cool the arc; RC snubbers are *active electronic* components that limit the rate of rise of voltage (dv/dt) across contacts. Both are arc-suppression techniques but are **not interchangeable** in a KTU answer.

---

**(b) Discuss the different types of overload relays used in motor protection. Explain the bimetallic OLR with a suitable diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Overload Relays (4 marks):**

1. **Bimetallic Thermal OLR:**
   - Uses a **bimetallic strip** — two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients (e.g., Invar and brass) welded together.
   - On overcurrent, $I^2R$ heating deflects the strip, tripping a ratchet mechanism that opens an NC contact in the contactor coil circuit.
   - **Inverse-time characteristic** — higher overload → faster trip (mimics $I^2t$ heating of motor windings).
   - Adjustable current setting: typically **0.8 – 1.2 × FLC**.

2. **Eutectic Alloy (Solder Pot) OLR:**
   - Uses a low-melting-point eutectic alloy (e.g., Wood's metal) that **melts at a specific temperature**.
   - On overload, a heating coil melts the alloy, releasing a ratchet → NC contact opens.
   - **Non-adjustable** but **self-compensating** for ambient temperature.
   - **Manual reset only** — safer for maintenance personnel.

3. **Electronic (Microprocessor-Based) OLR:**
   - Uses **current transformers (CTs)** to sense motor current.
   - Microprocessor computes $I^2t$ digitally and trips a contactor via an internal relay.
   - Highly **accurate** (± 2 %), **wide range** (3:1), **communicable** (Modbus/Profibus).
   - Supports **phase-loss, phase-imbalance, and earth-fault** protection.

4. **Magnetic (Instantaneous) OLR:**
   - Uses a **solenoid** with a plunger. Excessive current pulls the plunger → trip.
   - **No thermal lag** — instantaneous trip, used for **short-circuit protection** in conjunction with fuses/MCBs.

**Bimetallic OLR Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3 Phase Supply L1 L2 L3] --> B[Contactor Main Contacts]
    B --> C[Heater Coil of OLR wound around bimetal]
    C --> D[Heater coil in series with motor]
    D --> E[Bimetallic Strip Invar Brass]
    E --> F{Deflection}
    F -- Below threshold --> G[No Trip motor runs normally]
    F -- Above threshold --> H[Strip bends and pushes trip lever]
    H --> I[Trip lever releases spring loaded pawl]
    I --> J[NC Contact of OLR opens]
    J --> K[Contactor coil de energises]
    K --> L[Motor Stops]
```

**Working Description:**
- The heater coil carries the motor current. Under normal load, the heat is insufficient to deflect the bimetallic strip.
- Under overload (e.g., 1.2 × FLC), the $I^2R$ heat deflects the strip by an amount proportional to the **integral of $I^2 \, dt$** — implementing the $I^2t$ protection curve.
- After a predetermined deflection, the strip pushes a trip lever, opening the NC contact and de-energising the contactor coil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Q8]`

**(a) Explain the various contact configurations (Form A, Form B, Form C) used in relays with suitable diagrams. Discuss the meaning of the term 'pole' and 'throw' in relay specifications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions of Pole and Throw (2 marks):**

- **Pole:** The number of *separate* circuits the relay can switch. A single-pole relay switches *one* circuit; a double-pole relay switches *two* independent circuits simultaneously.
- **Throw:** The number of *contact positions* per pole. A single-throw contact has two positions (open or closed). A double-throw contact has three terminals (COM, NO, NC) and can route current to either of two destinations.

**Contact Configurations (5 marks):**

1. **Form A — SPST-NO (Single-Pole Single-Throw, Normally Open):**
   - Consists of two terminals. The contact is open at rest, closes on coil energisation.
   - **Schematic:** Two parallel bars (── ──) connected by a line when energised.
   - **Application:** Motor start pushbutton contact, simple on/off switching.

2. **Form B — SPST-NC (Single-Pole Single-Throw, Normally Closed):**
   - Consists of two terminals. The contact is closed at rest, opens on coil energisation.
   - **Schematic:** A line with a slash (──/──) that becomes broken when energised.
   - **Application:** Stop pushbutton, OLR trip contact, safety interlock.

3. **Form C — SPDT-CO (Single-Pole Double-Throw, Changeover):**
   - Has three terminals: **COM** (common), **NO**, and **NC**.
